prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 68-year-old male is brought to ED by ambulance with sudden onset right-sided weakness affecting his arm and leg, and difficulty speaking, that began approximately 90 minutes ago while sitting at rest.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden-onset focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, speech disturbance) in an older adult mandate urgent stroke assessment. Both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke must be considered, as must stroke mimics including hypoglycaemia, Todd's paresis after a seizure, and less likely structural lesions. The differential at this stage is broad and cannot be narrowed without further information.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The deficit came on over seconds and has remained stable since onset. There is no headache, no vomiting, no loss of consciousness, and no preceding seizure activity. He was well immediately before the onset with no preceding warning symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of headache and vomiting makes haemorrhagic stroke somewhat less likely but does not exclude it — haemorrhagic stroke can present without these features. Absence of preceding seizure reduces the likelihood of Todd's paresis. The static nature of the deficit argues against a progressive structural lesion, but all vascular stroke types remain in play. Brain tumour becomes less likely with the acute onset.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has known hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia, all managed for over a decade. He smokes 20 cigarettes per day. There is no history of atrial fibrillation and no current anticoagulation. He takes amlodipine, metformin, and atorvastatin.
prompts.reasoning: A heavy vascular risk factor burden — hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, and smoking — significantly raises the probability of cerebrovascular disease of either ischaemic or haemorrhagic type. Hypertension is a risk factor for both. Absence of AF lowers but does not eliminate the probability of cardioembolic stroke. Todd's paresis and hypoglycaemia become less clinically probable given the vascular history, but imaging and a blood glucose are still required. Brain tumour is now unlikely.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 178/96 mmHg, HR 84 bpm regular, temperature 36.8°C. Blood glucose 5.6 mmol/L. GCS 14/15. Right upper and lower limb weakness (arm 2/5, leg 3/5). Dysarthria without aphasia. Right plantar response upgoing. No facial asymmetry. No neck stiffness.
prompts.reasoning: Normal blood glucose excludes hypoglycaemia as a mimic. Regular cardiac rhythm does not eliminate embolic aetiology but is less consistent with AF-related embolism. The UMN pattern of weakness without cortical signs (no aphasia, no visual field defect) could be consistent with a deep or cortical lesion. Haemorrhagic and ischaemic stroke both remain plausible — CT imaging is required to differentiate. Todd's paresis is now effectively excluded by normal glucose and absence of seizure history.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): no intraparenchymal haemorrhage, no subarachnoid blood. Subtle loss of grey-white differentiation in the left basal ganglia region. No midline shift. No mass lesion identified.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of haemorrhage on CT excludes haemorrhagic stroke. The subtle early ischaemic change in the left hemisphere is consistent with an evolving infarct. Both thrombotic and embolic ischaemic stroke remain possible — the mechanism cannot be determined from CT alone. CT angiography and cardiac workup are required to identify the source.

prompts.label: Vascular Imaging & Cardiac Workup
prompts.text: CT angiography of neck vessels: severe (85%) stenosis at the left internal carotid artery bifurcation due to calcified atherosclerotic plaque. No intraluminal filling defect consistent with thrombus at a cardiac source. 12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm. 48-hour cardiac monitoring: no paroxysmal AF detected. Fasting LDL: 4.4 mmol/L.
prompts.reasoning: Severe ipsilateral carotid stenosis from calcified atherosclerotic plaque, in the absence of AF or cardiac thrombus on monitoring and echocardiography, confirms a thrombotic (large-vessel atherosclerotic) mechanism. Plaque rupture at the carotid bifurcation with local thrombosis and distal embolisation to the MCA territory is the underlying pathophysiology. Elevated LDL reinforces the atherogenic basis. Embolic stroke from a cardiac source is now excluded.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 74-year-old female is brought to ED with sudden onset of left arm weakness and difficulty understanding what people are saying to her, lasting approximately 2 hours. Onset was at rest.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden focal neurological deficit in an older adult requires urgent stroke assessment. Weakness and receptive speech disturbance suggest right hemisphere involvement. Both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke, as well as stroke mimics such as hypoglycaemia and Todd's paresis, remain in the differential at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The deficit developed over seconds and has not fluctuated. She has no headache and no vomiting. Her family recalls two brief episodes over the past month in which she transiently had difficulty speaking, each lasting about 15 to 20 minutes before fully resolving.
prompts.reasoning: Prior transient episodes of focal deficit in the same territory raise concern for recurrent vascular events from a proximal source, but do not yet distinguish thrombotic from embolic ischaemia or exclude haemorrhagic stroke. Absence of headache is a minor point against haemorrhage but does not exclude it. Hypoglycaemia and Todd's paresis remain less likely given the pattern of recurrent events.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has hypertension treated with amlodipine and takes aspirin 100mg daily. She does not have diabetes or a known history of atrial fibrillation. She is a lifelong non-smoker and reports no prior cardiac events or cardiac investigations.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension is a risk factor for both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke as well as large-vessel atherosclerosis. The absence of documented AF does not exclude it — AF is frequently paroxysmal and may be undetected. Aspirin monotherapy provides insufficient protection against cardioembolic events if AF is present. All three leading diagnoses remain viable pending examination and imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 162/88 mmHg, HR 92 bpm irregular rhythm, temperature 37.0°C. Blood glucose 6.1 mmol/L. GCS 13/15. Left arm power 1/5, left leg 3/5. Receptive dysphasia — unable to follow commands. No facial droop. No papilloedema.
prompts.reasoning: Normal blood glucose excludes hypoglycaemia. Irregular pulse raises suspicion for atrial fibrillation, which would increase the probability of a cardioembolic mechanism — but this must be confirmed with an ECG and is not yet diagnostic. Cortical signs (receptive dysphasia) suggest involvement of the posterior superior temporal cortex. Haemorrhagic and both ischaemic subtypes remain possible pending imaging.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast) & MRI DWI
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): no haemorrhage. Subtle hypodensity in the right posterior temporal region. MRI DWI: wedge-shaped cortical restricted diffusion in the right posterior MCA territory distribution, consistent with an acute cortical infarct.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of haemorrhage excludes haemorrhagic stroke. Wedge-shaped cortical infarction in a vascular territory is more consistent with an embolic mechanism — emboli tend to lodge at distal cortical branch points — but large-vessel thrombosis with distal propagation can also cause cortical infarcts. The source of embolism must now be established with cardiac workup and vascular imaging.

prompts.label: Cardiac Investigation & Source Identification
prompts.text: 12-lead ECG: atrial fibrillation confirmed. Transoesophageal echocardiogram: spontaneous echo contrast in the left atrial appendage with a 1.1cm mobile thrombus. Carotid Doppler: no significant stenosis bilaterally. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score: 5.
prompts.reasoning: Confirmed atrial fibrillation with a left atrial appendage thrombus on TOE establishes the cardioembolic source. Absence of significant carotid stenosis excludes large-vessel thrombotic disease as the mechanism. The cortical wedge-shaped infarct pattern, irregular rhythm, and now-confirmed LAA thrombus collectively confirm embolic stroke from AF. Anticoagulation is the definitive secondary prevention strategy.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 65-year-old male attends ED reporting that approximately 3 hours ago he developed sudden inability to move his right arm and slurred speech, lasting around 15 minutes. His symptoms have now fully resolved.
prompts.reasoning: A fully resolved focal neurological deficit demands urgent assessment. Although symptoms have resolved, an acute ischaemic stroke with early reperfusion, TIA, Todd's paresis after a brief seizure, hemiplegic migraine, and hypoglycaemia all remain in the differential at this stage. This is a time-sensitive situation with high short-term stroke risk.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The symptoms peaked within 1 minute and resolved completely within 15 minutes. There was no loss of consciousness, no tongue biting, no post-ictal drowsiness, and no preceding or accompanying headache. A neighbour witnessed the episode and confirms no jerking movements.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and witnessed convulsive activity makes Todd's paresis unlikely. Absence of headache reduces the probability of hemiplegic migraine, which typically accompanies or follows headache. A vascular event — either TIA or very early stroke with spontaneous reperfusion — remains the most likely category. Hypoglycaemia remains possible but no glucose has been checked yet.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia. He had a myocardial infarction 3 years ago and currently smokes 10 cigarettes per day. He takes aspirin, metformin, atorvastatin, and ramipril. No prior episodes and no documented history of atrial fibrillation.
prompts.reasoning: Significant vascular risk factor burden — hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, prior MI, and smoking — substantially raises the probability of a vascular event. Prior MI raises the possibility of a mural thrombus as a cardioembolic source. Migraine with motor aura is now less plausible given the vascular history and age. A vascular TIA or minor stroke is the leading hypothesis, with the specific mechanism yet to be determined.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination is entirely normal. BP 168/94 mmHg. HR 80 bpm regular. Blood glucose 5.8 mmol/L. No focal neurological deficits. No carotid bruits detected. MMSE 30/30.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination with complete resolution is consistent with TIA or early resolved stroke. Normal blood glucose excludes hypoglycaemia. Regular rhythm is noted but does not exclude paroxysmal AF. The completely normal examination does not distinguish between the three remaining vascular diagnoses — imaging and cardiac workup are required. The ABCD² score is high, indicating significant short-term stroke risk.

prompts.label: MRI DWI Brain & Carotid Imaging
prompts.text: MRI brain with DWI: a small (8mm) focus of restricted diffusion in the left corona radiata, consistent with a small acute ischaemic lesion. CT angiography of the neck: 60% stenosis at the left internal carotid artery bifurcation with a mixed echogenicity atherosclerotic plaque. No occlusion.
prompts.reasoning: A small DWI-positive lesion confirms ischaemia as the mechanism, excluding non-ischaemic mimics. The clinical syndrome resolved within 24 hours, satisfying the modern definition of TIA even with a small DWI lesion. Ipsilateral carotid stenosis with an unstable mixed plaque is a high-risk embolic source and the most likely mechanism. Cardioembolic source remains possible and must be excluded.

prompts.label: Cardiac Workup & Secondary Prevention
prompts.text: 12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm. 48-hour cardiac monitoring: no AF detected. Echocardiogram: mild LV hypertrophy, no intracardiac thrombus, no patent foramen ovale. Fasting LDL: 3.8 mmol/L. Dual antiplatelet therapy initiated; urgent neurovascular clinic referral for carotid endarterectomy assessment.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of AF, intracardiac thrombus, and PFO on monitoring and echocardiography excludes a cardioembolic mechanism. The culprit is the ipsilateral symptomatic carotid stenosis with an unstable plaque — consistent with thromboembolism from the carotid bifurcation causing a brief cortical ischaemic event. Symptoms resolved within 24 hours with a small DWI lesion, confirming TIA. Carotid endarterectomy within 2 weeks is indicated for symptomatic stenosis >50%.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 55-year-old male is admitted to ICU following return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) after an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. Bystander CPR was started within 3 minutes and ROSC was achieved after 18 minutes of resuscitation.
prompts.reasoning: Cardiac arrest with prolonged downtime places the entire brain at risk of global ischaemia. The primary concern is the extent and reversibility of brain injury. Both global ischaemia from the arrest and focal events that may have precipitated the arrest — including basilar artery occlusion or brainstem stroke — must be considered. Status epilepticus can also present with post-ictal coma and requires exclusion.

prompts.label: Collateral History
prompts.text: A colleague witnessed him clutch his chest and complain of central chest pain for approximately one hour before losing consciousness and collapsing. There is no prior history of seizure disorder and no witnessed convulsive activity before collapse.
prompts.reasoning: Chest pain preceding collapse suggests a primary cardiac event, most likely ventricular fibrillation complicating myocardial infarction, causing the arrest. This makes global cerebral ischaemia from the arrest the most probable mechanism of brain injury. A primary brainstem or posterior circulation stroke can also cause sudden collapse, but the prodrome of chest pain makes a cardiac cause more likely. Status epilepticus is now less likely.

prompts.label: Past Medical History
prompts.text: Known hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidaemia. Smokes 30 pack-years. No prior neurological history. No prior seizure disorder. He was in good baseline health before today.
prompts.reasoning: Heavy vascular risk factor burden strongly supports underlying coronary artery disease as the substrate for the cardiac arrest. Global cerebral ischaemia from prolonged cardiac arrest is now the leading neurological diagnosis. Brainstem stroke as the primary event becomes less likely given the cardiac history and chest pain prodrome, though CTA of the posterior circulation will be needed. Both 'global cerebral ischaemia' and 'hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy' describe the same process at different stages.

prompts.label: Physical Examination (Post-ROSC, Day 1)
prompts.text: GCS 6 (E1V1M4) on standardised assessment off sedation. Pupils 4mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive to light. No spontaneous eye movements. Decorticate posturing to painful stimuli. Generalised myoclonic jerks occurring every few minutes. Temperature 36.2°C on targeted temperature management protocol at 34°C.
prompts.reasoning: Depressed consciousness with bilateral sluggish pupils, decorticate posturing, and myoclonus following prolonged cardiac arrest is consistent with severe diffuse hypoxic-ischaemic brain injury. Myoclonic status epilepticus post-cardiac arrest is a recognised manifestation of severe cortical injury and carries a poor prognosis. The bilateral and symmetric nature of the examination argues against a focal structural lesion such as basilar artery occlusion. Neuroprognostication should be deferred until 72 hours post-ROSC.

prompts.label: Biomarkers, EEG & Coronary Workup
prompts.text: Neuron-specific enolase (NSE) at 48 hours: 89 µg/L (markedly elevated; >60 µg/L associated with poor neurological outcome). Continuous EEG: burst-suppression pattern with superimposed generalised myoclonic status. Coronary angiography: proximal LAD occlusion — treated with primary PCI.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated NSE at 48 hours confirms extensive neuronal death from global ischaemia. The EEG burst-suppression pattern with myoclonic status is a strongly adverse prognostic finding. Proximal LAD occlusion confirms the cardiac arrest mechanism as STEMI with VF. The underlying cause of the brain injury is now clearly established as global cerebral ischaemia from prolonged cardiac arrest. Imaging is required to define the distribution and severity.

prompts.label: MRI Brain (Day 3)
prompts.text: MRI DWI (Day 3 post-arrest): bilateral symmetric restricted diffusion in the parasagittal cortex (anterior watershed zone between ACA and MCA territories) and the parieto-occipital cortex (posterior watershed zone between MCA and PCA territories). Diffuse cortical ribboning on FLAIR indicating laminar necrosis. Selective restricted diffusion also visible in the hippocampi bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral symmetric watershed zone infarcts are the imaging hallmark of global cerebral ischaemia — the boundary zones between major arterial territories are most vulnerable when perfusion pressure falls globally. Cortical ribboning on FLAIR reflects laminar necrosis from selective neuronal vulnerability. Hippocampal involvement reflects the particular susceptibility of CA1 neurons. This pattern is diagnostic of global cerebral ischaemia and watershed infarction, confirming the final diagnosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 61-year-old male presents to ED with sudden onset of right arm and leg weakness that began 6 hours ago and has remained stable. He reports no other symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden focal motor deficit in an older adult requires urgent stroke assessment. Ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke are the priority diagnoses to exclude. Hypoglycaemia can mimic a focal stroke syndrome. The distribution (arm and leg) and absence of other reported symptoms are noted but cannot yet narrow the differential without further history and examination.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The weakness involves the arm and leg equally on the right side. He specifically denies any facial weakness, speech difficulty, visual disturbance, sensory changes, or headache. No preceding seizure or loss of consciousness. No vomiting.
prompts.reasoning: Pure motor hemiparesis of the arm and leg without facial involvement, speech disturbance, or sensory loss constitutes a clinically recognised lacunar syndrome pattern. However, the absence of cortical signs does not exclude large-vessel ischaemic stroke or a deep hypertensive haemorrhage at this stage — both can occasionally produce predominantly motor deficits. Imaging and examination are required before narrowing the differential further.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Longstanding hypertension, often poorly controlled, and type 2 diabetes for 8 years with suboptimal glycaemic management (last HbA1c 8.9%). Mild CKD stage 3. No history of atrial fibrillation. Takes amlodipine and metformin; not currently on a statin.
prompts.reasoning: Poorly controlled hypertension and diabetes are the principal risk factors for both cerebral small vessel disease (lacunar infarcts) and hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage. The absence of AF lowers the probability of cardioembolic stroke. Large-vessel ischaemic stroke remains possible, but the pure motor syndrome without cortical features and the risk factor profile are more consistent with small vessel disease. Haemorrhagic stroke must still be excluded with imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 172/100 mmHg, HR 76 bpm regular. Blood glucose 7.2 mmol/L. Right arm power 3/5, right leg 3/5. Brisk reflexes on the right with upgoing right plantar. No facial asymmetry. No aphasia, no neglect, no visual field deficit. No sensory loss on formal testing. NIHSS 6.
prompts.reasoning: Normal blood glucose excludes hypoglycaemia. The examination confirms a pure motor hemiparesis with no cortical signs whatsoever — no aphasia, no neglect, no visual field defect, and no hemisensory loss. This is consistent with a lacunar syndrome from a lesion in the posterior limb of the internal capsule or corona radiata. Hypertensive haemorrhage in a deep location and large-vessel ischaemia must still be excluded by imaging — both remain plausible.

prompts.label: CT Head & MRI Brain
prompts.text: CT head: no haemorrhage. No discrete hypodense lesion identified acutely. Moderate periventricular white matter change. MRI DWI: 12mm area of restricted diffusion in the posterior limb of the left internal capsule, consistent with an acute lacunar infarct. FLAIR: multiple small chronic lacunae (3–12mm) in the basal ganglia and centrum semiovale.
prompts.reasoning: CT excludes haemorrhage. MRI DWI demonstrates a small acute infarct in the posterior limb of the internal capsule — the precise location of the corticospinal tract fibres subserving arm and leg, explaining the pure motor hemiparesis without cortical involvement. Multiple chronic lacunae on FLAIR confirm established cerebral small vessel disease. The pattern is diagnostic of a lacunar infarct from small vessel disease.

prompts.label: Vascular & Cardiac Workup
prompts.text: Carotid Doppler ultrasound: no significant stenosis bilaterally. 48-hour Holter monitoring: no atrial fibrillation detected. Echocardiogram: normal. Fasting LDL 3.4 mmol/L. HbA1c 9.1%, eGFR 52 mL/min.
prompts.reasoning: No large-vessel disease and no cardiac embolic source are identified, confirming the lesion is due to intrinsic small vessel disease rather than artery-to-artery embolism or cardioembolism. The combination of MRI-confirmed small deep infarct, pure lacunar clinical syndrome, absence of large-vessel or cardiac source, and heavy small-vessel risk factor burden (poorly controlled hypertension and diabetes) establishes the diagnosis of lacunar infarct from cerebral small vessel disease.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old female is brought to ED by her husband with a severe generalised headache, confusion, and two witnessed seizures over the past 4 hours. Her husband also reports she has had blurred vision since yesterday.
prompts.reasoning: Headache, confusion, seizures, and visual disturbance constitute an acute encephalopathic presentation requiring urgent evaluation. The broad differential at this stage includes SAH, CNS infection (meningitis, encephalitis), hypertensive emergency with neurological complications, eclampsia in a woman of childbearing age, and haemorrhagic stroke. All must be urgently considered.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache has developed over several hours — it was not of sudden thunderclap onset. There is no fever. She is not pregnant and does not use the oral contraceptive pill. No recent travel, no sick contacts, no photophobia or neck stiffness reported at home. Bilateral blurred vision has been progressively worsening over 24 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Non-thunderclap onset of headache makes SAH less likely but does not exclude it — SAH can present with progressive headache in a minority of cases. Absence of fever reduces the probability of CNS infection but does not exclude it. Negative pregnancy test reported verbally by husband excludes eclampsia as a formal diagnosis. The progressive bilateral visual disturbance suggests a posterior cerebral process but is not yet diagnostic.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Known hypertension for 5 years. She stopped all three of her antihypertensive medications 3 weeks ago because of side effects, without medical review. No diabetes. No prior neurological conditions. No immunosuppression. Family history of hypertension.
prompts.reasoning: Abrupt discontinuation of antihypertensives 3 weeks ago is a recognised precipitant of hypertensive crisis and raises the pre-test probability of a hypertension-related neurological emergency substantially. This strongly focuses the differential toward hypertensive aetiology. SAH and hypertensive ICH remain possible as complications of a hypertensive crisis. CNS infection becomes less likely without fever, immunosuppression, or epidemiological risk factors.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 238/148 mmHg bilaterally. HR 96 bpm, temperature 37.0°C. GCS 12/15. No focal neurological deficits. No meningism. Fundoscopy: bilateral papilloedema with flame-shaped retinal haemorrhages. Urine dipstick: 3+ proteinuria.
prompts.reasoning: Severely elevated BP of 238/148 mmHg with papilloedema, retinal haemorrhages, and proteinuria confirms hypertensive emergency with multi-organ involvement (brain, eye, kidney). The absence of focal neurological deficits and meningism makes meningitis, encephalitis, and focal haemorrhagic stroke less likely, though ICH must still be excluded by CT. The encephalopathic presentation with bilateral optic disc changes is consistent with a global cerebrovascular process.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & CT Head
prompts.text: Pregnancy test: negative. FBC: normal platelets. Coagulation: normal. Creatinine 162 µmol/L (elevated), LDH mildly elevated. CT head (non-contrast): no haemorrhage, no infarct, no mass lesion. No subarachnoid blood.
prompts.reasoning: Negative pregnancy test formally excludes eclampsia. Normal platelets and LDH exclude TTP and HUS as causes of the hypertensive encephalopathy-like presentation. CT head excludes haemorrhagic stroke and subarachnoid haemorrhage. Renal impairment confirms multi-organ hypertensive damage. With ICH and SAH now excluded and all mimics eliminated, hypertensive encephalopathy becomes the definitive working diagnosis. MRI is required to confirm the neuroimaging pattern.

prompts.label: MRI Brain (FLAIR)
prompts.text: MRI brain FLAIR: bilateral symmetrical T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensity in the posterior parieto-occipital cortex and subcortical white matter. No restricted diffusion on DWI, confirming vasogenic rather than cytotoxic oedema. Findings completely resolved on repeat MRI performed 5 days after initiation of antihypertensive treatment.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral posterior parieto-occipital vasogenic oedema on FLAIR — the radiological manifestation of PRES — confirms hypertensive encephalopathy. The reversibility on repeat imaging after BP control is the diagnostic hallmark: vasogenic oedema from failed cerebral autoregulation resolves when perfusion pressure normalises. This distinguishes hypertensive encephalopathy with PRES from completed infarction or haemorrhagic injury.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 72-year-old male is brought to the memory clinic by his wife, who reports progressive cognitive decline over the past 18 months that has significantly impaired his ability to manage daily tasks and household finances.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive cognitive decline affecting daily function in a 72-year-old has a broad differential encompassing all major dementia syndromes, as well as treatable structural causes such as chronic subdural haematoma and hydrocephalus. The specific cognitive profile, temporal pattern, and associated features are required to distinguish between these possibilities.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His wife describes the decline as distinctly step-wise: he was stable, then declined suddenly over a few days on two separate occasions 18 months and 6 months ago respectively, with some partial recovery between episodes. He has also developed an unsteady gait and episodes of emotional lability, but no visual hallucinations and no significant change in personality.
prompts.reasoning: Step-wise deterioration with partial inter-episode recovery is more characteristic of vascular dementia from discrete infarcts than the gradual progressive decline of Alzheimer disease. Gait disturbance and urinary incontinence could suggest NPH (Hakim's triad). Absence of visual hallucinations makes Lewy body dementia less likely. Frontotemporal dementia is less likely without prominent behavioural change or personality change. At least three diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Longstanding hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia. Current smoker. A right hemisphere ischaemic stroke was documented 18 months ago, correlating with the first step of decline. He has atrial fibrillation diagnosed 2 years ago and is on warfarin, though his INR has frequently been subtherapeutic.
prompts.reasoning: A documented ischaemic stroke precisely coinciding with the first cognitive step strongly supports vascular dementia. Multiple vascular risk factors and poorly controlled AF with subtherapeutic anticoagulation explain ongoing cerebrovascular events. However, Alzheimer disease frequently co-exists with cerebrovascular disease, and a mixed dementia remains possible. NPH becomes less likely given the clear vascular history. Lewy body dementia and frontotemporal dementia are now less probable.

prompts.label: Physical Examination & Cognitive Testing
prompts.text: MMSE 21/30. MoCA 17/30 with predominant deficits in executive function, attention, and processing speed; orientation and episodic memory are relatively preserved. Broad-based gait with short shuffling steps. Bilateral brisk deep tendon reflexes and bilateral upgoing plantar responses. Emotional lability observed during the consultation.
prompts.reasoning: The neuropsychological profile of executive dysfunction with relatively preserved episodic memory is more characteristic of vascular dementia from frontal-subcortical circuit disruption than of Alzheimer disease, which preferentially affects episodic memory first. Bilateral UMN signs (bilateral upgoing plantars, brisk reflexes) indicate bilateral hemisphere damage from multiple infarcts. Pseudobulbar affect from bilateral corticobulbar involvement is consistent with multi-infarct dementia. Alzheimer disease alone would not explain the bilateral UMN signs.

prompts.label: Laboratory Workup
prompts.text: FBC, TFTs, B12, folate, CRP, LFTs: all within normal limits. HbA1c 8.8%, LDL 3.6 mmol/L. INR 1.6 (subtherapeutic). Carotid ultrasound: bilateral plaques, 50% stenosis on the right. ECG: atrial fibrillation confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: Normal metabolic screen excludes reversible causes of cognitive decline (hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, etc.). Persistently uncontrolled vascular risk factors (HbA1c, LDL) and confirmed AF with subtherapeutic anticoagulation confirm ongoing cerebrovascular disease risk. These findings support a primary vascular aetiology. Neuroimaging is now required for confirmation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: established right MCA territory infarct (corresponding to the stroke 18 months ago) and multiple subcortical lacunar infarcts in the basal ganglia bilaterally. Extensive periventricular and deep white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Fazekas grade 3). Moderate generalised cortical atrophy without disproportionate hippocampal loss.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple cortical and lacunar infarcts in both hemispheres together with extensive leukoaraiosis on MRI confirm the morphological substrate of multi-infarct vascular dementia. The established right MCA infarct correlating with the first cognitive step, combined with multiple lacunar infarcts, establishes cumulative ischaemic damage as the cause of cognitive decline. Absence of disproportionate hippocampal atrophy makes Alzheimer disease as the sole diagnosis less likely. The imaging pattern confirms the clinical diagnosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male is brought to ED by paramedics after sudden collapse at work. Witnesses report he complained of a severe headache before falling. He has left-sided weakness and his level of consciousness has been declining over the past 30 minutes.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden-onset focal neurological deficit with headache and declining consciousness demands urgent assessment. Haemorrhagic stroke is of higher priority here given the headache and rapid deterioration, but ischaemic stroke cannot be excluded on history alone. SAH typically causes thunderclap headache with less focal deficit, but must be considered. The progressive course suggests an expanding lesion.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache was severe and rapid in onset but not instantaneous — it peaked over approximately 2 minutes. The left-sided weakness was noted before the headache resolved. He has vomited twice. GCS has dropped from 14 to 10 over 30 minutes. No anticoagulant use, no trauma.
prompts.reasoning: Focal neurological deficit preceding and accompanying a severe headache with vomiting and rapid GCS decline suggests raised intracranial pressure from an expanding intracranial lesion — most consistent with intracerebral haemorrhage. SAH is less likely when focal deficits precede the headache, though it remains possible. Ischaemic stroke can cause headache but the rate of GCS decline and vomiting are more typical of haemorrhage. Anticoagulation has been excluded as a contributing factor.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Severe hypertension for 15 years, poorly controlled despite antihypertensive treatment. He frequently does not take his medications and has had multiple GP visits for elevated BP readings. No anticoagulant use. Heavy alcohol consumption. No family history of vascular malformations or bleeding disorders.
prompts.reasoning: Longstanding severely uncontrolled hypertension is the most important risk factor for spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage. It is also a risk factor for saccular aneurysm formation and rupture causing SAH. No family history of vascular malformations does not exclude an AVM as the bleeding source. Both hypertensive ICH and SAH from aneurysm remain in the differential — imaging is required to distinguish them and identify the site and type of haemorrhage.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 212/124 mmHg, HR 88 bpm. GCS 10 (E2V3M5). Left arm and leg plegia (0/5 bilaterally). Conjugate gaze deviation to the right. Bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy. No meningism. Right pupil 5mm, sluggishly reactive.
prompts.reasoning: Contralateral hemiplegia with ipsilateral gaze deviation localises to the right hemisphere. Markedly elevated BP supports a hypertensive aetiology. Papilloedema indicates significantly raised ICP from a mass lesion. Absence of meningism is a minor point against SAH, though meningism may be absent early. Unilateral pupillary dilatation suggests early uncal herniation. CT is urgently required to differentiate between these two conditions.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): large hyperdense intraparenchymal collection in the right basal ganglia and putamen region, 45mL volume with surrounding hypodense oedema. Intraventricular extension into the right lateral ventricle. 8mm midline shift. No subarachnoid blood in the basal cisterns. No surface blood or gyral pattern suggesting SAH.
prompts.reasoning: CT confirms intraparenchymal haemorrhage and excludes SAH as the primary diagnosis. The basal ganglia/putamen location is the classic site for hypertensive ICH, supplied by the lenticulostriate arteries where Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms form under chronically elevated BP. Intraventricular extension is an adverse prognostic feature. No subarachnoid blood pattern consistent with aneurysm rupture is seen. Vascular imaging is required to exclude an underlying structural cause.

prompts.label: Coagulation & Vascular Imaging
prompts.text: Coagulation screen: PT, APTT, INR, and platelets all within normal limits. CT angiography: no arteriovenous malformation, no saccular aneurysm, and no other underlying vascular structural abnormality identified. No contrast enhancement within the haematoma.
prompts.reasoning: Normal coagulation excludes anticoagulation-related or coagulopathic haemorrhage. Absence of AVM, aneurysm, or structural lesion on CTA confirms primary hypertensive aetiology. The combination of the classic deep location (basal ganglia/putamen), markedly elevated BP, longstanding poorly controlled hypertension, normal coagulation, and absence of structural vascular lesion establishes hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage as the definitive diagnosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old female presents to ED reporting a sudden severe headache that began while she was gardening approximately 3 hours ago. She describes it as the worst headache she has ever experienced.
prompts.reasoning: A severe headache reaching peak intensity rapidly demands urgent evaluation. The differential at this stage is broad: SAH is the most dangerous diagnosis to exclude, but migraine, hypertensive crisis, CVST, meningitis, and primary headache disorders including cluster headache remain plausible until further information is obtained. The patient's description of severity alone does not confirm the aetiology.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She describes the onset as instantaneous — maximal intensity within 1 to 2 seconds, unlike any headache she has had before. She has vomited twice since onset. No fever. No preceding aura or visual symptoms. No history of similar headaches. Her husband found her lying on the garden path, conscious but distressed.
prompts.reasoning: Instantaneous onset ('thunderclap') with vomiting is a strong predictor of SAH and substantially raises its probability. However, CVST can present with thunderclap headache, and haemorrhagic stroke occasionally presents similarly. Migraine and cluster headache very rarely present with truly instantaneous onset — these diagnoses are now less likely but require imaging before exclusion. Meningitis without fever is less probable.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has hypertension, currently taking amlodipine with incomplete BP control. She smokes 15 cigarettes per day. Her father died of a cerebral haemorrhage in his early 50s. No prior neurological history. No anticoagulant use.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension and smoking are risk factors for both SAH from aneurysm and hypertensive ICH. A family history of cerebral haemorrhage at a young age raises the possibility of an inherited vascular abnormality such as a saccular aneurysm. No prothrombotic risk factors are identified, making CVST less likely. The working differential now centres on haemorrhagic aetiologies.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 188/102 mmHg, HR 92 bpm regular, temperature 37.2°C. GCS 14/15. Neck stiffness present. Kernig's sign positive. No focal neurological deficits. No papilloedema. Fundoscopy: no subhyaloid haemorrhage identified.
prompts.reasoning: Meningism (neck stiffness and positive Kernig's sign) with thunderclap headache and no fever is strongly suggestive of SAH — blood in the subarachnoid space causes meningeal irritation without infection. However, bacterial meningitis must remain in the differential as meningism is the shared physical finding; fever may be absent early. No focal deficits and no papilloedema make a large focal mass lesion less likely. CT is required before LP.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): hyperdense blood in the basal cisterns, bilateral Sylvian fissures, and the interhemispheric fissure. No intraparenchymal haemorrhage. No hydrocephalus. No mass lesion.
prompts.reasoning: Hyperdense (white) blood in the subarachnoid cisterns on non-contrast CT is diagnostic of subarachnoid haemorrhage. This excludes bacterial meningitis, hypertensive ICH (no parenchymal blood), and CVST as primary diagnoses. CT is approximately 98% sensitive for SAH within 6 hours of onset. The source of haemorrhage must now be identified.

prompts.label: CT Angiography
prompts.text: CT angiography of the cerebral vasculature: 7mm saccular aneurysm identified at the junction of the left internal carotid artery and posterior communicating artery. Smooth dome with a narrow neck. No other aneurysms detected. Lumbar puncture (performed as CT was done >6 hours after onset): xanthochromic CSF confirmed on spectrophotometry.
prompts.reasoning: A saccular aneurysm at the ICA-PCoA junction is the identified bleeding source — one of the most common locations for berry aneurysms at the Circle of Willis. LP with xanthochromia on spectrophotometry provides confirmatory evidence of blood breakdown products in the CSF, important when CT is negative or borderline. The diagnosis of aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage is confirmed. Neurosurgical or endovascular management is required urgently to prevent rebleed.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old female is referred to the neurology outpatient clinic after an MRI ordered for recurrent headaches incidentally identified an abnormality in the region of the Circle of Willis. She has no acute neurological symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: An incidental vascular or structural abnormality at the Circle of Willis on MRI in an otherwise asymptomatic patient has several possible diagnoses. Saccular aneurysm, AVM, dural AV fistula, meningioma, and venous anomalies all represent plausible findings. The nature of the abnormality cannot be determined from the referral letter alone — further characterisation with vascular imaging is required.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Her headaches are bilateral, pressure-like, and mild — similar in character to tension headaches she has experienced for years. She has never had a sudden severe headache or a headache that was different in quality from her usual ones. No diplopia, no visual disturbance, no focal weakness, and no hearing disturbance.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of thunderclap headache makes prior aneurysm rupture unlikely but does not exclude an unruptured aneurysm. Absence of pulsatile tinnitus makes dural AV fistula less likely, as these often produce objective bruit heard by the patient. No seizure history reduces the probability of AVM or cortical meningioma. Saccular aneurysm and AVM remain the leading possibilities for a vascular abnormality at this location.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Family History
prompts.text: Hypertension diagnosed 2 years ago, currently managed with perindopril. Non-smoker. Her brother was recently diagnosed with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPCKD). She has not yet been screened for ADPCKD. No family history of intracranial haemorrhage.
prompts.reasoning: ADPCKD carries a significantly increased risk of saccular aneurysm formation — this familial risk factor substantially raises the pre-test probability of a berry aneurysm in this patient. Hypertension is also a risk factor for aneurysm formation and rupture. AVM remains possible as a congenital vascular malformation, but is not associated with ADPCKD. Meningioma and dural AV fistula are now less likely given the Circle of Willis location and risk factor context.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 152/94 mmHg on current antihypertensive therapy. Neurological examination entirely normal — no cranial nerve deficits, no focal motor or sensory signs, no papilloedema. No cranial bruit on auscultation over the skull and orbits. Abdominal examination: no palpable renal masses.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination confirms the lesion is currently asymptomatic and unruptured. No cranial bruit does not exclude AVM — bruits are present in less than 25% of cases. No CN III palsy or cranial nerve compression from the aneurysm. Incompletely controlled hypertension is noted. Both saccular aneurysm and AVM remain possible — dedicated vascular imaging is required for characterisation.

prompts.label: CT Angiography & MR Angiography
prompts.text: CT angiography and time-of-flight MRA: 6mm saccular outpouching at the right internal carotid artery-posterior communicating artery junction. Smooth dome, narrow neck. No nidus or flow voids to suggest AVM. No venous anomaly or dural AV fistula identified. No other lesions.
prompts.reasoning: The saccular morphology, ICA-PCoA location, and absence of any nidus or feeding/draining vessel complex confirms a saccular aneurysm rather than AVM. This is one of the classic locations for berry aneurysms. AVM is now excluded by imaging. Renal screening for ADPCKD should be arranged. Management decisions (intervention versus surveillance) depend on size, location, morphology, patient age, and rupture risk calculation.

prompts.label: Renal Screening & Management Planning
prompts.text: Renal ultrasound: multiple bilateral renal cysts consistent with ADPCKD. Genetics referral arranged. Neurovascular multidisciplinary team review: PHASES score calculated at 2.5% 5-year rupture risk for this aneurysm. Shared decision-making discussion held — options of endovascular coiling versus surveillance MRA every 6 months discussed with the patient.
prompts.reasoning: Confirmation of ADPCKD establishes the predisposing connective tissue abnormality — ADPCKD is associated with a 5–10-fold increased risk of saccular aneurysm. The diagnosis is confirmed: an unruptured saccular (berry) aneurysm at the ICA-PCoA junction in a patient with ADPCKD and hypertension. The PHASES score guides the treatment decision. The lesion arises from a focal defect in the internal elastic lamina and media at the arterial bifurcation point.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old male presents after a first-ever generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 3 minutes, witnessed by his flatmate. He has no prior history of epilepsy.
prompts.reasoning: A first unprovoked seizure in a young adult requires urgent neuroimaging to exclude a structural cause. Brain tumour, AVM, cavernous malformation, abscess, and encephalitis are all structural or infective causes of new-onset seizure that must be systematically excluded. Idiopathic epilepsy remains possible but is a diagnosis of exclusion.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The seizure was generalised from the outset without a clear aura or focal onset. Post-ictal drowsiness lasted 20 minutes. He reports no fever, no recent headache, no neck stiffness, and no head trauma. He mentions he has noticed a faint whooshing sound in his ears intermittently over the past year, though he attributed it to stress.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of fever and neck stiffness makes encephalitis and meningitis less likely. No clear focal onset reduces the probability of a small focal lesion driving a partial seizure with secondary generalisation, though a focal onset can be unwitnessed. Intermittent pulsatile tinnitus heard by the patient could reflect high-flow arteriovenous shunting — a subtle but notable feature pointing toward a vascular malformation. Brain tumour, cavernous malformation, and AVM remain the leading structural diagnoses.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Family History
prompts.text: No significant medical history. No anticoagulants or medications. No family history of brain tumours, vascular malformations, or hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia. Non-smoker, minimal alcohol. His development and prior health have been entirely normal.
prompts.reasoning: No family history of hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT/Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome) does not exclude AVM — most are sporadic. No clear epidemiological risk factors favour brain tumour or abscess. Cavernous malformations can also be sporadic or familial. All three structural diagnoses remain viable — neuroimaging is required.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. BP 128/76 mmHg. Neurological examination entirely normal — no focal motor or sensory deficits, no visual field abnormality, no cranial nerve palsy, no papilloedema. MoCA 28/30 with mild attention difficulty, likely post-ictal. Auscultation of the skull: no detectable cranial bruit.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination does not exclude any of the three leading structural diagnoses — all can be clinically silent until they cause a seizure or haemorrhage. Absence of papilloedema argues against a large space-occupying tumour with significant mass effect, but small tumours may not cause papilloedema. No cranial bruit — present in fewer than 25% of AVMs — does not exclude a vascular malformation. MRI is essential.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (T2 and FLAIR): a compact cluster of serpiginous flow voids in the left temporal lobe measuring approximately 3cm in diameter, with surrounding haemosiderin staining. No surrounding oedema or mass effect. DWI: no acute ischaemia. No mass lesion or ring-enhancing lesion on contrast sequences.
prompts.reasoning: Flow voids on MRI represent rapid arteriovenous shunting through an abnormal nidus — this pattern is characteristic of AVM. Haemosiderin staining indicates prior microhaemorrhage, explaining the seizure. There is no mass lesion to suggest tumour and no cavernous malformation signal pattern (which would show a 'popcorn' appearance). AVM is now the single remaining diagnosis — catheter angiography is required for definitive characterisation.

prompts.label: Catheter Angiography
prompts.text: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA): a nidus in the left temporal lobe fed by branches of the left middle cerebral artery, with early venous drainage into the superficial Sylvian vein. Spetzler-Martin grade II (small size, eloquent cortex, superficial drainage). No associated aneurysm. Annual haemorrhage risk estimated 2–4%.
prompts.reasoning: DSA is the gold standard for AVM characterisation — defining nidus morphology, feeding arteries, and draining veins with precision required for treatment planning. The AVM consists of an abnormal tangle of dysplastic vessels connecting arterial to venous circulation without a normal capillary bed. The Spetzler-Martin grade II designation informs procedural risk assessment for stereotactic radiosurgery, embolisation, or microsurgery.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 79-year-old female is referred to the neurology clinic by her GP following three episodes over the past 6 months in which she transiently lost function in her right arm, each lasting 10 to 20 minutes before fully resolving.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent transient focal neurological episodes (TFNEs) in an elderly female have a broad differential. TIA from atherosclerotic or cardioembolic disease is the most immediately dangerous possibility. Focal seizures with transient postictal deficit, cortical spreading depression, and structural lesions must also be considered. The differential cannot be narrowed without characterising the nature of the episodes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The episodes are stereotyped and always involve the same spreading positive sensory symptoms — tingling that begins in the fingers and spreads to the wrist and forearm over 1 to 2 minutes — before weakness develops and then resolves completely. There is no headache during or after episodes. She also reports memory difficulties developing gradually over 2 years.
prompts.reasoning: Positive sensory symptoms spreading in a march pattern are characteristic of focal cortical irritation — either a focal seizure or cortical spreading depression — rather than TIA, which typically causes negative symptoms (loss of function). The march of positive symptoms makes TIA less likely. Focal seizures with subsequent transient motor weakness remain a strong differential. Co-existing cognitive decline adds clinical complexity and raises the question of a unifying diagnosis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has no history of hypertension, diabetes, atrial fibrillation, or prior cardiac disease. She was diagnosed with early-onset probable Alzheimer disease 2 years ago based on cognitive testing and memory clinic assessment. She takes donepezil and aspirin 75mg daily. Non-smoker, no alcohol excess.
prompts.reasoning: The absence of vascular risk factors makes atherosclerotic TIA less likely. However, absence of hypertension is a particularly useful discriminating feature: hypertensive small vessel disease produces deep (basal ganglia, thalamic) lesions, whereas cerebral amyloid angiopathy preferentially affects cortical and leptomeningeal vessels. The co-existence of cognitive decline previously attributed to Alzheimer disease is notable — CAA and AD frequently co-exist and share amyloid pathology. Focal seizures remain a strong possibility. The specific neuroimaging pattern will be diagnostic.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 126/76 mmHg. Neurological examination currently normal between episodes. MMSE 24/30 with short-term memory and orientation deficits. No focal motor or sensory signs. Normal fundoscopy. No papilloedema.
prompts.reasoning: Normal inter-ictal neurological examination is consistent with both CAA and focal seizures — neither diagnosis requires persistent deficits between episodes. Normal BP reinforces that this is not hypertensive small vessel disease. Cognitive testing confirms genuine impairment consistent with either co-existing AD or CAA-related cognitive decline. The diagnosis will be established by neuroimaging — specifically MRI with haemosensitive sequences.

prompts.label: MRI Brain (Standard Sequences)
prompts.text: MRI brain FLAIR: multiple cortical and subcortical signal abnormalities in the posterior parietal and occipital regions bilaterally. Linear hypointensity along several cortical sulci consistent with superficial siderosis. No deep (basal ganglia or thalamic) lesions. White matter changes are present but mild.
prompts.reasoning: Cortical and subcortical signal abnormalities in a lobar distribution — posterior parieto-occipital predominance — with cortical superficial siderosis (haemosiderin deposition along the cortical surface) is the MRI signature of cerebral amyloid angiopathy. Deep lesions are absent, distinguishing this from hypertensive small vessel disease. Superficial siderosis is a consequence of recurrent cortical microhaemorrhages from fragile amyloid-laden cortical vessels. Focal seizures are now less likely as the primary diagnosis.

prompts.label: MRI — Susceptibility-Weighted Imaging (SWI)
prompts.text: MRI SWI sequence: more than 10 cortical microhaemorrhages (blooming artefacts) in the posterior parietal and occipital regions bilaterally. Lobar distribution confirmed. No deep haemorrhages. ApoE genotyping: ApoE ε4/ε4 homozygote. Boston Criteria v2.0: probable cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
prompts.reasoning: SWI is exquisitely sensitive to haemosiderin — microhaemorrhages appear as dark 'blooms'. More than 10 cortical microhaemorrhages in a lobar distribution combined with cortical superficial siderosis satisfies the Boston Criteria for probable CAA. ApoE ε4/ε4 homozygosity is the strongest genetic risk factor for both CAA and Alzheimer disease, explaining their co-existence. The transient focal neurological episodes are 'amyloid spells' — cortical spreading depression or focal seizures triggered by cortical microhaemorrhages.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 82-year-old female is brought to ED by ambulance after a witnessed fall from standing height, striking her head on a tiled floor. She was initially responsive at the scene but has become progressively less responsive during transport.
prompts.reasoning: Head trauma with witnessed impact and declining consciousness in an elderly patient demands urgent CT. All forms of traumatic intracranial haemorrhage — subdural, extradural, and subarachnoid — must be considered. In the elderly, cerebral atrophy stretches bridging veins, predisposing to subdural haemorrhage even from relatively minor impacts. A stroke causing the fall must also be excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: GCS on ambulance arrival was 13; it has now declined to 10 over the 40 minutes since the fall. There was no period of full recovery after the initial impact — the decline has been continuous. She is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation; her INR was checked 48 hours ago and was 3.2.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive neurological deterioration without a classic lucid interval is more consistent with acute subdural haematoma or cerebral contusion than with extradural haematoma, which classically follows a lucid interval then rapid deterioration. Therapeutic warfarin with a supratherapeutic INR significantly increases the risk of haematoma expansion and worsens prognosis. Ischaemic stroke becomes less likely as the cause of the fall given the subsequent progressive deterioration from the head impact. Urgent CT and INR reversal are required simultaneously.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Atrial fibrillation (on warfarin), hypertension, and mild cognitive impairment documented over the past 2 years. Lives alone. Multiple previous falls recorded in her medical record. No known coagulopathy beyond warfarin anticoagulation.
prompts.reasoning: A history of recurrent falls, mild cognitive impairment suggesting cerebral atrophy, and warfarin anticoagulation constitute a classic triad of risk factors for acute subdural haematoma in the elderly. Cerebral atrophy stretches the bridging veins traversing the subdural space, making them vulnerable to rupture even from low-energy impacts. Supratherapeutic anticoagulation further impairs haemostasis. Extradural haematoma is less likely given the absence of a lucid interval and the patient's age (the dura is more adherent in the elderly).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 10/15 (E2V3M5). BP 166/92 mmHg, HR 88 bpm irregular. Temperature 36.9°C. Right-sided hemiparesis (arm 2/5, leg 3/5). Left pupil 5mm sluggishly reactive; right pupil 3mm reactive. No meningism. Scalp laceration present over the left parietal region.
prompts.reasoning: Right hemiparesis with an ipsilateral left-sided scalp laceration indicates a left hemisphere lesion — contralateral to the weakness. Left pupillary dilatation suggests early uncal herniation from a left-sided mass causing left CN III compression. This crossed presentation (left lesion, right weakness, left dilated pupil) is consistent with a left hemispheric space-occupying haematoma. The irregular pulse confirms AF. Both acute SDH and a large cerebral contusion could produce this picture — CT is the critical next step.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): hyperdense crescent-shaped collection over the left cerebral convexity, following the brain surface and crossing the coronal suture. Maximum thickness 14mm. 9mm midline shift to the right. Underlying cortical sulci are effaced. No underlying contusion. Concurrent small subarachnoid haemorrhage in the left Sylvian fissure.
prompts.reasoning: A hyperdense crescent-shaped collection that follows the brain surface and crosses suture lines is the hallmark CT appearance of acute subdural haematoma — distinguishing it from extradural haematoma (biconvex, bounded by sutures) and cerebral contusion (intraparenchymal). The subdural space communicates freely, allowing the collection to spread. Midline shift >5mm and clinical deterioration are indications for urgent neurosurgical assessment and haematoma evacuation. INR reversal is urgently required.

prompts.label: Coagulation & Surgical Management
prompts.text: INR on admission: 3.8 (supratherapeutic). Urgent reversal initiated with IV vitamin K and 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (target INR <1.5). Emergency craniotomy performed after INR reversal: dark venous blood clot confirmed beneath the dura with torn bridging vein identified intraoperatively. Post-operative CT: haematoma evacuated, midline shift resolved. GCS improved to 13 post-operatively.
prompts.reasoning: Dark venous blood (distinct from bright arterial blood of EDH) and a torn bridging vein identified intraoperatively confirm the acute SDH mechanism. Anticoagulation reversal prior to surgery was essential to minimise intraoperative and post-operative haemorrhagic risk. The diagnosis of acute traumatic subdural haematoma from bridging vein rupture in an anticoagulated elderly patient with cerebral atrophy is confirmed. Outcome is significantly worse than in younger patients — mortality in elderly anticoagulated patients with acute SDH is 30–60% even with surgery.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 74-year-old male is brought to his GP by his daughter, who has noticed he has become increasingly forgetful and confused over the past 4 weeks. He has also been moving more slowly and had two recent near-falls.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute cognitive decline with gait impairment in an elderly male has a broad differential. Dementia subtypes (Alzheimer, vascular), normal pressure hydrocephalus (with its classic triad), structural lesions including brain tumour and chronic subdural haematoma, and infective collections such as subdural empyema are all plausible. Chronic SDH is crucially important to identify early as it is reversible with appropriate intervention.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: On direct questioning, the patient recalls bumping his head on a car door frame approximately 6 weeks ago. He dismissed it at the time as trivial and did not seek medical attention. He has had mild intermittent headaches since then but has not had fever, vomiting, or visual disturbance.
prompts.reasoning: A head injury 6 weeks ago raises the possibility of a chronic subdural haematoma developing over the subsequent weeks. However, many elderly patients with chronic SDH cannot recall a precipitating injury, and cognitive decline beginning around the same time as a minor injury could also be coincidental. Alzheimer disease, NPH, and brain tumour remain plausible — the temporal association with head injury is clinically important but not confirmatory. Subdural empyema is now less likely without fever.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He lives alone since his wife died 18 months ago. He takes aspirin for secondary prevention after a myocardial infarction 3 years ago. He drinks 2 to 3 units of alcohol daily. No anticoagulants. No prior neurological diagnoses. A CT scan performed 4 years ago for an unrelated reason noted mild generalised cerebral atrophy.
prompts.reasoning: Cerebral atrophy stretches the bridging veins traversing the subdural space, significantly increasing the risk of subdural haematoma after even minor trauma. Aspirin use impairs platelet function and may promote haematoma expansion. Alcohol contributes to both cerebral atrophy and impaired haemostasis. Living alone means gradual cognitive changes may go unobserved for weeks. Brain tumour is less likely without other systemic features. All three remaining diagnoses remain plausible pending examination and imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14/15. MMSE 23/30 with deficits in orientation and attention. Mild right upper limb drift. Brisk reflexes on the right, equivocal right plantar response. Broad-based gait with shortened stride length. BP 158/88 mmHg. No papilloedema. No meningism.
prompts.reasoning: Mild right-sided UMN signs (drift, brisk reflexes) combined with cognitive impairment and gait disturbance could be consistent with either a left hemisphere space-occupying lesion (chronic SDH or tumour) or NPH affecting frontal-subcortical circuits. Absence of papilloedema does not exclude chronic SDH. The combination of gait impairment, cognitive change, and mild focal signs in an elderly patient who recently sustained a head injury, on aspirin, with known cerebral atrophy, significantly elevates the suspicion for chronic SDH over a primary neurodegenerative condition. Urgent neuroimaging is required.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): bilateral crescent-shaped collections over both cerebral convexities, isodense to slightly hypodense compared to adjacent brain parenchyma, right greater than left. Right-sided collection measures 17mm with 6mm midline shift. Underlying cortical sulci are effaced. No discrete mass lesion.
prompts.reasoning: Crescent-shaped collections crossing suture lines are consistent with subdural haematomas. The isodense appearance (similar density to brain parenchyma) is characteristic of chronic subdural haematoma — haemoglobin degrades over 2 to 4 weeks, causing the collection to become isodense then hypodense. This is distinct from acute SDH (hyperdense) and distinguishes the diagnosis from NPH (ventricular dilation, no collection). Bilateral collections are common in chronic SDH. Midline shift indicates significant mass effect.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Surgical Management
prompts.text: MRI brain T1/T2: bilateral subdural collections with mixed signal intensity, indicating re-bleeding within chronic collections (heterogeneous content). Burr-hole drainage performed under local anaesthesia. Intraoperative fluid: dark brown, motor oil-like appearance confirming chronic haematoma. Post-operative cognitive improvement noted within 48 hours of drainage.
prompts.reasoning: Mixed signal intensity on MRI reflects blood products at varying stages of degradation, consistent with a chronic haematoma with repeated minor re-bleeds from fragile membrane vessels. The characteristic dark brown 'motor oil' appearance of intraoperative fluid is the definitive operative confirmation of chronic SDH. Rapid cognitive improvement after drainage confirms the haematoma was responsible for the clinical syndrome and distinguishes this from a primary neurodegenerative condition. The pathological basis is initial bridging vein rupture followed by organisation, membrane formation, and recurrent re-bleeding from the fragile outer membrane.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old male is brought to ED after sustaining a blow to the right side of his head during a cricket match. He was briefly unconscious for approximately 2 minutes, after which he regained consciousness and was talking normally to teammates.
prompts.reasoning: Head trauma with brief loss of consciousness followed by apparent recovery in a young adult requires careful monitoring and assessment. While concussion is common after such an injury, more serious intracranial pathology must not be assumed to have been excluded by the apparent recovery. All forms of traumatic intracranial pathology remain plausible at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over the 45 minutes following the injury, he has developed a rapidly worsening headache and vomited twice. His teammates report he is becoming increasingly confused and agitated. His GCS has dropped from 15 to 12 since arrival.
prompts.reasoning: A period of apparent normality followed by progressive neurological deterioration — a lucid interval pattern — is a recognised feature of expanding intracranial haematomas, most classically associated with extradural haematoma from arterial bleeding. However, acute subdural haematoma and cerebral contusion with developing oedema can also cause progressive deterioration after an initial lucid period. Concussion alone would not explain the trajectory of clinical deterioration with vomiting and declining GCS.

prompts.label: Past Medical History
prompts.text: He is otherwise fit and well with no significant past medical history. He takes no medications, including no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents. No known bleeding disorder. No prior head injuries.
prompts.reasoning: No coagulopathy or anticoagulant use means the haemorrhage is purely traumatic. The mechanism — a direct lateral head impact in a young person with no cerebral atrophy — is consistent with either EDH (arterial, from meningeal vessel disruption) or less commonly acute SDH (venous, from bridging vein rupture) or contusion. Young patients have tightly adherent dura, making EDH from arterial stripping more likely than in the elderly, but clinical distinction requires imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 11/15. BP 148/78 mmHg, HR 54 bpm. Pupils: right 6mm dilated and unresponsive; left 3mm reactive. Left-sided hemiparesis (arm and leg 3/5). Right-sided scalp haematoma at the temporoparietal region. Temperature 36.8°C.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension with bradycardia suggests Cushing's response — a sign of critically elevated intracranial pressure and impending herniation. Ipsilateral (right) pupillary dilatation with contralateral (left) hemiparesis indicates a right-sided compressive mass lesion causing ipsilateral CN III compression from uncal herniation. Both EDH and acute SDH can produce this picture with this degree of urgency. Cerebral contusion alone would be less likely to produce such dramatic signs without haematoma. Immediate CT is required.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection at the right temporoparietal region, 3cm maximal thickness, not crossing the coronal suture. Midline shift of 12mm to the left. Temporal bone linear fracture crossing the middle meningeal arterial groove. No underlying contusion. Active contrast blush ('swirl sign') suggests ongoing bleeding.
prompts.reasoning: A biconvex lens-shaped hyperdense collection bounded by cranial sutures is the pathognomonic CT appearance of extradural haematoma — distinguishing it from subdural haematoma (crescent-shaped, crosses suture lines). The collection sits between the skull and the dura, stripped by expanding arterial pressure. The temporal bone fracture crossing the middle meningeal groove confirms the bleeding source. Active swirl sign indicates ongoing arterial haemorrhage. This is a neurosurgical emergency.

prompts.label: Emergency Craniotomy & Outcome
prompts.text: Emergency right temporal craniotomy performed within 75 minutes of CT: middle meningeal artery laceration at the fracture site confirmed as the bleeding source. 60mL of arterial haematoma evacuated. Dura tacked to the inner table of the skull. GCS improved to 14/15 within 24 hours post-operatively — excellent neurological recovery.
prompts.reasoning: Intraoperative confirmation of middle meningeal artery laceration at the fracture site with arterial haematoma evacuation confirms the diagnosis of extradural haematoma. EDH is caused in 95% of cases by middle meningeal artery rupture from temporal bone fracture, producing a rapidly expanding arterial haematoma under pressure. Young patients — with a tightly adherent dura separating the lucid interval from critical deterioration — have an excellent prognosis with timely surgery. Mortality is less than 5% with decompression within 4 hours but approaches 100% untreated.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male presents to his GP with a 6-week history of progressive headaches and a single episode of a generalised tonic-clonic seizure yesterday. His wife reports he has been increasingly forgetful and 'not himself' over the past month.
prompts.reasoning: New-onset seizure with progressive headache and behavioural change in a middle-aged adult demands exclusion of a space-occupying lesion. The differential remains broad: primary brain tumours, secondary metastases, abscess, meningioma, subdural haematoma, and less likely hypertensive encephalopathy. Age and progression favour tumour, but nothing is confirmed.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headaches are diffuse, worse in the morning, and have been steadily worsening. Over the past three weeks his wife has noticed progressive left arm clumsiness and he has had difficulty finding words in conversation. No fever, no recent illness, no travel abroad.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive focal deficits (left arm weakness, expressive language difficulty) localise to the right hemisphere. Morning headache worsening suggests raised ICP. Absence of fever makes abscess less likely but does not exclude it. A progressive space-occupying lesion is the leading category — subdural haematoma is less typical without head trauma history.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Ex-smoker (15 pack-years, quit 8 years ago). Treated hypertension. No known malignancy. No prior cranial irradiation. No immunosuppression. No recent dental work, sinusitis, or otitis media. Family history: father died of lung cancer, no family history of brain tumours.
prompts.reasoning: No known primary malignancy makes metastases less likely but does not exclude occult lung or other primary. Absence of infection source or immunosuppression makes abscess unlikely. Prior smoking history maintains metastases as plausible. Meningioma and primary glioma both remain equally plausible at this stage.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14/15. Bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy. Left arm power 3/5 with pronator drift. Mild expressive dysphasia. Brisk left-sided deep tendon reflexes. Left plantar extensor. No meningism. Temperature 37.1°C.
prompts.reasoning: Papilloedema confirms raised ICP. UMN pattern left arm weakness and dysphasia localise to the right fronto-parietal region. Absent fever with no meningism makes abscess very unlikely — removed from differential. Low-grade astrocytoma less likely given rapid progression and significant raised ICP. Primary high-grade glioma, metastases, and meningioma remain.

prompts.label: Bloods and Staging CT
prompts.text: FBC, CRP, and metabolic panel normal. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no primary malignancy identified. CT head: large heterogeneous right frontal mass with central necrosis, surrounding oedema, and 8mm midline shift to the left.
prompts.reasoning: No systemic primary malignancy on staging CT makes metastases substantially less likely — removed. CT appearance of central necrosis within the mass suggests a high-grade primary tumour rather than meningioma, which typically enhances uniformly without necrosis. However, MRI with contrast is required to characterise further.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: irregular ring-enhancing right frontal mass (4.5cm) with central necrosis, extensive surrounding FLAIR hyperintensity (oedema and infiltration), and no dural tail. Stereotactic biopsy: densely cellular astrocytic tumour with pseudopalisading necrosis, microvascular proliferation, and >10 mitoses per high-power field. IDH wild-type. MGMT promoter unmethylated.
prompts.reasoning: Ring enhancement with central necrosis and absence of a dural tail excludes meningioma (which is extra-axial with dural tail and uniform enhancement). Histopathology confirms WHO Grade IV glioblastoma: pseudopalisading necrosis and microvascular proliferation are its defining features. IDH wild-type and MGMT unmethylated are the most common molecular profile of de novo GBM and carry the worst prognosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric ED with a 6-week history of daily morning vomiting, progressive unsteadiness when walking, and intermittent headaches that wake him from sleep.
prompts.reasoning: Morning vomiting with gait disturbance and nocturnal headache in a child strongly suggests raised ICP from a posterior fossa lesion. The leading paediatric posterior fossa tumours are pilocytic astrocytoma, medulloblastoma, and ependymoma. Brainstem glioma can also present with gait disturbance. Abdominal causes of vomiting remain on the initial broad differential until neurological evaluation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The vomiting is most prominent on waking before he eats, and his parents note it improved transiently when he sits up. The gait unsteadiness is progressive — he now holds walls when walking. No fever, no ear pain, no visual changes reported by parents. His teacher notes declining handwriting quality over 2 months.
prompts.reasoning: Positional pattern (worse on waking, improved sitting up) and progressive cerebellar signs strongly support raised ICP from a posterior fossa mass. Declining handwriting may indicate fine motor involvement or early cerebellar dysfunction. Abdominal causes are now excluded. The main differential remains among the three commonest paediatric posterior fossa tumours.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Normal birth history and neurodevelopmental milestones — walking and talking at appropriate ages. No family history of NF1, NF2, tuberous sclerosis, or other phakomatoses. No prior cranial irradiation or chemotherapy. Fully vaccinated. No recent infections. Previously an active, healthy child.
prompts.reasoning: No NF1 association (which would increase risk of optic pathway or cerebellar astrocytoma, but absence does not exclude pilocytic astrocytoma). No prior radiotherapy (which predisposes to secondary tumours). All three main posterior fossa tumours remain equally plausible — history alone cannot distinguish them.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II). Nystagmus on right lateral gaze. Right-sided limb ataxia — past-pointing on finger-nose test, dysdiadochokinesia, and positive heel-shin test. Gait: wide-based with rightward deviation. Truncal stability preserved when sitting. No cranial nerve palsies. No meningism.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral (right) limb ataxia with ipsilateral nystagmus localises to the right cerebellar hemisphere — the right cerebellar hemisphere controls right limb coordination. Preserved truncal stability with lateralised limb signs suggests a hemispheric rather than vermis lesion. Medulloblastoma more typically presents with vermian ataxia (truncal, midline). Pilocytic astrocytoma favours the cerebellar hemisphere. However, all three tumours can show overlapping features — imaging is required.

prompts.label: Routine Bloods
prompts.text: FBC, CRP, metabolic panel, and LFTs all within normal range. No anaemia, no electrolyte disturbance. Urinalysis normal. Blood cultures sent: no growth at 48 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Normal inflammatory markers and cultures make infectious causes (abscess, cerebellitis) effectively excluded. Ependymoma is less consistent with pure hemispheric ataxia without lower cranial nerve signs or floor-of-4th-ventricle involvement. MRI is now essential for definitive characterisation. The differential now rests between pilocytic astrocytoma and medulloblastoma.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: well-circumscribed cystic lesion (4cm) in the right cerebellar hemisphere with a discrete avidly enhancing mural nodule at the lateral wall.
