prompts.reasoning: Restricted diffusion on DWI (bright DWI, dark ADC) is the key MRI feature distinguishing abscess from tumour — viscous pus with high cell density restricts water molecule diffusion. Streptococcus anginosus (milleri group) is the classic pathogen in dental/abdominal-origin cerebral abscesses. Histological stages of abscess: early cerebritis (oedema, necrosis) → late cerebritis → early capsule (fibroblast ring forms) → late capsule (thick collagen wall). Treatment: surgical drainage + 6-8 weeks IV antibiotics (penicillin + metronidazole for dental origin).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old female is brought to ED by her husband with a 3-day history of worsening headache, fever, and increasingly erratic behaviour. This morning she had a tonic-clonic seizure witnessed by her husband.
prompts.reasoning: Encephalitis (altered consciousness, behavioural change, seizure) with fever must be treated as HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise — it is the most common cause of fatal sporadic encephalitis in adults and is treatable. Delay in aciclovir initiation significantly worsens mortality. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) is an important mimic, particularly in young women, but fever and rapid progression favour infectious cause.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The behavioural change preceded the headache — her husband noticed she seemed confused, repetitive in conversation, and had an unusual 'strange smell' sensation she described before each episode of confusion. Today's seizure involved lip-smacking and right arm automatisms before generalising. She has also had olfactory and gustatory hallucinations.
prompts.reasoning: Focal temporal lobe features — olfactory/gustatory hallucinations, complex partial seizures with lip-smacking automatisms, memory disturbance and personality change — precisely localise the pathology to the temporal lobe. HSV-1 has a predilection for the temporal lobes and limbic system, producing a necrotising haemorrhagic temporal lobe encephalitis. Olfactory auras and complex partial seizures are hallmarks of temporal lobe involvement.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No significant past medical history. No immunosuppression. No history of herpes labialis (cold sores). No recent travel. No sick contacts. Not on any regular medications. Never had a prior seizure. No family history of epilepsy or autoimmune disease.
prompts.reasoning: HSV-1 encephalitis in adults typically arises from reactivation of latent HSV-1 in the trigeminal ganglion with retrograde spread along branches of CN V to the temporal and frontal lobes — NOT necessarily from active herpes labialis. Primary HSV-1 infection can also cause encephalitis. HSV-2 typically causes encephalitis in neonates (transmitted perinatally) and aseptic meningitis in adults during primary genital herpes infection.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.1°C, HR 116 bpm, BP 132/78 mmHg. GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Confused and disoriented. Neck stiffness present (mild). No focal limb weakness. Bilateral plantar responses equivocal. Post-ictal drowsiness noted. No skin rash, no oral herpetic ulcers. MMSE not assessable due to confusion.
prompts.reasoning: Encephalitis (altered consciousness + behavioural change) with fever and meningism. The absence of focal limb weakness reflects predominantly limbic/temporal involvement rather than motor cortex. Post-ictal drowsiness following the seizure explains the reduced GCS. IV aciclovir (10 mg/kg TDS) must be started IMMEDIATELY without waiting for LP or MRI results, as the mortality of untreated HSV encephalitis exceeds 70%.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (FLAIR and DWI): hyperintense signal change involving the left medial temporal lobe, hippocampus, insula, and inferior frontal lobe — with haemorrhagic foci seen on T2*. The changes spare the basal ganglia. EEG: periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) over the left temporal region.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral (or asymmetric bilateral) temporal lobe FLAIR hyperintensity — particularly involving the hippocampus, insula, and inferior frontal lobe — is the MRI signature of HSV encephalitis. Haemorrhagic foci on T2* reflect the necrotising haemorrhagic nature of HSV-1 encephalitis. Pathologically: haemorrhagic necrosis with Cowdry type A intranuclear inclusion bodies in neurons and astrocytes. EEG PLEDs over the temporal lobe strongly support the diagnosis.

prompts.label: CSF PCR & Histopathology
prompts.text: CSF: opening pressure 220 mmH₂O, 120 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 1.1 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (ratio 0.6). Red blood cells in CSF (xanthochromia absent — RBCs from haemorrhagic necrosis, not SAH). CSF HSV PCR: positive for HSV-1. CSF culture: negative for bacteria. If brain biopsy were performed (rarely needed): Cowdry type A intranuclear eosinophilic inclusion bodies in neurons, haemorrhagic necrosis, perivascular lymphocytic cuffing in the temporal lobe. Treatment: IV aciclovir 14-21 days; outcome dramatically improved with early treatment.
prompts.reasoning: CSF HSV PCR is the gold standard — sensitivity 96%, specificity 99%. RBCs in CSF (without xanthochromia) reflect haemorrhagic temporal lobe necrosis. Lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and normal glucose is typical of viral encephalitis. Pathological hallmark: Cowdry A intranuclear eosinophilic inclusions (representing viral replication) in neurons and astrocytes of the temporal lobe, with surrounding haemorrhagic necrosis and lymphocytic infiltration. Temporal lobe predilection is unique to HSV-1.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 44-year-old male with known HIV infection presents with a 3-month history of progressive cognitive decline, slowed thinking, difficulty with memory, and personality change. His partner reports he is no longer able to manage his finances or drive safely.
prompts.reasoning: Cognitive decline in an HIV-positive patient must prompt a systematic exclusion of opportunistic CNS infections and neoplasms before diagnosing HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder (HAND). Cryptococcal meningitis, cerebral toxoplasmosis, CMV encephalitis, primary CNS lymphoma, and neurosyphilis are all important treatable causes of cognitive decline in HIV.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The decline has been gradual with no fluctuation. No headache, no fever, no focal limb weakness. He has noticed slowed movement and difficulty with his handwriting over the past 2 months. He is taking no antiretroviral therapy (ART) — was started on therapy but stopped 18 months ago due to side effects. He reports decreased libido and social withdrawal.
prompts.reasoning: Insidious progressive subcortical dementia — cognitive slowing, psychomotor retardation, personality change, and motor slowing (bradykinesia) — is the clinical hallmark of HIV-associated dementia (HAD / HIV encephalopathy). This is predominantly a subcortical syndrome (affecting basal ganglia, deep white matter), unlike cortical dementias (Alzheimer's, FTD). Absence of ART for 18 months allows viral replication and cerebral injury. Concurrent neurosyphilis must be excluded by syphilis serology.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV diagnosed 6 years ago. Nadir CD4 count 56 cells/µL. Currently not on ART (stopped 18 months ago). Sexually active with multiple partners — no condom use. Last CD4 and viral load not tested in 18 months. History of oral candidiasis and one previous episode of PCP pneumonia (AIDS-defining). No hepatitis B or C co-infection.
prompts.reasoning: Low nadir CD4 (56 cells/µL) confirms advanced AIDS. HIV encephalopathy (HIV-associated dementia) is classified as an AIDS-defining illness. The primary mechanism is direct HIV neurotoxicity via infected macrophages/microglia releasing neurotoxic cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β) and viral proteins (gp120, Tat). HIV infects brain via infected monocytes crossing the blood-brain barrier ('Trojan horse' mechanism). ART cessation directly increases risk — ART reduces HIV-associated dementia by controlling viral replication in the CNS.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.2°C, BP 118/74 mmHg. GCS 15 but cognitively slow. MMSE 19/30 (attention and recall impaired). Psychomotor slowing evident. Mild bilateral hyperreflexia. Bilateral extensor plantars. Mild ataxic gait. No focal weakness. No meningism. No papilloedema. HIV viral load (today): 142,000 copies/mL. CD4 count: 38 cells/µL.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral pyramidal signs (hyperreflexia, extensor plantars) with cognitive impairment and psychomotor retardation — without focal deficit or meningism — is the classic presentation of HIV encephalopathy. Bilateral UMN signs reflect diffuse white matter and corticospinal tract involvement. CD4 38 cells/µL confirms profound immunosuppression. HIV viral load 142,000 copies/mL confirms active replication. The absence of fever, headache, or focal signs makes acute opportunistic CNS infections less likely here.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (FLAIR): diffuse, bilateral, confluent white matter hyperintensity — predominantly in the deep and periventricular white matter, symmetric, without mass effect or contrast enhancement. Diffuse cortical and subcortical atrophy disproportionate to age. No ring-enhancing lesions. No restricted diffusion. MR spectroscopy: elevated choline and myo-inositol; reduced NAA (reflecting neuronal loss).
prompts.reasoning: Diffuse, symmetric, non-enhancing white matter FLAIR hyperintensity (HIV leukoencephalopathy) without mass effect is characteristic of HIV encephalopathy — distinguishing it from toxoplasmosis (ring-enhancing) and CNS lymphoma (periventricular enhancing lesions). MRS shows elevated choline (membrane turnover/inflammation), elevated myo-inositol (microglial activation), and reduced NAA (irreversible neuronal loss). Diffuse atrophy reflects widespread neuronal injury.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Histopathology
prompts.text: CSF analysis: mildly elevated protein (0.78 g/L), lymphocytic pleocytosis (25 cells/µL), normal glucose, negative bacterial culture. CSF HIV RNA: elevated (neurovirulent). Toxoplasma serology CSF: negative. CSF VDRL negative. EBV PCR: negative (excludes CNS lymphoma). Syphilis TPPA: negative. Histopathology (post-mortem findings in late-stage): microglial nodules, multinucleated giant cells (fused HIV-infected macrophages), diffuse white matter pallor (vacuolar myelopathy), and astrogliosis — without direct neuronal infection (HIV does not infect neurons directly).
prompts.reasoning: HIV encephalopathy is diagnosed by exclusion of treatable causes combined with positive CSF HIV RNA and imaging findings. The pathological hallmark is the multinucleated giant cell (fused HIV-infected macrophages/microglia). Vacuolar myelopathy affects the posterior and lateral spinal cord columns. HIV infects macrophages and microglia (CD4 expressed on these cells) — NOT neurons directly. Neuronal death occurs through indirect mechanisms: viral proteins (gp120, Tat), excitotoxicity, cytokines. Treatment: ART re-initiation is the definitive and most effective treatment.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 55-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of progressive gait difficulty — he is unsteady particularly in the dark. He has also noticed loss of sensation in his feet. His wife reports personality changes and memory lapses over the same period.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: Progressive sensory ataxia (worse in dark — loss of proprioceptive input) with peripheral neuropathy and cognitive change is the classic presentation of tabes dorsalis and general paresis of the insane — two manifestations of tertiary neurosyphilis. Vitamin B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) causes a similar picture and must be excluded. HIV co-infection and neurosyphilis frequently co-occur.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He reports shooting (lancinating) pains in his legs occurring unpredictably, described as electric shock-like. He has had difficulty knowing where his feet are, and tends to walk looking at the ground. He has had no bowel or bladder problems. He recalls a painless penile ulcer approximately 20 years ago that resolved without treatment. No visual symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The 'lightning pains' (tabetic crises) — sudden severe shooting pains in the legs — are pathognomonic of tabes dorsalis (posterior column disease from tertiary syphilis). The positive Romberg sign (worse in dark), sensory ataxia, and lancinating pains all localise to the posterior spinal columns and dorsal root ganglia. The history of a painless ulcer (likely a primary syphilitic chancre) 20 years ago, untreated, is the key epidemiological clue — neurosyphilis manifests 10-20 years after untreated primary infection.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Never tested for syphilis or HIV. Multiple sexual partners throughout adulthood. No prior treatment for any sexually transmitted infection. Former commercial sex worker contact. No IV drug use. Drinks 2-3 units/week. Non-smoker. No known family history of neurological disease. Not on any medications.
prompts.reasoning: Neurosyphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum CNS infection occurring in tertiary syphilis (untreated). Risk factors: male sex, HIV co-infection (accelerates progression), multiple sexual partners, untreated primary infection. General paresis (frontal lobe dementia from meningoencephalitis) and tabes dorsalis (posterior column degeneration) are the classical late manifestations. HIV testing is mandatory as co-infection dramatically accelerates neurosyphilis and requires modified treatment protocols.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. MMSE 23/30 (frontal lobe tasks impaired). Argyll Robertson pupils — small, irregular pupils that accommodate but do not react to light ('near-far dissociation'). Loss of proprioception and vibration sense in the feet bilaterally. Romberg positive. High-stepping ('stamping') gait. Knee and ankle jerks absent. Power and tone normal. No UMN signs.
prompts.reasoning: Argyll Robertson pupils (prostitute's pupils — accommodate but do not react to light) are the iconic sign of neurosyphilis, caused by damage to the pretectal area (light reflex pathway) with preservation of the Edinger-Westphal nucleus (accommodation pathway). Loss of proprioception and vibration sense with areflexia and a positive Romberg sign localise to the dorsal root ganglia and posterior columns (tabes dorsalis). The stamping gait results from loss of proprioceptive feedback. UMN signs are absent in tabes (pure LMN/sensory).

prompts.label: MRI Spine & Brain
prompts.text: MRI spine (T2): posterior column atrophy with T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal columns of the thoracic and cervical cord. MRI brain: mild frontotemporal atrophy; leptomeningeal enhancement visible on gadolinium-enhanced images. No focal lesions, no plaques. Chest X-ray: normal.
prompts.reasoning: Posterior column T2 signal change on spinal MRI in tabes dorsalis reflects demyelination and axonal loss in the gracile and cuneate fasciculi. Leptomeningeal enhancement reflects active syphilitic meningitis underlying the parenchymal degeneration. Frontotemporal atrophy reflects general paresis (meningoencephalitis of tertiary syphilis). MRI findings must be combined with serology and CSF analysis for definitive diagnosis.

prompts.label: Serology & CSF Analysis
prompts.text: Serum VDRL: reactive (titre 1:32). Serum TPPA: positive. HIV test: negative. CSF analysis: lymphocytic pleocytosis (42 cells/µL), elevated protein (0.9 g/L), positive CSF VDRL (diagnostic of neurosyphilis). Serum vitamin B12: normal (excludes SCD). Glucose normal. Histopathology of tabes dorsalis (classical findings): posterior column demyelination with axonal loss, fibrosis of dorsal roots, and degeneration of dorsal root ganglia neurons.
prompts.reasoning: CSF VDRL positivity is the diagnostic gold standard for neurosyphilis — it is highly specific (though insensitive; a positive result confirms, but a negative does not exclude). TPPA confirms syphilis exposure (remains positive for life). Pathologically: tabes dorsalis — demyelination and atrophy of the posterior columns and dorsal root ganglia (Treponema invades and destroys the dorsal root ganglia neurons); general paresis — diffuse meningoencephalitis with cortical atrophy. Treatment: high-dose IV aqueous penicillin G (18-24 MU/day × 10-14 days). Early treatment can halt progression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 36-year-old male with known HIV infection and a CD4 count of 62 cells/µL presents with a 10-day history of progressive right arm weakness and difficulty speaking. He has been increasingly drowsy.
prompts.reasoning: Focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) with progressive drowsiness in an HIV-positive patient with profound immunosuppression (CD4 62) demands urgent evaluation. In AIDS patients, focal brain lesions are caused by toxoplasmosis in approximately 50%, primary CNS lymphoma in 20-30%, and other opportunistic infections. CT/MRI ring-enhancing lesions in this context are toxoplasmosis until proven otherwise.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The arm weakness came on subacutely over 5-7 days. He has had mild headache throughout. He has been non-compliant with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole) prophylaxis for 4 months — he stopped it due to rash. He keeps two cats at home. He had a fever last week (38.8°C). No seizures.
prompts.reasoning: Non-compliance with co-trimoxazole prophylaxis (which covers both PCP and Toxoplasma) in a patient with CD4 < 100 directly predisposes to cerebral toxoplasmosis. Cat ownership is a key epidemiological risk factor — cats are the definitive host of Toxoplasma gondii, and humans acquire infection through ingestion of oocysts in cat faeces or undercooked meat. Cerebral toxoplasmosis results from reactivation of latent Toxoplasma cysts in the immunocompromised brain, not new infection.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV diagnosed 5 years ago. Current ART: suboptimal — missed doses frequently. CD4 nadir: 22 cells/µL. Previous episode of PCP pneumonia 2 years ago. Toxoplasma IgG serology (from 2 years ago): positive (prior exposure/latent infection). Cat owner (2 cats). No recent foreign travel. No prior CNS disease. No IV drug use.
prompts.reasoning: Positive Toxoplasma IgG confirms latent infection — reactivation occurs when immunity fails (CD4 < 100). Cerebral toxoplasmosis almost exclusively represents reactivation of latent tissue cysts (bradyzoites in brain) in AIDS patients, not new infection. ART non-compliance allows CD4 decline. Primary prophylaxis (co-trimoxazole) must be continued until CD4 > 200 on ART. The key clinical dilemma is distinguishing toxoplasmosis from primary CNS lymphoma — both present with ring-enhancing brain lesions.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.6°C, HR 104 bpm, GCS 12. Right hemiparesis (arm power 2/5, leg 3/5, UMN pattern). Right plantar extensor. Mild aphasia (expressive). Bilateral papilloedema Grade I. No meningism. No rash. CD4 today: 62 cells/µL. HIV viral load: 85,000 copies/mL.
prompts.reasoning: Focal UMN deficits (right hemiparesis, expressive aphasia) localise to the left hemisphere. Papilloedema indicates raised ICP from cerebral oedema around lesions. Fever is more consistent with toxoplasmosis than CNS lymphoma (lymphoma is usually afebrile). The papilloedema and focal signs mandate urgent CT head. HIV viral load confirms poor viral control, consistent with CD4 suppression.

prompts.label: CT Head with Contrast & MRI
prompts.text: CT head (with contrast): multiple bilateral ring-enhancing lesions — largest 2.8 cm in left frontal lobe, additional lesions in basal ganglia and thalamus bilaterally. Surrounding vasogenic oedema and mass effect. MRI with gadolinium confirms multiple ring-enhancing lesions with the characteristic 'target sign' (concentric rings: central enhancement, surrounding necrosis, outer rim enhancement). Located at grey-white junction and basal ganglia.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple bilateral ring-enhancing lesions at the grey-white junction and basal ganglia in an AIDS patient with CD4 < 100 is the classic CT/MRI appearance of cerebral toxoplasmosis. The 'target sign' (concentric ring pattern) on MRI is characteristic. Basal ganglia predilection distinguishes toxoplasmosis from CNS lymphoma (which is typically periventricular). Multiple lesions favour toxoplasmosis; solitary lesion favours CNS lymphoma. In practice, empirical anti-toxoplasma treatment is started first — failure to respond within 2 weeks warrants brain biopsy.

prompts.label: Serology, CSF & Empirical Treatment Response
prompts.text: Toxoplasma IgG serum: positive (titre 1:1024). CSF (LP deferred due to raised ICP — given empirical therapy first): reserved. SPECT/PET: hypometabolism in lesions (favours toxoplasmosis over lymphoma, which shows hypermetabolism). EBV DNA PCR (CNS lymphoma marker): negative. Empirical treatment started: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid. After 2 weeks: MRI repeat shows significant reduction in lesion size and oedema — confirming toxoplasmosis (diagnostic-therapeutic trial). Histopathology (if biopsy needed): bradyzoite-containing tissue cysts within areas of coagulative necrosis, surrounded by free tachyzoites and microglial reaction.
prompts.reasoning: In AIDS patients with multiple ring-enhancing lesions and positive Toxoplasma IgG, empirical treatment is first-line — brain biopsy is reserved for non-responders. Response to anti-toxoplasma therapy within 2 weeks (clinical + radiological improvement) is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Histologically: coagulative necrosis surrounded by Toxoplasma tachyzoites (acute replication) and bradyzoite-filled tissue cysts (latent). Negative EBV PCR excludes CNS lymphoma. Maintenance therapy continues indefinitely until CD4 > 200 on ART.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 68-year-old retired farmer is brought by his daughter with a 6-week history of rapidly progressive cognitive decline. He went from fully independent to requiring care for all activities of daily living in just 6 weeks.
prompts.reasoning: Rapidly progressive dementia (RPD) — defined as dementia developing over weeks to months — is a medical emergency with a broad differential. CJD must be prominently considered in rapidly progressive dementia in an older patient. Other causes include autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR, anti-LGI1), paraneoplastic, viral encephalitis, vasculitis, and metabolic causes. The speed of progression (6 weeks) is alarming.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The decline began with memory lapses and depression-like symptoms, progressing to profound confusion, visual disturbances (seeing shapes/colours that are not there), and myoclonic jerks that began 2 weeks ago. The jerks are sudden, brief, and affect both arms. He has become mute over the last week. He appears frightened and has periods of staring. His appetite is completely lost.
prompts.reasoning: Myoclonus in the context of rapidly progressive dementia is the cardinal clinical feature of sporadic CJD. Cortical myoclonus (sudden involuntary muscle jerks) reflects widespread cortical involvement. Visual hallucinations (Heidenhain variant of CJD — occipital predominance), psychiatric prodrome, and rapid cognitive decline followed by akinetic mutism are the clinical stages of sporadic CJD. The combination of RPD + myoclonus + psychiatric features is highly suspicious for prion disease.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Retired farmer — worked with cattle and sheep for 35 years. No prior neurodegenerative disease. No family history of dementia or prion disease (excluding inherited prion disease). No prior neurosurgical procedures, no corneal transplant, no dura mater graft. No history of blood transfusion in the UK (1980-1996). No growth hormone treatment. Normal screening colonoscopy 3 years ago.
prompts.reasoning: Sporadic CJD (sCJD) — the most common form (~85% of cases) — occurs without identifiable cause; the farmer occupation is not a proven risk factor for sCJD. Iatrogenic CJD risk factors include: dura mater grafts, pituitary-derived human growth hormone, and corneal transplants (historical routes). vCJD (variant CJD — linked to BSE/'mad cow disease') is more common in young people and has different clinical/pathological features (psychiatric prodrome more prominent; kuru plaques). Genetic CJD (PRNP mutation) requires family history testing.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 8 — akinetic mutism. Myoclonic jerks on minimal stimulation (touch, sound). Startle myoclonus. Bilateral extensor plantars. No focal limb weakness. Fundoscopy normal. No meningism. Cortical blindness on formal testing (Heidenhain variant features). MMSE untestable. Cerebellar ataxia on examination (ataxia of gait prior to akinesis).
prompts.reasoning: Akinetic mutism (awake but immobile and speechless — a state of profound dementia) at 6 weeks is extraordinarily rapid for any dementia except CJD. Stimulus-sensitive myoclonus is characteristic. Bilateral extensor plantars indicate diffuse cortical damage. Cortical blindness (Heidenhain variant) reflects occipital cortex destruction by spongiform degeneration. The constellation of RPD + myoclonus + akinetic mutism fulfils clinical criteria for probable sCJD.

prompts.label: MRI Brain (DWI/FLAIR)
prompts.text: MRI brain DWI: cortical ribboning — symmetric restricted diffusion ('bright' DWI) in a gyriform cortical pattern across multiple cortical regions (frontal, parietal, occipital cortex). Restricted diffusion also in the bilateral caudate nuclei and putamen ('hockey stick sign' in basal ganglia). FLAIR: corresponding hyperintensity in the same regions. No mass effect, no enhancement. EEG: periodic sharp wave complexes (PSWCs) at approximately 1 Hz — triphasic waves.
prompts.reasoning: 'Cortical ribboning' on DWI — diffuse gyriform cortical restricted diffusion — is the pathognomonic MRI finding of sporadic CJD, reflecting neuronal death from spongiform degeneration (vacuolation of neurons creates a sponge-like 'dead' cortex with restricted water diffusion). Basal ganglia signal change ('hockey stick sign') is also characteristic. EEG PSWCs (1 Hz periodic sharp wave complexes) are another diagnostic criterion. Both MRI DWI and EEG findings are incorporated into diagnostic criteria for sCJD.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis, RT-QuIC & Histopathology
prompts.text: CSF: normal cells, protein, and glucose. CSF 14-3-3 protein: positive (marker of rapid neuronal death). CSF RT-QuIC (real-time quaking-induced conversion): positive for misfolded PrPSc — highly sensitive and specific. Brain biopsy (or post-mortem): spongiform vacuolation of the grey matter neuropil, neuronal loss, astrogliosis — without inflammatory infiltrate. PrP immunohistochemistry: synaptic/diffuse PrPSc deposits in cortex. No plaque (unlike vCJD which shows 'florid kuru plaques'). Western blot of brain tissue: protease-resistant PrPSc (PrP 27-30). PRNP gene sequencing: methionine homozygosity at codon 129 (MM genotype — most susceptible to sporadic CJD).
prompts.reasoning: RT-QuIC is now the most sensitive and specific antemortem test for CJD. 14-3-3 CSF protein is a non-specific marker of rapid neuronal death. Definitive diagnosis requires pathology: spongiform vacuolation (sponge-like holes in the cortical neuropil) is the cardinal histological finding — caused by intracytoplasmic vacuoles in neuronal processes and cell bodies. PrPSc (misfolded, protease-resistant prion protein) accumulates as the infectious/pathological agent. CJD is invariably fatal — median survival 5 months from symptom onset. No treatment is available.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old male returns from a 3-month stay in rural Vietnam and presents to ED with a 4-day history of fever, agitation, and extreme anxiety. He is highly distressed and refuses fluids.
prompts.reasoning: Acute encephalitis with agitation and hydrophobia following travel to a rabies-endemic region is a potential rabies emergency. Rabies must be considered in any encephalitis following animal exposure in an endemic region — it is virtually 100% fatal once symptomatic. Other causes of encephalitis, psychiatric emergency, tetanus (without animal bite), and drug toxicity must be considered. Travel and animal exposure history is essential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His family mentions he was bitten on the right calf by a stray dog 6 weeks ago while in rural Vietnam. He cleaned the wound briefly but did not seek medical attention and did not receive rabies post-exposure prophylaxis. The current symptoms began with tingling and burning at the wound site, followed by anxiety and insomnia. Today he has become extremely agitated when offered water — attempting to swallow causes violent, painful pharyngeal spasms.
prompts.reasoning: This clinical picture is classic rabies encephalitis (furious/encephalitic form): hydrophobia (violent pharyngeal spasm on attempting to swallow water), aerophobia (spasms triggered by air currents), agitation, and preceding tingling at the wound site (prodromal neuropathic pain along the bitten limb from retrograde viral spread). The 6-week incubation period is typical (range 2 weeks to 2 years, depending on bite location — bites near the face/head have shorter incubation due to shorter neural pathway to CNS). No PEP = disease progression to fatal encephalitis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior rabies vaccination. Did not seek medical attention after dog bite. No prior neurological or psychiatric illness. Non-smoker, non-drinker. No medications. No family history of psychiatric illness. The wound had healed within days and was forgotten.
prompts.reasoning: Rabies is caused by the Rhabdovirus Rabies lyssavirus, transmitted via saliva of infected animals (dogs in developing world, bats in developed world). After a bite, the virus replicates in muscle, then binds peripheral nerve endings and travels retrograde via axonal transport up peripheral nerves to the spinal cord and brain. Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP: wound washing + immunoglobulin + vaccine) is nearly 100% effective if started within days of exposure — delayed or absent PEP in an unvaccinated patient leads to inevitable fatal encephalitis once symptoms appear.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.2°C, HR 134 bpm (tachycardia). GCS 12 — agitated and combative. Hydrophobia demonstrated — attempted cup of water causes severe throat spasms. Aerophobia — blowing air towards face triggers similar spasm. Autonomic instability: hypersalivation, diaphoresis, piloerection. Healed scar on right calf. No neck stiffness. Pupils dilated and reactive.
prompts.reasoning: Hydrophobia and aerophobia are pathognomonic of furious rabies encephalitis — caused by inflammation and spasm of the swallowing musculature and laryngeal muscles mediated by brainstem involvement. Autonomic features (hypersalivation — 'foaming at the mouth', diaphoresis, tachycardia) reflect brainstem and limbic system involvement. The hypersalivation contributes to virus transmission via biting. Paradoxically, the patient may have moments of lucidity between episodes of extreme agitation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the brainstem (midbrain and medulla), basal ganglia, hippocampus, and upper cervical spinal cord. Findings are relatively subtle despite severe clinical disease. No haemorrhage. No ring enhancement. EEG: generalised slowing.
prompts.reasoning: MRI in rabies may show only subtle changes despite severe clinical encephalitis — brainstem, basal ganglia, and hippocampal T2 signal changes reflect viral infiltration of these regions (areas with highest viral tropism). The disproportionate severity of clinical disease relative to subtle MRI findings is characteristic of rabies. The brainstem involvement explains the hydrophobia, aerophobia, and autonomic dysfunction (medullary involvement). MRI is not diagnostic — it is used to exclude other causes.

prompts.label: Diagnostic Testing & Histopathology
prompts.text: Rabies direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) on skin biopsy (nape of neck — hair follicles contain nerve fibres): positive for rabies viral antigen. Saliva rabies RT-PCR: positive. CSF: mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (28 cells/µL), elevated protein 0.78 g/L, normal glucose. Serum rabies antibody: undetectable (unvaccinated). Post-mortem histopathology (invariably fatal): Negri bodies — eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions in hippocampal pyramidal neurons and Purkinje cells of the cerebellum. Perivascular lymphocytic cuffing. Basal ganglia and brainstem necrosis.
prompts.reasoning: Negri bodies (eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions representing viral nucleocapsid aggregates) in hippocampal neurons and cerebellar Purkinje cells are the pathognomonic histological finding of rabies encephalitis — classically found at autopsy. In life, DFA on skin biopsy (cornea or nape of neck) or salivary RT-PCR provides the diagnosis. Once symptomatic rabies develops, survival is exceptionally rare (< 5 documented cases worldwide). Palliative care is the mainstay — intubation, sedation, and supportive care in a darkened, quiet room to minimise stimuli triggering spasms.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 6-year-old unvaccinated boy from a polio-endemic region is brought by his parents with a 3-day history of acute onset weakness in his left leg. He had a fever and flu-like illness 5 days prior which has now resolved.
prompts.reasoning: Acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) in a child is a notifiable condition worldwide, and poliomyelitis must be urgently excluded. AFP is defined as sudden onset flaccid (floppy, hypotonic) paralysis. Key differentials: GBS (ascending, symmetrical), polio (asymmetric, lower limb), botulism (descending), transverse myelitis, and acute flaccid myelitis (AFM — caused by enterovirus D68). Unvaccinated status in an endemic region markedly increases polio probability.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The weakness is affecting only the left leg — onset was very sudden, overnight. The right leg and arms are unaffected. He had diarrhoea and fever (38.9°C) 5-7 days ago that resolved. The left leg is now completely limp and the child refuses to bear weight. There is no sensory complaint — he reports no pain and has normal skin sensation. No back pain, no bladder or bowel involvement.
prompts.reasoning: Poliomyelitis causes acute, asymmetric, flaccid paralysis (LMN) WITHOUT sensory deficits — this is the distinguishing feature from GBS and transverse myelitis (which both cause sensory involvement). The pattern of single limb involvement is characteristic of polio. The preceding febrile diarrhoeal illness reflects the faecal-oral transmission and viraemia phase. The 'minor illness' phase (fever, GI upset) precedes the 'major illness' (paralytic polio) by days.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No polio vaccinations received — family declined immunisation on religious grounds. Born in Nigeria (a polio-endemic country). Recently immigrated to Singapore. Drinks untreated water at home. No prior neurological illness. No family history. Normal development prior to this illness. No other sick contacts. No recent antibiotic use.
prompts.reasoning: Poliovirus (types 1, 2, 3 — RNA enterovirus) is transmitted via the faecal-oral route. Unvaccinated individuals in endemic regions are at high risk. Most polio infections (>95%) are asymptomatic or cause only a minor febrile illness. Paralytic polio occurs in <1% of infections — the virus crosses the blood-brain barrier during viraemia and infects and destroys anterior horn motor neurons. Bulbar polio involves the brainstem motor nuclei and can cause respiratory paralysis — life-threatening.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.4°C (fever resolved). Left leg: flaccid paralysis — power 0/5 in left hip flexors, quadriceps, and dorsiflexors. Left knee and ankle jerks absent (areflexia). Left leg hypotonia. No sensory deficit (sensation intact bilaterally). Right leg and both arms: normal power, tone, and reflexes. No extensor plantar responses. No meningism. No autonomic dysfunction. No respiratory distress.
prompts.reasoning: Asymmetric, flaccid, areflexic monoplegia of the left leg WITHOUT sensory deficits is the classic presentation of paralytic polio (spinal polio — lumbar anterior horn). Anterior horn motor neurons are selectively destroyed — PURE MOTOR LMN deficits. The asymmetry (unlike GBS which is symmetric) is an important diagnostic clue. Normal sensation rules out dorsal horn or sensory pathway involvement. Respiratory muscles must be assessed — bulbar and cervical anterior horn involvement can cause life-threatening respiratory failure.

prompts.label: MRI Spine
prompts.text: MRI spine: T2 hyperintensity in the anterior grey matter (anterior horns) of the lumbar spinal cord at L2-L4 bilaterally, with slight left-sided predominance. No cord compression. No mass. Enhancement of anterior horn on gadolinium. Chest X-ray: normal lung fields, diaphragm intact. Nerve conduction studies: normal sensory action potentials; reduced compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitude in the left leg — consistent with anterior horn cell disease.
prompts.reasoning: MRI spinal cord: anterior horn T2 signal change (anterior horn cells — lower motor neurons of the spinal cord) is the imaging hallmark of acute polio and acute flaccid myelitis. The anterior horn involvement corresponds exactly to the motor-only deficit. NCS confirms anterior horn cell disease: normal sensory responses (dorsal root ganglia/sensory neurons unaffected) but reduced CMAP (loss of motor units). This NCS pattern (normal sensory, abnormal motor) distinguishes anterior horn cell disease from peripheral neuropathy (where both are abnormal).

prompts.label: Stool Virology & CSF
prompts.text: Stool sample (two samples 24 hours apart — WHO protocol): Poliovirus type 1 isolated on cell culture. Poliovirus sequencing: wild-type (not vaccine-derived). CSF analysis: mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (45 cells/µL), mildly elevated protein (0.55 g/L), normal glucose — consistent with viral meningitis phase. Serum poliovirus neutralising antibody: low titre (confirms lack of prior vaccination). Histopathology (if autopsy): anterior horn motor neuron loss — chromatolysis, then neuronophagia (phagocytosis of dead neurons by microglia), followed by gliosis and anterior horn atrophy.
prompts.reasoning: Stool viral isolation is the definitive diagnostic test for poliomyelitis — two stool samples are required per WHO acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) surveillance protocol. Wild-type poliovirus confirms natural infection (vaccine-derived poliovirus — VDPV — can also cause paralysis in under-immunised populations). Histopathologically: poliovirus selectively infects and destroys anterior horn motor neurons — causing chromatolysis (loss of Nissl substance), neuronophagia (microglial invasion of dying neurons), and permanent motor neuron loss. Post-polio syndrome (fatigue, new weakness 30 years later) is a recognised late sequela.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 74-year-old retired schoolteacher is brought by her son with a 2-year history of progressive memory loss. She frequently forgets recent conversations, misplaces items, and has asked the same questions repeatedly.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive memory loss in an elderly patient is most commonly Alzheimer disease, but all reversible causes must be excluded (hypothyroidism, vitamin B12 deficiency, depression/pseudodementia, normal pressure hydrocephalus). The four main irreversible dementias — Alzheimer (most common, ~60%), vascular, Lewy body, and frontotemporal — must be distinguished by clinical pattern.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The memory loss began with difficulty remembering recent events — she remembers her childhood well but cannot remember yesterday's lunch. She gets lost in familiar neighbourhoods. She has recently been unable to manage her bank accounts. Personality-wise she seems more passive and less engaged. No visual hallucinations, no falls, no fluctuation. Stepwise progression has not occurred.
prompts.reasoning: Anterograde amnesia (impaired new memory formation) with preserved remote memory is the hallmark of early Alzheimer disease — reflecting hippocampal and entorhinal cortex dysfunction (these structures first affected). Progression from episodic memory → executive dysfunction → language → visuospatial deficits → global dementia follows a cortical pattern. No fluctuation or falls argues against Lewy body dementia. No stepwise decline or focal deficits argues against vascular dementia.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Her mother had dementia in her 80s. She carries one APOE ε4 allele (tested privately). Hypertension managed with amlodipine. No prior strokes or TIAs. Well-educated (Master's degree). Non-smoker. No heavy alcohol use. No thyroid problems. No family history of early-onset Alzheimer disease or Down syndrome.
prompts.reasoning: APOE ε4 allele (chromosome 19) is the single most important genetic risk factor for late-onset sporadic Alzheimer disease — ε4 homozygotes have ~15-fold increased risk. Family history confirms additional genetic burden. Higher education (cognitive reserve) may delay symptom onset. The mutations causing early-onset familial AD (autosomal dominant): APP (chromosome 21 — explains AD in Down syndrome), PSEN1 (most common familial AD cause, chromosome 14), PSEN2. Age is the greatest risk factor overall.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Vitals normal. MMSE 19/30 — significant losses in orientation (1/5), delayed recall (0/3), and serial 7s (2/5). MoCA: 14/30. No focal neurological deficits. Normal gait, no extrapyramidal features. No visual hallucinations. Fundoscopy normal. No papilloedema. Normal deep tendon reflexes.
prompts.reasoning: MMSE 19/30 indicates moderate dementia. The pattern of cognitive deficits (episodic memory, orientation, concentration impaired) is consistent with Alzheimer disease. No focal neurological signs, no extrapyramidal features (against Parkinson's disease dementia), and no papilloedema. MoCA is more sensitive for early dementia than MMSE. The absence of gait disturbance and urinary incontinence excludes normal pressure hydrocephalus ('wet, wobbly, and wacky').

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: bilateral medial temporal lobe atrophy — hippocampal volume reduced bilaterally, with enlargement of the temporal horns of the lateral ventricles and widening of the entorhinal sulci. Mild global cortical atrophy disproportionate to age, most pronounced in the parietal and temporal lobes. No vascular lesions, no hydrocephalus, no space-occupying lesion. CT head (if available): similarly shows medial temporal atrophy.
prompts.reasoning: Medial temporal lobe atrophy (hippocampus, entorhinal cortex, parahippocampal gyrus) — visible on MRI as enlargement of the temporal horns and narrowing of the hippocampus — is the radiological hallmark of Alzheimer disease, reflecting the pathological progression (Braak staging: neurofibrillary tangles spread from entorhinal cortex → hippocampus → neocortex). Parietal and temporal cortical atrophy corresponds to language and visuospatial dysfunction. Absence of vascular lesions excludes vascular dementia.

prompts.label: CSF Biomarkers & PET Imaging
prompts.text: Bloods: TFTs normal, vitamin B12 normal, FBC normal, LFTs normal (excludes reversible causes). CSF biomarkers: low Aβ42 (amyloid deposited in brain, less in CSF), elevated total tau and phospho-tau (neuronal degeneration). Amyloid PET (florbetapir): diffuse cortical amyloid deposition confirmed — bilateral parietal, temporal, frontal cortex. FDG-PET: temporoparietal hypometabolism. Histopathology (post-mortem definitive diagnosis): neuritic (senile) plaques (extracellular amyloid β-peptide core surrounded by dystrophic neurites) + neurofibrillary tangles (intracellular hyperphosphorylated tau forming paired helical filaments) in hippocampus and cortex. Loss of cholinergic neurons in the nucleus basalis of Meynert.
prompts.reasoning: The dual pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer disease: (1) Neuritic/senile plaques — extracellular amyloid β (Aβ42) deposits (APP processed by β- and γ-secretase) surrounded by dystrophic neurites and reactive microglia/astrocytes. (2) Neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) — intracellular hyperphosphorylated tau forming paired helical filaments, spreading in a Braak stage pattern. Loss of cholinergic neurons in the nucleus basalis of Meynert is the basis for cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil, rivastigmine). CSF biomarkers (low Aβ42, high tau) reflect pathological processes years before clinical symptoms.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 67-year-old male retired engineer presents with a 2-year history of progressive tremor in his right hand. His wife notes he has become increasingly slow in his movements and his face appears 'blank'.
prompts.reasoning: A unilateral rest tremor with slowness of movement (bradykinesia) and facial hypomimia in a 67-year-old suggests parkinsonism. The most common cause is idiopathic Parkinson disease, but drug-induced parkinsonism (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), vascular parkinsonism, and atypical parkinsonian syndromes (MSA, PSP, CBD) must be considered. Essential tremor is an important differential — ET is an action/intention tremor, while PD tremor is a rest tremor.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The tremor is worst at rest and improves when he reaches for objects (rest tremor). It is pill-rolling in character. He has noticed his handwriting has become very small (micrographia). He has had REM sleep behaviour disorder — acting out dreams, shouting in his sleep — for 5 years. He has also had significant constipation and loss of smell (anosmia) for several years before the motor symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The prodromal features of Parkinson disease — REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD), anosmia, constipation — can predate motor symptoms by years. RBD (acting out dreams due to loss of muscle atonia during REM sleep) is a highly specific prodromal marker of synucleinopathies (PD, MSA, Lewy body dementia). Anosmia reflects early olfactory bulb Lewy body pathology (Braak Stage 1). Micrographia (progressively smaller handwriting) and pill-rolling rest tremor are characteristic of PD. Rest tremor distinguishing from essential tremor (action tremor).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior antipsychotic or antiemetic use (excludes drug-induced parkinsonism). No family history of Parkinson disease. No history of head trauma or encephalitis. Non-smoker (smokers have lower PD risk — nicotinic receptors may be neuroprotective). Drinks black coffee daily. No exposure to pesticides or MPTP (environmental risk factors for PD). Retired indoors job.
prompts.reasoning: Drug-induced parkinsonism (most common secondary cause) must be excluded — dopamine-blocking drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) can cause all features of parkinsonism; cessation of the offending drug is required. Genetic risk factors for PD: LRRK2 mutations (most common familial PD), PARK7/DJ-1, Parkin (young-onset), SNCA (α-synuclein gene — multiplications cause PD). The vast majority are sporadic. No prior drug exposure makes idiopathic PD most likely.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP lying 132/82, standing 118/74 (mild postural hypotension). Right hand rest tremor at 4-6 Hz (pill-rolling). Bilateral bradykinesia — reduced arm swing on left, slowing of finger taps. Cogwheel rigidity in right arm (rigidity with superimposed tremor). Hypomimia (reduced facial expression). Soft monotone voice (hypophonia). Slightly flexed posture. MMSE 28/30. No early falls, no vertical gaze palsy.
prompts.reasoning: The cardinal features of parkinsonism: tremor (rest tremor, 4-6 Hz, pill-rolling), rigidity (cogwheel rigidity — combined with tremor; lead-pipe rigidity — without tremor), bradykinesia (essential for diagnosis), and postural instability (later feature). MMSE 28 — cognition relatively preserved (early PD). Absence of early falls and vertical gaze palsy excludes progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP — early falls and downward gaze palsy are cardinal). Mild postural hypotension is common in PD (autonomic involvement).

prompts.label: DaTscan & MRI Brain
prompts.text: DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT): reduced dopamine transporter uptake in the left striatum (contralateral to right-sided symptoms) > right — the characteristic asymmetric comma-shaped to rounded pattern, confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit. MRI brain: normal (no vascular lesions, no atrophy to suggest MSA or PSP). Substantia nigra MRI (neuromelanin-sensitive): reduced signal in the right substantia nigra.
prompts.reasoning: DaTscan confirms nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration — it cannot distinguish PD from MSA or PSP (all show abnormal DaTscan) but excludes drug-induced parkinsonism and essential tremor (normal DaTscan). MRI brain is normal in PD — unlike MSA (putaminal atrophy, 'hot cross bun' sign in pons) and PSP (hummingbird sign — midbrain atrophy). Normal MRI with abnormal DaTscan in the appropriate clinical context confirms idiopathic PD. Response to levodopa is the most important clinical diagnostic criterion.

prompts.label: Levodopa Challenge & Histopathology
prompts.text: Levodopa trial (co-careldopa 25/100 mg TDS): dramatic symptomatic improvement in tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity — confirming dopamine-responsive parkinsonism. Histopathology (post-mortem, definitive): depigmentation and neuronal loss in the substantia nigra (pars compacta). Surviving neurons contain Lewy bodies — eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions with a dense core and pale halo — composed of aggregated α-synuclein. α-Synuclein immunohistochemistry: strongly positive Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites in substantia nigra and other brainstem nuclei.
prompts.reasoning: A sustained, robust response to levodopa is the hallmark of idiopathic Parkinson disease (MSA and PSP respond poorly). The pathological hallmarks: (1) Loss of melanin-pigmented dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta (macroscopic pallor of the substantia nigra). (2) Lewy bodies — eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions of aggregated, misfolded α-synuclein (encoded by SNCA). Parkinson disease is a synucleinopathy. Loss of striatal dopamine → disinhibition of the indirect pathway → increased subthalamic nucleus activity → increased inhibition of the thalamus → reduced cortical motor activation (bradykinesia, rigidity).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old male is brought by his wife with a 3-year history of involuntary dance-like movements of his arms and trunk, personality change, and increasingly impaired memory. His wife is concerned about his increasingly erratic behaviour.
prompts.reasoning: Chorea (rapid, involuntary, irregular, non-repetitive, flowing movements) in a middle-aged adult with psychiatric symptoms and dementia is strongly suggestive of Huntington disease. Wilson disease must be excluded (treatable cause of chorea + psychiatric symptoms in young adults — check copper studies). Drug-induced chorea (levodopa, antipsychotics) must be excluded. The triad of chorea + psychiatric disturbance + dementia with a positive family history is pathognomonic of HD.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The movements began insidiously — initially appearing as restlessness and clumsiness, now clearly choreiform. He has become impulsive, irritable, and has had two episodes of explosive anger. He recently lost his job due to erratic behaviour. His wife mentions his father died in a nursing home in his 50s with 'a movement disorder and dementia' — her mother-in-law said the diagnosis was never clearly explained.
prompts.reasoning: The family history (father with movement disorder and dementia dying in 50s) strongly suggests autosomal dominant inheritance — Huntington disease follows an autosomal dominant pattern with full penetrance. HD is caused by a CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene (chromosome 4) encoding huntingtin — >36 repeats is pathogenic. Psychiatric features (depression, personality change, impulsivity, psychosis) often precede or accompany the motor symptoms. Anticipation — earlier and more severe disease in successive generations — occurs in paternal transmission.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No medications, no antipsychotic use. No history of rheumatic fever (Sydenham's chorea). No liver disease or family history of liver disease. No prior psychiatric diagnosis. No Wilson disease screening performed. Not a heavy drinker. Non-smoker. Two children (aged 10 and 14) — concerned about their risk. No other family members known to be affected, though a paternal uncle reportedly had 'mental health problems' in his 40s.
prompts.reasoning: Autosomal dominant inheritance: each child has a 50% risk of inheriting the expanded CAG repeat. Genetic counselling and predictive testing (with pre-test counselling and psychological support) must be offered to at-risk family members. Pre-symptomatic genetic testing of minors is generally not offered. Wilson disease (autosomal recessive, liver + neuropsychiatric) must be excluded — kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp exam, ceruloplasmin, 24hr urinary copper. No antipsychotic use excludes drug-induced chorea (tardive dyskinesia).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: General: involuntary choreiform movements of face, trunk, and limbs. Oculomotor: impaired saccades (slowed voluntary saccades — characteristic of HD). Motor: choreiform movements are prominent; dystonia in hands. Tone reduced. Deep tendon reflexes brisk. Upgoing plantars absent (UMN features variable). MMSE 21/30. No Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp examination. No hepatosplenomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: Impaired saccadic eye movements (slowed initiation of voluntary saccades — patients need to blink or use head thrusts to initiate saccades) are an early, characteristic feature of HD — reflecting striatal and brainstem dysfunction. Choreiform movements (involuntary, flowing, irregular — 'dance-like'), reduced tone, and brisk reflexes are typical. Cognitive decline (frontal-subcortical pattern — executive dysfunction > memory) accompanies motor features. Absent Kayser-Fleischer rings effectively excludes Wilson disease.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: bilateral caudate nucleus atrophy — loss of the normal convex inner border of the caudate ('boxcar' or 'butterfly' appearance of lateral ventricles — enlarged frontal horns with flattened caudate head). Putaminal atrophy also present. Generalised cortical atrophy disproportionate to age. FDG-PET (if available): hypometabolism in the caudate and putamen bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: Caudate atrophy is the MRI hallmark of HD — the caudate head normally bulges into the lateral ventricle (convex inner border); in HD, caudate atrophy produces the 'boxcar' appearance (squared-off, parallel lateral ventricles with concave inner border). This corresponds to selective loss of GABA-ergic medium spiny neurons in the striatum (caudate and putamen). FDG-PET shows caudate/putaminal hypometabolism even before structural changes on MRI. Caudate atrophy distinguishes HD from other choreiform disorders.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Histopathology
prompts.text: Genetic testing (HTT gene CAG repeat): 47 CAG repeats (pathogenic — >36 repeats; full penetrance >39 repeats). Father's sample (retrieved): estimated 44 repeats — anticipation confirmed (father had fewer repeats but presented at same age). Wilson disease screen: ceruloplasmin and 24hr urinary copper normal. Histopathology: selective loss of GABA-ergic medium spiny neurons in the caudate and putamen; astrogliosis; frontal cortical atrophy. Mutant huntingtin forms intranuclear and perinuclear aggregates (neuronal intranuclear inclusions — NII) — ubiquitin and huntingtin positive on IHC.
prompts.reasoning: HD is caused by CAG repeat expansion in HTT (chromosome 4p16.3) — >36 repeats is pathogenic, >39 is fully penetrant. Anticipation occurs most commonly with paternal transmission (instability of repeat during spermatogenesis). The mutant huntingtin protein (with expanded polyglutamine tract) forms cytotoxic intranuclear inclusions in neurons. Selective loss of GABA-ergic medium spiny neurons in the striatum (which project via the indirect pathway to inhibit movement) causes the loss of motor inhibition — resulting in chorea. HD is universally fatal — no disease-modifying therapy exists. Symptomatic treatment: tetrabenazine for chorea.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male solicitor presents with a 9-month history of progressive weakness starting in his right hand — he can no longer button shirts or write clearly. He has also noticed muscle twitching in his arms.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive asymmetric hand weakness with fasciculations (muscle twitching) requires urgent evaluation for motor neuron disease. MND/ALS is characterised by combined UMN and LMN degeneration — both must be identified on examination. Cervical myelopathy can mimic MND and requires MRI exclusion. Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) is a treatable differential that also causes fasciculations and LMN weakness without sensory deficits.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Weakness has spread from the right hand to include the right arm and now affects swallowing — he has been choking on liquids. He has noticed his speech becoming dysarthric and nasal-sounding over the last 3 months. He has had no sensory symptoms at any point — no numbness, no tingling. Fasciculations are visible in his arms and thighs. No eye movement problems, no sphincter disturbance.
prompts.reasoning: Bulbar involvement (dysphagia to liquids, dysarthria) combined with limb weakness and fasciculations indicates UMN + LMN involvement across multiple CNS regions — the hallmark of ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis). Pure motor syndrome (absolutely no sensory symptoms) is the cardinal feature of MND. Eye movements and sphincters are classically spared in ALS. Spread from one region to others (limb → bulbar) over months, without sensory involvement, is diagnostic of ALS.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No family history of MND or neurological disease — sporadic ALS. No prior military service or toxin exposure. Works as a solicitor (known occupational risk group). Non-smoker. Drinks 10 units/week. No medications. No prior surgery. Retired rugby player — repeated head trauma not thought to be causative. SOD1 genetic testing not yet performed.
prompts.reasoning: ALS is mostly sporadic (~90%); 10% are familial. Familial ALS causes: SOD1 mutations (most common, chromosome 21 — superoxide dismutase), FUS, TARDBP (TDP-43), C9orf72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion (most common in familial + sporadic ALS — ~40%). TDP-43 aggregates are the pathological hallmark of most ALS cases. Occupational risks suggested but not definitively proven. Mean age of onset 55-65 years; median survival 2-5 years (worse with bulbar onset). Riluzole (glutamate antagonist) extends survival by ~3 months.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Nasal dysarthria. Tongue fasciculations. Tongue wasting. Swallowing impaired (liquid aspiration). Right arm: wasted small muscles, fasciculations, power 3/5 globally, LMN findings. Left arm: brisk reflexes despite normal power (UMN). Both legs: brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars bilaterally. No sensory deficits on full testing. No oculomotor abnormalities. No sphincter disturbance.
prompts.reasoning: Simultaneous UMN signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars — corticospinal tract) AND LMN signs (wasting, fasciculations, reduced power — anterior horn cell and corticobulbar tract) in multiple regions — limbs AND bulbar — meets El Escorial criteria for definite ALS. The fasciculations on tongue are critical LMN bulbar signs. Brisk reflexes despite wasting is a paradoxical sign that strongly suggests ALS (normally wasted muscles have depressed reflexes — preserved/brisk reflexes indicate concurrent UMN damage). Preserved eye movements and sphincters help exclude other MND variants.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Spine
prompts.text: MRI cervical spine: no cord compression, no intrinsic cord signal change — excludes cervical myelopathy as the sole cause. MRI brain: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity along the bilateral corticospinal tracts (posterior limb of internal capsule, cerebral peduncles) — the 'UMN tract sign'. Cortical thinning in the motor cortex. No mass lesions, no demyelinating plaques. EMG/NCS requested.
prompts.reasoning: MRI brain corticospinal tract T2 hyperintensity reflects UMN degeneration — the descending corticospinal tracts degenerate, increasing water content and T2 signal. This is NOT a diagnostic criterion but supports UMN involvement. MRI spine must exclude cervical spondylotic myelopathy (common mimic). Crucially, MRI is performed to EXCLUDE other diseases — ALS is a clinical diagnosis supported by electrophysiology. No treatment of an identified compressive lesion would cause ALS; this is a neurodegenerative disease.

prompts.label: EMG/NCS & Histopathology
prompts.text: EMG (electromyography): widespread acute denervation changes (fibrillations, positive sharp waves) AND chronic reinnervation changes (large polyphasic motor unit potentials) in ≥3 regions (cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral, bulbar) — meeting Awaji criteria for definite ALS. NCS: normal sensory nerve action potentials (pure motor syndrome). Anti-GM1 antibodies: negative (excludes multifocal motor neuropathy). SOD1 gene test: negative. C9orf72 repeat expansion: negative. Histopathology (post-mortem): loss of Betz cells (large motor neurons, layer V primary motor cortex) and anterior horn motor neurons. TDP-43 positive neuronal cytoplasmic inclusions (ubiquitinated) in remaining neurons — hallmark of most ALS cases.
prompts.reasoning: EMG is the diagnostic cornerstone — it confirms LMN dysfunction (denervation) in multiple regions, providing electrophysiological evidence for widespread anterior horn cell loss. Normal sensory NCS confirms pure motor involvement (distinguishing from peripheral neuropathy). TDP-43 (transactive response DNA binding protein 43 kDa) cytoplasmic inclusions are the pathological hallmark of sporadic ALS and frontotemporal dementia — TDP-43 mislocalises from nucleus to cytoplasm, forms ubiquitinated aggregates, causing nuclear TDP-43 loss of function + cytoplasmic gain of toxic function. Both corticospinal tract (UMN) and anterior horn (LMN) degeneration are pathologically confirmed.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 17-year-old male is brought by his parents with a 3-year history of progressive gait unsteadiness and frequent falls. He played football at school until 2 years ago when his coordination became too impaired.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive ataxia in a teenager demands exclusion of hereditary ataxia syndromes. Friedreich's ataxia is the most common autosomal recessive hereditary ataxia — onset typically between 5-15 years. Spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs — autosomal dominant) typically present later. Multiple sclerosis can cause ataxia in young people. Ataxia-telangiectasia, vitamin E deficiency, and abetalipoproteinaemia are rarer causes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The gait difficulty is progressive and he now requires a walking aid. He has also noticed weakness in his legs. He has chest pain on exertion and his parents note palpitations — a cardiologist noted 'heart thickening' on recent echocardiogram. His hands show some clumsiness. He has developed mild slurring of speech. No bladder problems. Vision is intact.
prompts.reasoning: Friedreich's ataxia classically combines: (1) progressive cerebellar + sensory ataxia, (2) hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM — present in ~65%, the leading cause of death in FA), (3) limb weakness, and (4) dysarthria. HCM causing exertional chest pain and palpitations in a young ataxic patient is a highly specific combination pointing to Friedreich's ataxia. The cardiac involvement distinguishes FA from most other hereditary ataxias and is critical to manage.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: His parents are first cousins (consanguineous marriage). An older sister has similar symptoms, milder in degree — diagnosed as 'early-onset ataxia'. No family history of MS or autoimmune disease. He has had progressive pes cavus (high-arched feet) noted since childhood, and scoliosis requiring a back brace. No medications. Non-smoker. No recreational drugs. Normal birth history.
prompts.reasoning: Consanguinity and an affected sibling confirm autosomal recessive inheritance. Friedreich's ataxia is caused by a GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the frataxin gene (FXN) on chromosome 9 — the most common mutation (~96% homozygous GAA expansion). Frataxin is a mitochondrial protein involved in iron-sulphur cluster assembly; its deficiency causes mitochondrial iron accumulation and oxidative damage. Pes cavus (high-arched feet) and scoliosis are characteristic skeletal deformities seen in FA, reflecting posterior column degeneration.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Gait: wide-based, lurching, unsteady — falls without support. Cerebellar signs: limb ataxia (dysdiadochokinesia, dysmetria), nystagmus, scanning dysarthria. Sensory: absent vibration and proprioception in feet and knees bilaterally. Romberg positive. Power: mild proximal leg weakness. Reflexes: absent knee and ankle jerks (LMN) — but plantars UPGOING (UMN). Pes cavus. Scoliosis. No telangiectasia. ECG: LVH and T-wave inversion.
prompts.reasoning: The paradoxical combination of absent reflexes (LMN — dorsal root ganglion degeneration) with upgoing plantar responses (UMN — corticospinal tract degeneration) alongside dorsal column sensory loss (vibration, proprioception) is pathognomonic of Friedreich's ataxia. It simultaneously affects: posterior columns, spinocerebellar tracts, corticospinal tracts, and dorsal root ganglia — explaining the mixed picture. Pes cavus and scoliosis reflect chronic proprioceptive loss and muscle imbalance.

prompts.label: MRI Spine & Brain
prompts.text: MRI cervical and thoracic spine: significant atrophy of the spinal cord, predominantly in the posterior and lateral columns. T2 signal change in the posterior columns. MRI brain: mild cerebellar hemisphere and vermis atrophy; superior cerebellar peduncle atrophy. No demyelinating plaques. Echocardiogram: concentric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy — LV wall thickness 17 mm (severely hypertrophied). ECG: LVH pattern, non-specific ST changes.
prompts.reasoning: Spinal cord atrophy with posterior and lateral column T2 signal change corresponds to the pathological degeneration of the posterior columns (sensory ataxia, vibration/proprioception loss) and spinocerebellar and corticospinal tracts (cerebellar ataxia, weakness, UMN signs). Superior cerebellar peduncle atrophy reflects dentato-rubro-thalamic pathway degeneration. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy on echo reflects mitochondrial dysfunction from frataxin deficiency — cardiac iron accumulation → oxidative stress → hypertrophy and fibrosis.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Histopathology
prompts.text: FXN gene GAA repeat expansion: homozygous expanded alleles (allele 1: 750 GAA repeats; allele 2: 820 GAA repeats — both pathogenic, normal <36 repeats). Vitamin E level: normal (excludes AVED — ataxia with vitamin E deficiency). Alpha-fetoprotein: normal (excludes ataxia-telangiectasia — elevated AFP in AT). Histopathology (post-mortem in advanced cases): degeneration of: (1) posterior columns (gracile and cuneate fasciculi), (2) spinocerebellar tracts (Clarkes column), (3) corticospinal tracts, (4) dorsal root ganglia neurons — loss of large myelinated sensory neurons. Dentate nucleus neuronal loss. Frataxin protein absent in mitochondria of neurons and cardiomyocytes.
prompts.reasoning: Homozygous GAA repeat expansion (>66 repeats pathogenic) in FXN gene is diagnostic — PCR-based repeat length analysis is the standard genetic test. GAA expansion in intron 1 causes chromatin compaction → reduced frataxin transcription. Frataxin deficiency → mitochondrial iron accumulation → free radical production → neuronal and cardiomyocyte death. Pathological hallmarks: posterior column + spinocerebellar tract + corticospinal tract + dorsal root ganglion degeneration (explaining the unique mixed UMN/LMN/sensory clinical picture). No disease-modifying treatment; cardiac management and physiotherapy are key. Omaveloxolone (Nrf2 activator) recently FDA-approved.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old female presents with a 2-week history of painful vision loss in her right eye. She describes the pain as worsening with eye movement. Her vision has been blurry with a central scotoma.
prompts.reasoning: Painful monocular vision loss with a central scotoma in a young woman is optic neuritis until proven otherwise — it is the presenting feature of MS in 20-25% of cases. NMO (NMOSD — anti-AQP4 antibody disease) causes more severe optic neuritis. Ischaemic optic neuropathy (anterior ION) occurs in older patients and is painless. Orbital pathology and retinal disease must be excluded by ophthalmological assessment.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The vision loss came on over 2 days and is worst centrally — difficulty reading and seeing colours (particularly red desaturation). The pain is worse with eye movement. She recalls an episode 18 months ago of right arm tingling and weakness lasting 4 weeks then fully resolving, for which she sought no medical attention. She lives in northern Europe. Symptoms worsen in the heat (Uhthoff phenomenon).
prompts.reasoning: The 'prior episode' — arm tingling and weakness 18 months ago that resolved fully — represents a previous demyelinating relapse. Combined with optic neuritis now, this fulfils the criterion of 'dissemination in time' (two episodes) AND will likely demonstrate 'dissemination in space' (different CNS locations: optic nerve now, spinal cord then) on MRI — meeting McDonald criteria for MS. Uhthoff phenomenon (worsening with heat) is pathognomonic of demyelination. Northern European origin correlates with higher MS prevalence.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior eye problems. No systemic illness. Family history: maternal aunt diagnosed with MS. HLA-DRB1*15:01 (tested in research context). Non-smoker. Normal diet. Vitamin D deficiency diagnosed last year (corrected). Oral contraceptive pill. No trauma. Works as a nurse. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) serology: positive (past infection — as in essentially all MS patients).
prompts.reasoning: MS is a T-cell mediated autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS. Risk factors: female sex (F:M 3:1), HLA-DRB1*15:01 (strongest genetic risk), EBV (near-universal in MS patients — molecular mimicry or direct viral role), vitamin D deficiency, smoking, northern latitude (higher UV exposure correlates with lower MS risk). Family history increases risk ~20-40 fold. MS is NOT caused by vaccination. Relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) is the most common course — acute relapses followed by partial/full recovery.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Visual acuity (Snellen): right eye 6/36, left eye 6/6. Colour vision (Ishihara): right eye severely impaired (3/16 plates), left eye normal. Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) in right eye. Fundoscopy: right optic disc normal acutely (retrobulbar optic neuritis — disc only swells if anterior). Pain on right eye movement. Previous episode: right arm — residual brisk reflexes and equivocal right plantar. Bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia not present.
prompts.reasoning: Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD — Marcus Gunn pupil) confirms unilateral optic nerve dysfunction. Retrobulbar optic neuritis shows normal disc acutely (disc swelling = papillitis — more anterior). Residual brisk reflexes from the prior episode confirm UMN dysfunction from spinal cord demyelination — providing evidence of a prior relapse. Red desaturation (colour vision impairment) is an early and sensitive sign of optic neuritis.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Spine
prompts.text: MRI brain (FLAIR + gadolinium): multiple (>9) periventricular T2 hyperintense lesions — 'Dawson fingers' (periventricular lesions oriented perpendicularly to the ventricular surface, following medullary veins). Juxtacortical and infratentorial lesions also present. One lesion enhances with gadolinium (active/acute plaque). MRI spine: T2 hyperintense lesion in the right posterior-lateral cervical cord at C3-C4 (< 3 vertebral segments). MRI right optic nerve: increased T2 signal in the right optic nerve sheath.
prompts.reasoning: Dawson fingers — periventricular white matter lesions oriented perpendicularly to the corpus callosum (along medullary veins) — are the pathognomonic MRI appearance of MS. Lesion locations required for dissemination in space (McDonald criteria 2017): periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, or spinal cord (≥2 of these locations). Gadolinium enhancement indicates an active (acute) plaque (breakdown of blood-brain barrier). Short spinal cord lesions (<3 vertebral segments) are typical of MS; lesions >3 segments suggest NMOSD.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis, VEPs & Histopathology
prompts.text: CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis (14 cells/µL), protein 0.52 g/L, normal glucose. IgG index: elevated (>0.7). Oligoclonal bands (OCBs): 6 bands in CSF not present in serum — positive (present in >95% of MS). Visual evoked potentials (VEPs): prolonged P100 latency in the right eye (normal amplitude but slowed conduction — demyelination without axonal loss). AQP4-IgG (anti-aquaporin-4 antibody): negative (excludes NMOSD). MOG-IgG: negative. Histopathology of MS plaque: perivenous demyelination with preserved axons (early plaque), lymphocytic T-cell infiltrate, macrophages with myelin debris, reactive gliosis (chronic plaque — shadow plaque if remyelination).
prompts.reasoning: OCBs (oligoclonal bands of IgG unique to CSF — not mirrored in serum) are present in >95% of MS cases, reflecting intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis — a hallmark of CNS immune activation. AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG negative excludes NMOSD and MOG-AD. VEP prolonged latency (slowed conduction) with preserved amplitude confirms demyelination without axonal transection — demyelination slows but does not block nerve conduction (until complete). Histologically, MS plaques show perivenous demyelination with macrophage-mediated myelin destruction, T-cell infiltrate, and axonal injury in acute plaques — astrogliotic 'sclerotic' plaque in chronic lesions (hence 'multiple sclerosis' = multiple scars).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 48-year-old male is brought by his partner with acute confusion and unsteady gait. He appeared to be looking in two different directions when the partner spoke to him. He has been unable to walk unaided for the past 2 days.
prompts.reasoning: The clinical triad of confusion (encephalopathy), ophthalmoplegia (eye movement disorder), and ataxia (gait unsteadiness) is Wernicke encephalopathy until proven otherwise. Only 16% of patients present with the full classic triad — any one or two features in a patient at risk (alcoholism, malnutrition) should trigger urgent IV thiamine administration. Delay is catastrophic — Korsakoff syndrome is the irreversible consequence.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His partner reports he has been drinking heavily (1-2 bottles of wine per day) for over 5 years with very poor dietary intake — he 'lives on alcohol and doesn't eat properly'. He vomited extensively last week from a GI illness and ate nothing for 5 days. The confusion has come on gradually over 4 days. He has developed horizontal diplopia. He is apathetic and drowsy.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged alcoholism causes thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency through: (1) poor dietary intake (alcohol substitutes for food), (2) impaired intestinal absorption, (3) reduced hepatic thiamine storage. Acute precipitants — vomiting, reduced intake — deplete remaining thiamine stores. Glucose administration without thiamine (in undiagnosed Wernicke's) is dangerous: it depletes residual thiamine and can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy in at-risk patients. This patient must receive IV Pabrinex (high-dose thiamine) BEFORE any glucose.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Heavy alcohol dependence — 5 years. Malnutrition — poor diet. No known liver disease (though likely). No prior neurological illness. No prior Wernicke's encephalopathy (though may have been missed). Previous alcohol-related admissions for detox. Lives with partner. Unemployed. Has lost significant weight over 2 years. No other medications. No recreational drugs.
prompts.reasoning: Thiamine (vitamin B1) is an essential cofactor for several critical enzymes in glucose metabolism: pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase (Krebs cycle), and transketolase (pentose phosphate pathway). Deficiency impairs cellular energy production, particularly in metabolically active areas: mammillary bodies, thalamus (mediodorsal nucleus), periaqueductal grey, cranial nerve nuclei — selectively vulnerable. Other at-risk groups: hyperemesis gravidarum, bariatric surgery, prolonged IV nutrition without thiamine, cancer, HIV.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.3°C. BP 112/68 (lying), 96/58 (standing — orthostatic hypotension). GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Confused and disoriented. Bilateral nystagmus (horizontal). Bilateral lateral rectus palsy (CN VI — bilateral abducens palsy = convergent strabismus). Broad-based, ataxic gait — unable to tandem walk. Peripheral neuropathy signs: absent ankle jerks, reduced vibration in feet. Mild hepatomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: The Wernicke triad: (1) Encephalopathy (global confusion, apathy, disorientation), (2) Ophthalmoplegia (bilateral CN VI palsies causing lateral gaze limitation, nystagmus — reflects cranial nerve nuclei involvement in the pons/midbrain), (3) Ataxia (gait and limb ataxia from cerebellar and vestibular damage). Peripheral neuropathy reflects chronic thiamine deficiency affecting the PNS. Orthostatic hypotension reflects autonomic neuropathy. IV thiamine (Pabrinex 2 pairs TDS) must be administered IMMEDIATELY — this is a medical emergency.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
