prompts.text: MRI brain (T2/FLAIR): symmetric hyperintensity of the bilateral mammillary bodies, medial thalami (mediodorsal nuclei), periaqueductal grey matter, and floor of the fourth ventricle (cranial nerve nuclei region). DWI: restricted diffusion in the thalami and periaqueductal grey. Gadolinium: enhancement of mammillary bodies (active inflammation/necrosis). Mammillary bodies appear small and bright.
prompts.reasoning: The MRI findings are pathognomonic: symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the MAMMILLARY BODIES and MEDIODORSAL THALAMI is the radiological hallmark of Wernicke encephalopathy. Periaqueductal grey and cranial nerve nuclei involvement explains the ophthalmoplegia and nystagmus. Mammillary body enhancement and restricted diffusion reflect acute cytotoxic oedema from thiamine-dependent metabolic failure. Mammillary body atrophy on chronic/follow-up imaging reflects permanent haemorrhagic necrosis. These structures are exquisitely sensitive to thiamine deficiency.

prompts.label: Thiamine Levels & Histopathology
prompts.text: Serum thiamine: undetectable. Erythrocyte transketolase activity: severely reduced (confirms functional thiamine deficiency). Blood glucose: 4.1 mmol/L. Liver function: deranged (raised GGT, AST, bilirubin — alcoholic liver disease). Coagulation: mildly prolonged PT. MCV: 104 fL (macrocytosis — B12/folate deficiency and alcoholism). Histopathology: petechial haemorrhages and neuronal necrosis in the mammillary bodies and mediodorsal thalami; proliferation of small blood vessels (endothelial hyperplasia); haemosiderin-laden macrophages in chronic cases. After IV thiamine: ophthalmoplegia improves within hours; ataxia improves over days; confusion improves over days-weeks.
prompts.reasoning: Pathologically: petechial haemorrhages, neuronal necrosis, and vascular proliferation in the mammillary bodies, mediodorsal thalami, and periaqueductal grey — reflect thiamine-dependent metabolic failure leading to mitochondrial dysfunction, glutamate excitotoxicity, and lactate accumulation in these selectively vulnerable regions. Haemosiderin-laden macrophages in chronic lesions indicate prior haemorrhage. Ophthalmoplegia is the most rapidly thiamine-responsive sign. Failure to treat causes irreversible Korsakoff syndrome (mammillary body-thalamic amnesia circuit destroyed permanently).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old female with known alcohol dependence is referred from a rehabilitation unit with persistent, severe memory problems 8 months after an acute episode of confusion and ophthalmoplegia that was treated in hospital.
prompts.reasoning: Persistent severe anterograde amnesia following an acute Wernicke encephalopathy episode in an alcoholic patient is Korsakoff syndrome — the chronic amnestic phase of thiamine-related brain injury. ~80% of untreated Wernicke encephalopathy progresses to Korsakoff syndrome. The severe anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories) with confabulation and relatively preserved intelligence distinguishes Korsakoff from other dementias.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Since discharge from hospital 8 months ago, she has been unable to learn new information — she cannot remember what she had for breakfast or that she was in hospital. She fills gaps in her memory with plausible but invented stories (confabulation) — she described yesterday's lunch in vivid detail but it was entirely fabricated. She appears unaware of her memory problem (anosognosia). Her intelligence and remote memory are relatively preserved.
prompts.reasoning: Korsakoff syndrome is characterised by: (1) Severe anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories), (2) Retrograde amnesia (patchy loss of remote memories, usually graded — recent past worse than distant past), (3) Confabulation (unconscious fabrication of false memories to fill memory gaps — not deliberate lying), (4) Anosognosia (unawareness of own memory deficit), (5) Relative preservation of intelligence and long-term memory. The memory circuit (hippocampus → fornix → mammillary bodies → mammillothalamic tract → anterior thalamus → cingulate → hippocampus) is disrupted by irreversible mammillary body and thalamic damage.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: 20-year history of heavy alcohol use — 30+ units/week. Malnutrition. Treated in hospital 8 months ago for Wernicke encephalopathy — received IV thiamine for 3 days only (inadequate dose/duration). Ophthalmoplegia resolved, but amnesia persisted. Now in a specialist alcohol rehabilitation unit. No family history of dementia. No liver failure (Child-Pugh B cirrhosis — compensated). Not taking any potentially amnestic medications. Non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Korsakoff syndrome is the irreversible chronic consequence of Wernicke encephalopathy — resulting from inadequate or delayed thiamine treatment. Thiamine must be given in high doses (IV Pabrinex 2 pairs TDS for minimum 5 days) to prevent progression. The lesion responsible is predominantly the bilateral mediodorsal thalami and mammillary bodies — permanent destruction disrupts the Papez circuit (memory circuit). Korsakoff syndrome is not a consequence of alcohol toxicity per se, but of thiamine deficiency.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Fully alert and oriented to name but not to date or location. MMSE 16/30 — severely impaired delayed recall (0/3), but well-preserved language and visuospatial tasks. Confabulation evident — invents plausible recent memories when asked. No ophthalmoplegia (resolved). Mild peripheral neuropathy. Intact remote memory (childhood events recalled accurately). No frontal disinhibition. Hepatomegaly. No asterixis (excluding active hepatic encephalopathy).
prompts.reasoning: The clinical hallmarks: severe anterograde amnesia (recall 0/3), confabulation, and intact remote memory, intelligence, and language — distinguishing Korsakoff from global dementias (Alzheimer, vascular) where other cognitive domains are also severely impaired. The selective memory impairment reflects selective damage to structures critical for memory consolidation (mammillary bodies, thalamus, fornix). No active asterixis excludes current hepatic encephalopathy (which would be fluctuating and reversible).

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: bilateral mammillary body atrophy (small, shrunken mammillary bodies — best seen on T1 sagittal views). Bilateral mediodorsal thalamic signal change and mild atrophy. Generalised cerebral atrophy disproportionate to age. No active enhancement. Old haemosiderin signal in mammillary bodies on T2* (prior haemorrhagic necrosis). No hydrocephalus. Hippocampal volumes mildly reduced.
prompts.reasoning: Mammillary body atrophy (best seen on T1 sagittal MRI) is the chronic radiological hallmark of Korsakoff syndrome — representing irreversible haemorrhagic necrosis of the mammillary bodies from the acute Wernicke phase. Thalamic (mediodorsal nucleus) atrophy also reflects permanent neuronal loss. The mammillary bodies are normally prominent grape-like structures in the floor of the third ventricle; in Korsakoff, they are small and shrunken. Chronic haemosiderin (on T2*) indicates prior petechial haemorrhage from acute Wernicke injury.

prompts.label: Neuropsychological Testing & Histopathology
prompts.text: Formal neuropsychological assessment: severe anterograde amnesia (verbal and visual memory < 1st percentile), intact intelligence (IQ 96 — normal range), intact language, intact executive function, intact procedural memory (can still learn new motor skills despite being unaware of having done so). CSF: no acute infection. Histopathology (post-mortem): cystic cavities and haemosiderin-laden macrophages in the bilateral mammillary bodies and mediodorsal thalami — representing old haemorrhagic necrosis. Marked gliosis. Neuronal loss in the thalamic intralaminar and mediodorsal nuclei. Purkinje cell loss in the cerebellar vermis.
prompts.reasoning: Intact procedural memory (learns new motor skills) demonstrates that implicit memory systems (basal ganglia, cerebellum) are spared — only the explicit/declarative memory circuit (hippocampus-fornix-mammillary body-thalamus-cingulate) is destroyed. This is a 'double dissociation' between explicit memory (abolished) and implicit memory (intact). Pathologically: cystic spaces and haemosiderin in the mammillary bodies and thalami represent the burnt-out lesions of Wernicke encephalopathy. Korsakoff syndrome is largely irreversible once established — only 20% partially recover with prolonged abstinence and thiamine supplementation.

case_0049.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
case_0049.finalDiagnosis: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old female presents with a 4-month history of progressive numbness and tingling in both feet and hands, spreading proximally. She also has difficulty walking, particularly in the dark.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: Progressive symmetrical sensory disturbance with gait ataxia (worse in dark — proprioceptive loss) requires evaluation for subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (SCD). SCD involves both the posterior columns (sensory ataxia) and lateral columns (corticospinal tracts — UMN). Vitamin B12 deficiency is the most common cause. The 'combined' refers to combined posterior and lateral column degeneration — distinguishing it from isolated peripheral neuropathy.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The tingling began in the feet and spread to the hands — 'glove-and-stocking' pattern initially. She now has difficulty knowing where her feet are on the ground and cannot walk straight in the dark or with eyes closed (Romberg positive). She also reports cognitive slowing and memory lapses over 2 months. She has been a strict vegan for 7 years with no B12 supplementation. She had gastric bypass surgery 5 years ago.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: Vitamin B12 deficiency has multiple aetiologies — strict veganism (no animal-product intake) and gastric bypass surgery (reduces intrinsic factor production and terminal ileum absorptive surface) are dual risk factors in this patient. B12 is essential for myelin synthesis (methylcobalamin is required for methionine synthase — involved in myelin basic protein production). Deficiency → subacute combined degeneration of the posterior and lateral spinal cord columns. Cognitive involvement ('megaloblastic madness') also occurs.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Gastric bypass surgery 5 years ago (Roux-en-Y procedure). Strict vegan for 7 years — no animal products and no B12 supplementation. No family history of pernicious anaemia or autoimmune disease. No alcohol use. No prior neurological problems. Takes iron and vitamin D supplements but no B12. No metformin use (metformin impairs B12 absorption). Non-smoker.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency causes: neurological — subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord, peripheral neuropathy, optic neuropathy, cognitive impairment; haematological — megaloblastic macrocytic anaemia. Causes: (1) Pernicious anaemia — anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (most common in Western populations), (2) Dietary deficiency — strict veganism, (3) Malabsorption — gastric bypass, terminal ileum disease (Crohn's, resection), (4) Drug-induced — metformin, nitrous oxide, long-term PPI. Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anaemia but NOT subacute combined degeneration.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Loss of vibration sense to knee level bilaterally. Loss of proprioception in toes and fingers. Romberg test positive. Gait: ataxic, wide-based. Lower limb deep tendon reflexes: brisk bilaterally (UMN — lateral column involvement). Bilateral extensor plantars. Mild distal weakness (LMN from neuropathy). Tongue: smooth, beefy-red (glossitis). Pallor. MMSE 24/30. Mild cognitive slowing.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: The paradox of brisk reflexes (UMN — lateral column corticospinal tract degeneration) with sensory ataxia (posterior column degeneration — vibration and proprioception loss) combined with peripheral neuropathy signs confirms SCD. This is the 'combined' picture: (1) Posterior column degeneration — sensory ataxia, vibration/proprioception loss, Romberg positive. (2) Lateral column degeneration — UMN signs (brisk reflexes, extensor plantar). (3) Peripheral neuropathy — the LMN signs (distal weakness, areflexia in feet may be masked by UMN). Glossitis reflects mucosal B12 dependence.

prompts.label: MRI Spine
prompts.text: MRI cervical and thoracic spine (T2): symmetric T2 hyperintensity in the posterior columns of the cervical and upper thoracic cord — the classic 'inverted V' sign (posterior column signal change on axial sections looks like an inverted V). Lateral column changes less prominent but present. No cord compression, no mass. MRI brain: white matter T2 hyperintensity (less specific). No demyelinating plaques typical of MS.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: The 'inverted V sign' (or 'inverted U' sign) on axial T2 MRI of the spinal cord — symmetric posterior column T2 hyperintensity — is the pathognomonic MRI finding of SCD due to vitamin B12 deficiency. This reflects posterior column demyelination (dorsal funiculi — gracile and cuneate tracts). Lateral column changes represent corticospinal tract demyelination. The posterior-lateral cord signal change on sagittal T2 is highly characteristic. This MRI pattern combined with B12 deficiency confirms SCD.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Histopathology
prompts.text: Serum vitamin B12: 68 pg/mL (severely low — normal >200). Methylmalonic acid: markedly elevated (most sensitive functional marker of B12 deficiency). Homocysteine: elevated. FBC: haemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, MCV 112 fL (macrocytic anaemia), hypersegmented neutrophils on film. Folate: normal. Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies: negative (pernicious anaemia excluded in this case — dietary/surgical aetiology). Peripheral blood film: oval macrocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils (>5 lobes). Histopathology of spinal cord: vacuolar myelopathy of the posterior and lateral funiculi — foci of myelin and axon destruction with spongy vacuolation, macrophage infiltration, and astrogliosis.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: Severely low B12 with elevated methylmalonic acid (functional marker — confirming true tissue B12 deficiency, as opposed to falsely low serum B12) confirms the diagnosis. Megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils) coexists. Methylcobalamin is required as a cofactor for methionine synthase (converts homocysteine to methionine) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (converts methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA — deficiency → methylmalonic acid accumulation). Myelin synthesis requires methionine → S-adenosylmethionine. B12 deficiency → impaired myelin synthesis → vacuolar myelopathy (SCD). Treatment: IM hydroxocobalamin. Neurological recovery possible if treated early — incomplete if severe/established.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 61-year-old male with known liver cirrhosis is brought in by family with a 2-day history of increasing confusion, altered sleep pattern (sleeping during the day, awake at night), and personality change. He has become aggressive and difficult to manage.
prompts.reasoning: Acute confusional state in a patient with known liver cirrhosis should be considered hepatic encephalopathy until proven otherwise. However, this is a diagnosis of exclusion — all other causes of confusion must be actively excluded (subdural haematoma — common in cirrhotics due to coagulopathy, Wernicke's, infection including spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, GI bleeding as a precipitant). Sleep-wake reversal is a characteristic early feature of HE.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Three days ago he had an upper GI bleed — haematemesis — managed with proton pump inhibitor but not scoped yet. He has also been constipated. He has missed 2 days of lactulose. His abdomen has been more distended and he has had ankle oedema. No fever. No fall or head trauma. No new medications. He is usually on propranolol, spironolactone, and lactulose.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple precipitants of hepatic encephalopathy identified: (1) GI bleed — blood in the gut is digested and produces large amounts of ammonia (absorbed from the colon), (2) Constipation — increases intestinal ammonia production and absorption, (3) Lactulose non-compliance — lactulose acidifies the colon (NH₃ → NH₄⁺, trapped ionically) and reduces ammonia absorption. In cirrhosis, the liver cannot detoxify portal ammonia → systemic ammonia → crosses blood-brain barrier → neurotoxicity. Recognising and removing precipitants is the key to HE management.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Alcoholic liver cirrhosis — Child-Pugh Class C. Two prior episodes of hepatic encephalopathy. Known oesophageal varices (treated with banding). Splenomegaly. On transplant waiting list. Current medications: propranolol, spironolactone, lactulose, rifaximin (recently started). Ex-smoker. Stopped alcohol 2 years ago. No hepatitis B or C co-infection.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent hepatic encephalopathy in a cirrhotic patient with known varices, coagulopathy, and recent GI bleed is a classic clinical scenario. Child-Pugh C indicates severe liver dysfunction (most likely to have HE). Rifaximin (poorly absorbed antibiotic) reduces gut bacterial ammonia production — used for HE prophylaxis/prevention of recurrence. Prior episodes of HE indicate established portosystemic shunting (TIPS or natural collaterals) — blood bypasses the liver and reaches the systemic circulation directly, delivering gut-derived toxins including ammonia to the brain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.1°C, HR 92, BP 104/62 mmHg. GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Asterixis (flapping tremor) present bilaterally — inability to maintain wrist extension → episodic loss of postural tone. Jaundice. Fetor hepaticus (musty, sweet-smelling breath). Distended abdomen — shifting dullness positive (ascites). Spider naevi, palmar erythema, leuconychia. Peripheral oedema. No focal neurological deficits. No neck stiffness.
prompts.reasoning: Asterixis (hepatic flap) — sudden, brief lapses in sustained posture ('negative myoclonus') — is the pathognomonic examination finding of hepatic encephalopathy (also seen in other metabolic encephalopathies — uraemia, CO₂ retention). It reflects disrupted postural motor mechanisms from ammonia-mediated neurotoxicity. Fetor hepaticus is the distinctive breath odour in HE (dimethyl sulphide and other gut-derived mercaptans). Jaundice, ascites, spider naevi, and palmar erythema confirm underlying severe cirrhosis.

prompts.label: CT Head & Abdominal Ultrasound
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): no intracranial haemorrhage, no subdural haematoma, no cerebral oedema. Mild generalised atrophy consistent with age. CT abdomen: liver cirrhosis — shrunken, nodular liver, splenomegaly (18 cm), large volume ascites, dilated portal vein, varices. No biliary obstruction. No portal vein thrombosis. EEG: diffuse slowing with triphasic waves — consistent with metabolic encephalopathy.
prompts.reasoning: CT head excludes subdural haematoma (common in cirrhotics due to coagulopathy and portal hypertension-related falls) and other structural causes of confusion. CT abdomen confirms the clinical picture of portal hypertension (ascites, splenomegaly, varices, nodular liver). EEG shows triphasic waves — the characteristic, though non-specific, EEG pattern of metabolic encephalopathies including HE. These are not specific to HE but support the diagnosis in the appropriate context.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Histopathology
prompts.text: Serum ammonia: 142 µmol/L (elevated — normal <40). LFTs: bilirubin 98 µmol/L, ALT 62, albumin 22 g/L (severely low). INR: 2.4. FBC: thrombocytopaenia (54 × 10⁹/L — hypersplenism). Blood cultures: no growth. Paracentesis: ascites WBC 18 cells/µL (no spontaneous bacterial peritonitis). Endoscopy: bleeding oesophageal varices (banded). Histopathology of brain (post-mortem in fatal HE): Alzheimer type II astrocytes (large, pale nuclei with prominent nucleoli — swollen, ammonia-intoxicated astrocytes in the cortex and basal ganglia). Increased astrocyte glycogen. Cerebral oedema in acute liver failure.
prompts.reasoning: Elevated ammonia confirms the pathogenetic mechanism: ammonia crosses the blood-brain barrier, is metabolised by astrocytes (via glutamine synthetase — converting ammonia + glutamate → glutamine), causing astrocyte swelling (osmotic effect of intracellular glutamine accumulation). Alzheimer type II astrocytes (enlarged nuclei, pale chromatin) are the pathological hallmark of chronic hepatic encephalopathy — representing ammonia-intoxicated, metabolically stressed astrocytes. They are predominantly found in the basal ganglia, cortex, and thalamus. Treatment: lactulose ± rifaximin, correct precipitants (treat variceal bleed, constipation), protein intake optimisation, liver transplantation (definitive).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 7-month-old infant of Ashkenazi Jewish descent is brought by her parents with progressive developmental regression over the past 2 months — she has lost motor milestones she had previously achieved and is no longer rolling or sitting. She has an exaggerated startle response to loud noises.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive developmental regression (loss of previously acquired milestones — 'neurodevelopmental regression') in an infant is a neurological emergency demanding evaluation for metabolic/storage disorders. The Ashkenazi Jewish ethnic background, age of onset, and hyperekplexia (exaggerated acoustic startle) are the classic hallmarks of Tay-Sachs disease. Niemann-Pick type C, Krabbe, and other lysosomal storage diseases are important differentials.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The child was developing normally until 4-5 months of age. The parents describe progressive hypotonia, loss of social engagement, and now apparent blindness. She has been having brief myoclonic seizures. She responds abnormally to loud noises — a loud clap causes a pronounced whole-body startle and sometimes a seizure. She now has difficulty swallowing and has lost weight.
prompts.reasoning: The clinical triad of: (1) Progressive neurodegeneration beginning after 3-6 months of apparently normal development, (2) Hyperekplexia (exaggerated startle), and (3) Macula cherry-red spot (to be confirmed on fundoscopy) is pathognomonic of GM2 gangliosidosis (Tay-Sachs or Sandhoff disease). Blindness reflects retinal ganglion cell involvement. Myoclonic seizures reflect cortical neuronal disease. Progressive hypotonia reflects LMN involvement. The clinical course is one of relentless regression.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Both parents are Ashkenazi Jewish. Neither parent had prior carrier testing. One maternal uncle died in infancy with 'a neurological condition' that was never definitively diagnosed. No consanguinity. Normal pregnancy — no prenatal diagnosis performed. Birth history normal — full term, normal birth weight, normal APGAR scores. No other siblings. No family history of seizure disorders.
prompts.reasoning: Tay-Sachs disease is autosomal recessive — caused by HEXA gene mutations (chromosome 15) leading to hexosaminidase A (HexA) deficiency. HexA normally degrades GM2 ganglioside; deficiency causes progressive GM2 ganglioside accumulation in neurons. Carrier frequency in Ashkenazi Jews: 1 in 30 (much higher than general population — 1 in 300) — due to a founder effect. Carrier couples have 25% risk of an affected child. Prenatal diagnosis (amniocentesis or CVS) is available. The maternal uncle likely also had Tay-Sachs.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Weight below 3rd percentile (weight loss). Profound generalised hypotonia ('floppy infant'). No head control. Absent visual tracking — cortical blindness. Fundoscopy: bilateral cherry-red spot at the macula — a central circular bright red area surrounded by white (opaque) retina due to lipid-laden ganglion cells. Hyperekplexia — exaggerated startle to touch and sound. Myoclonic jerks observed. No hepatosplenomegaly (distinguishing from Niemann-Pick and Gaucher disease).
prompts.reasoning: The cherry-red macula spot: the macula appears bright red because the fovea is nourished by the choroidal vasculature (visible through the thin foveal region) — the surrounding retinal ganglion cells, swollen with GM2 ganglioside accumulation, appear white/opaque, creating the cherry-red contrast. This is PATHOGNOMONIC of GM2 gangliosidosis (Tay-Sachs and Sandhoff). The ABSENCE of hepatosplenomegaly differentiates Tay-Sachs from Niemann-Pick type A and Gaucher disease (which have organomegaly). Sandhoff disease is clinically identical to Tay-Sachs but HAS organomegaly.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: bilateral symmetric T2 hyperintensity in the basal ganglia (putamen and caudate) and thalami. Cerebellar atrophy. Loss of grey-white matter differentiation at the cortex. No normal white matter development for age. Progressive cerebral atrophy on serial MRI. EEG: generalised epileptiform activity with multifocal spike-wave complexes.
prompts.reasoning: MRI in Tay-Sachs: bilateral basal ganglia T2 hyperintensity reflects GM2 ganglioside accumulation in neurons — the basal ganglia and thalami are the earliest and most severely affected structures. Progressive loss of white matter signal (failure of myelination) and cerebral atrophy reflect progressive neuronal death. The MRI pattern evolves over time, correlating with the clinical regression. Basal ganglia signal change in an infant with cherry-red spot and progressive regression is essentially diagnostic.

prompts.label: Enzyme Assay & Histopathology
prompts.text: Leukocyte hexosaminidase A (HexA) enzyme assay: severely reduced (<5% of normal activity). Hexosaminidase B (HexB): normal (distinguishes Tay-Sachs from Sandhoff — in Sandhoff, BOTH HexA and HexB are deficient). HEXA gene sequencing: homozygous for the Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation (c.1274_1277dupTATC — 4 bp insertion in exon 11). Carrier testing parents: both confirmed carriers. Histopathology: ballooned neurons filled with cytoplasmic lipid-laden lysosomes ('meganeurites') — GM2 ganglioside accumulation visible as membranous cytoplasmic bodies on EM. Progressive neuronal death, reactive astrogliosis. No foam cells in liver/spleen.
prompts.reasoning: Enzyme assay (severely reduced HexA, normal HexB) is diagnostic and distinguishes Tay-Sachs from Sandhoff disease (both HexA and HexB deficient in Sandhoff — caused by HEXB gene mutation). HEXA gene sequencing confirms the Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation. Histologically: 'ballooned neurons' with cytoplasmic GM2 ganglioside in membranous cytoplasmic bodies (zebra bodies on EM) — progressive neuronal death. No effective treatment — fatal, usually by age 2-4 years. Prenatal testing and carrier screening are the only prevention strategies.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 6-month-old infant of Ashkenazi Jewish descent presents with progressive hypotonia, feeding difficulties, and failure to thrive. Her parents have noticed increasing abdominal distension and she has not been meeting developmental milestones.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive developmental regression, hypotonia, and failure to thrive in an infant with Ashkenazi Jewish background should prompt evaluation for lysosomal storage disorders. Hepatosplenomegaly (causing abdominal distension) is a key early feature that helps distinguish Niemann-Pick from Tay-Sachs (which lacks organomegaly). The broad differential includes other sphingolipidoses and lysosomal storage disorders.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She was born normally and had no problems until around 3 months. Since then, she has been progressively losing milestones — she no longer tracks objects visually and has lost her social smile. She has had recurrent respiratory infections and an episode of jaundice.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive neurodegeneration with organomegaly (hepatosplenomegaly) and pulmonary involvement (recurrent respiratory infections from sphingomyelin-laden macrophages in the lungs) is characteristic of Niemann-Pick disease type A. The presence of hepatosplenomegaly distinguishes this from Tay-Sachs. Gaucher disease also causes organomegaly but neurological involvement is primarily type II/III. Recurrent respiratory infections reflect pulmonary infiltration with foam cells.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Both parents are Ashkenazi Jewish and are first-generation cousins. A previous sibling died in infancy with 'liver disease and a neurological condition'. No prenatal diagnosis was performed. Neonatal screen was not specific for lysosomal storage disorders.
prompts.reasoning: Niemann-Pick disease types A and B are autosomal recessive, caused by mutations in the SMPD1 gene encoding acid sphingomyelinase (ASM). Consanguinity increases risk of homozygosity. Carrier frequency in Ashkenazi Jews is ~1 in 80 for type A. The previously affected sibling strongly suggests a recessively inherited metabolic disorder. Type A is the severe neuronopathic form (fatal by age 2-3), while type B is non-neuronopathic.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Weight and length below 3rd percentile. Profound hypotonia. Massive hepatosplenomegaly — liver palpable 5 cm below costal margin, spleen 6 cm. Fundoscopy: bilateral cherry-red spot at the macula. No exaggerated startle response (differentiating from Tay-Sachs). Mild jaundice. Coarse facial features. Diffuse pulmonary crackles bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of cherry-red macula, massive hepatosplenomegaly, and progressive neurodegeneration in an infant is the classic triad of Niemann-Pick disease type A. The cherry-red spot is caused by sphingomyelin-laden ganglion cells in the perifoveal retina making it appear white/opaque, contrasting with the normal fovea. Critically, unlike Tay-Sachs, Niemann-Pick has hepatosplenomegaly and lacks exaggerated startle. Foam cells (sphingomyelin-laden macrophages) infiltrate liver, spleen, bone marrow, and lungs.

prompts.label: Abdominal Ultrasound & MRI Brain
prompts.text: Abdominal USS: massive hepatomegaly (8.5 cm) and splenomegaly (7 cm) with diffusely echogenic parenchyma. No biliary obstruction. MRI brain: bilateral T2 hyperintensity in the thalami and cerebellum; progressive cerebral and cerebellar atrophy. Chest X-ray: bilateral interstitial infiltrates (foam cell infiltration).
prompts.reasoning: USS confirms massive organomegaly with echogenic parenchyma from sphingomyelin-laden foam cell infiltration. MRI brain shows neurodegeneration with thalamic signal change and progressive atrophy — reflecting neuronal sphingomyelin accumulation and neuronal death. Pulmonary infiltrates on CXR reflect alveolar foam cell accumulation, causing recurrent respiratory infections. These multi-system findings confirm a systemic storage disorder.

prompts.label: Enzyme Assay & Histopathology
prompts.text: Leukocyte acid sphingomyelinase (ASM) activity: severely reduced (<5% of normal). SMPD1 gene sequencing: homozygous for p.Arg496Leu (Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation). Bone marrow biopsy: numerous 'foam cells' — large macrophages with pale, vacuolated cytoplasm filled with sphingomyelin (positive on Filipin stain). Liver biopsy: Kupffer cells and hepatocytes distended with lipid droplets. Electron microscopy: concentric lamellar lipid inclusions ('zebra bodies') in lysosomes.
prompts.reasoning: Definitive diagnosis requires demonstration of markedly reduced ASM enzyme activity (type A/B) confirmed by SMPD1 gene mutation analysis. The pathological hallmark is the 'foam cell' (Niemann-Pick cell) — a large lipid-laden macrophage with pale vacuolated cytoplasm filled with sphingomyelin. On EM, lamellar concentric inclusions are visible in lysosomes. Type A (neuronopathic): fatal by age 3; no effective treatment. Type B (non-neuronopathic): enzyme replacement therapy (olipudase alfa) is available. Type C: NPC1/NPC2 mutations, cholesterol trafficking defect — different pathology.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A neonate is born at 38 weeks gestation with a visible sac-like lesion over the lower back noted immediately at delivery. The obstetrician notes the lesion was suspected on the 20-week anomaly scan. The infant has absent spontaneous movement of both lower limbs.
prompts.reasoning: A posterior midline sac at birth with absent lower limb movement is a neural tube defect until proven otherwise. The spectrum ranges from spina bifida occulta (asymptomatic, no sac) to meningocele (meninges only) to myelomeningocele (neural tissue in sac — most severe). The presence of lower limb paralysis indicates neural tissue involvement (myelomeningocele). Sacrococcygeal teratoma must be excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Antenatal history: maternal folic acid supplementation was not taken before or during early pregnancy. The 20-week anomaly scan showed an open spinal defect at the L3-L4 level with associated Arnold-Chiari type II malformation (small posterior fossa, cerebellar herniation). Elevated maternal serum AFP was noted at 16 weeks.
prompts.reasoning: Failure to take periconceptional folic acid (400 mcg/day) is the key modifiable risk factor for neural tube defects (NTDs). Open NTDs leak AFP into amniotic fluid, raising maternal serum AFP — a key screening marker at 15–20 weeks. Arnold-Chiari type II malformation is almost universally associated with myelomeningocele (the hindbrain herniates through the foramen magnum due to the tethering of the spinal cord). The level of the lesion (L3-L4) determines the neurological deficit.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No family history of NTDs. Mother is 19 years old, unplanned pregnancy. No pre-pregnancy folic acid supplementation. No valproate exposure. No maternal diabetes. No prior pregnancies. No consanguinity.
prompts.reasoning: Risk factors for NTDs include: folic acid deficiency, maternal valproate use (antifolate mechanism — increases NTD risk 10-fold), maternal diabetes, prior NTD pregnancy, and obesity. Valproate is a critical teratogen to remember. The recurrence risk with one affected child is approximately 3–4%. MTHFR gene variants reduce folate metabolism and are associated with increased NTD risk. Prevention: 400 mcg/day folic acid pre-conception and throughout first trimester (4 mg/day if previous NTD).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Large myelomeningocele at L3-L4: a cystic sac covered by a thin transparent membrane, containing visible neural tissue (cauda equina elements visible). Complete flaccid paralysis of both lower limbs below L3. Absent deep tendon reflexes in lower limbs. Absent anal wink reflex. Distended bladder (urinary retention). Head circumference 37 cm — within normal range. Anterior fontanelle mildly full.
prompts.reasoning: The myelomeningocele (neural tissue contained within the sac) is the most severe form of spina bifida, causing LMN deficits at and below the level of the lesion (L3-L4: loss of knee extension partially, complete loss of ankle/foot movement, bladder/bowel dysfunction). Absent anal wink indicates sacral involvement. A mildly full fontanelle suggests early hydrocephalus from associated Chiari II malformation. Bladder dysfunction (neurogenic bladder) is universal and a major cause of morbidity.

prompts.label: MRI Spine & Brain
prompts.text: MRI spine (postnatal): open neural tube defect at L3-L4 with herniation of the cauda equina into the sac. Skin covering absent (open lesion). Tethered spinal cord. MRI brain: Arnold-Chiari type II malformation — small posterior fossa, inferior herniation of the cerebellar vermis and tonsils through the foramen magnum, tectal beaking. Moderate obstructive hydrocephalus (dilated lateral and third ventricles). Partial agenesis of the corpus callosum.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is the gold standard for defining the anatomical extent. Chiari II is virtually pathognomonic of myelomeningocele — the tethering of the cord creates a caudal traction force that pulls the hindbrain downward. Hydrocephalus (present in ~80% of myelomeningocele) results from obstruction of CSF flow at the foramen magnum by the herniated cerebellum. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt is required in most cases. The tethered cord causes progressive neurological deterioration if not surgically released.

prompts.label: Urodynamics & Surgical Planning
prompts.text: Renal ultrasound: bilateral hydronephrosis (Grade II). Urodynamics: high-pressure neurogenic bladder with incomplete emptying and vesicoureteric reflux. Karyotype: 46,XY — no chromosomal abnormality. Surgical repair of myelomeningocele performed within 24 hours of birth (primary closure). VP shunt inserted on day 3 for progressive hydrocephalus. Neurophysiology: absent motor and sensory evoked potentials below L3.
prompts.reasoning: Myelomeningocele requires urgent surgical closure within 24–72 hours to reduce infection risk (bacterial meningitis from open neural tissue). VP shunting is needed for progressive hydrocephalus. Neurogenic bladder causes vesicoureteric reflux and hydronephrosis — bilateral renal damage is a leading cause of death in spina bifida. Long-term management: clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC), bowel programme, orthopaedic bracing, and educational support. Foetal surgery (in utero myelomeningocele repair) is now offered at specialised centres and improves outcomes.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 20-year-old primigravida attends her 12-week combined first-trimester screening scan. The sonographer identifies an abnormal fetal head and requests immediate review by a consultant obstetrician. Maternal serum AFP at 16 weeks is markedly elevated.
prompts.reasoning: An abnormal fetal head at the 12-week scan demands evaluation for cranial neural tube defects. Anencephaly (failure of cranial neural tube closure) causes absence of the skull vault and cerebral hemispheres — the 'frog-eye' appearance on USS. Elevated AFP is caused by leakage from open neural tissue. Encephalocele (herniation of brain through a skull defect) must be differentiated. Anencephaly is the second most common NTD after spina bifida.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The 12-week scan shows absence of the fetal cranial vault above the orbits with exposed, disorganised neural tissue ('area cerebrovasculosa'). No cerebral hemispheres are visible. The face and lower body are structurally normal. Fetal movements are present. Amniotic fluid volume is increased (polyhydramnios).
prompts.reasoning: The sonographic findings of absent cranial vault, absent cerebral hemispheres, and exposed neural tissue confirm anencephaly. Polyhydramnios occurs because the fetus lacks the cortical swallowing centres — amniotic fluid is not swallowed normally. The area cerebrovasculosa is the exposed, vascularised neuroglial tissue remnant. Anencephaly results from failure of closure of the anterior neural tube (neuropore) by gestational day 24–26. It is lethal — most die within hours to days of birth.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No folic acid supplementation taken before or during pregnancy (unplanned). No valproate or teratogen exposure. No maternal diabetes or obesity. No family history of NTDs. Mother is non-consanguineous. No prior pregnancy losses.
prompts.reasoning: Anencephaly shares the same risk factors as all NTDs: folic acid deficiency, valproate, maternal diabetes, and obesity. The recurrence risk after one affected pregnancy is ~3%. Folic acid supplementation reduces NTD risk by ~70%. Anencephaly has approximately equal sex incidence (unlike spina bifida, which is more common in females). The condition is invariably lethal — parents should be counselled about the prognosis and offered the option of termination of pregnancy.

prompts.label: Postnatal Examination
prompts.text: The infant is born at term (parents declined termination). On examination: complete absence of the skull vault above the supraorbital ridges. Prominent frog-like eyes (exophthalmos due to shallow orbits). Exposed reddish-brown vascular tissue (area cerebrovasculosa) where the cranial vault should be. No cerebral cortex palpable. Brainstem reflexes present (suck, Moro reflex). Limb movements present. No response to painful stimuli above the neck.
prompts.reasoning: Anencephalic neonates have intact brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla) explaining preserved primitive reflexes and respiratory effort. The cerebral cortex and basal ganglia are absent or rudimentary. The area cerebrovasculosa is a disorganised tangle of vascular channels, glial tissue, and choroid plexus remnants, covered by angiomatous stroma. There is no higher cortical function. Anencephalic infants are not brain dead (brainstem is present) but are incompatible with prolonged survival — comfort care is the standard approach.

prompts.label: Prenatal Ultrasound & MRI
prompts.text: 12-week USS: absent cranial vault ('acrania') — no bony echo above the orbits. Heterogeneous disorganised echogenic material visible at the base of the skull (area cerebrovasculosa). 'Mickey-mouse sign' (frog eyes). 20-week anomaly scan: confirms absent calvarium, absent cerebral hemispheres. Polyhydramnios confirmed (AFI 28 cm). Fetal MRI at 22 weeks: confirms complete absence of cerebral cortex and basal ganglia; brainstem and cerebellum partially preserved.
prompts.reasoning: Anencephaly is detectable from 10–12 weeks on USS by the absence of the bony calvarium ('acrania'). By 12 weeks, the characteristic frog-eye/Mickey-mouse appearance is diagnostic. Fetal MRI adds anatomical detail, confirming brainstem preservation. This allows parental counselling regarding the invariably lethal prognosis. The diagnosis requires specialist counselling including the option of termination, palliative care planning, and discussion of recurrence risk with periconceptional folate supplementation for future pregnancies.

prompts.label: Investigations & Pathological Examination
prompts.text: Maternal serum AFP: massively elevated (>10 MoM). Amniotic fluid AFP and acetylcholinesterase: elevated (confirmatory of open NTD). Karyotype (amniocentesis): 46,XX — no chromosomal abnormality. Post-mortem examination (infant died at 4 hours): complete absence of cerebrum and skull vault. Brainstem intact — midbrain, pons, medulla present. Partial cerebellum. Rudimentary pituitary. Spinal cord intact.
prompts.reasoning: Amniotic fluid AFP + acetylcholinesterase are highly sensitive and specific for open NTDs — acetylcholinesterase is derived from exposed neural tissue and does not normally cross into amniotic fluid. Karyotype is normal in most anencephaly (isolated NTDs are usually chromosomally normal). Post-mortem confirms the pathological anatomy. Unlike trisomy 18 or 13 (which may also have CNS abnormalities), isolated anencephaly is a structural defect without chromosomal basis. Outcome is universally lethal.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of progressive occipital headaches that worsen with coughing, straining, and Valsalva manoeuvre. She also reports new-onset difficulty swallowing and a hoarse voice over the past 6 months.
prompts.reasoning: Headache worsening with Valsalva (cough, straining, bending) is the hallmark of Chiari malformation — it reflects intermittent obstruction of CSF flow at the foramen magnum and transient increase in intracranial pressure. Posterior fossa tumour must be excluded. Bulbar symptoms (dysphagia, hoarseness) suggest lower cranial nerve compromise at the foramen magnum. Syringomyelia (which frequently accompanies Chiari I) can cause additional sensory and motor symptoms.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She describes the headache as a heavy, pressure sensation at the back of the head radiating to the neck, lasting minutes after triggering manoeuvres. She has also noticed bilateral hand numbness and clumsy fine motor tasks over the past year, and reports a cape-like distribution of pain and temperature loss in her arms and upper chest. No incontinence. No limb weakness.
prompts.reasoning: The 'cape-like' (shawl-like) distribution of dissociated sensory loss (pain and temperature loss with preserved joint position sense) in the arms and upper chest is pathognomonic of syringomyelia — a syrinx (fluid-filled cavity) in the central spinal cord damages the crossing spinothalamic fibres in the anterior commissure. Chiari I malformation is the most common cause of syringomyelia, as obstruction of CSF at the foramen magnum creates a pulsatile pressure gradient that drives fluid into the cord.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No history of birth trauma or spina bifida. No prior neurological conditions. She reports she had occasional headaches since childhood but they worsened significantly in her mid-twenties. Physiotherapy for 'neck pain' has not helped. No family history of similar symptoms. Not on any medications. Non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Chiari type I malformation (>5 mm tonsillar herniation below the foramen magnum) is the most common type in adults — it is often congenital but presents in adulthood when symptoms become prominent, sometimes triggered by events that transiently increase ICP. Unlike Chiari II (universally associated with myelomeningocele), Chiari I is usually isolated. Patients are often investigated for years for 'cervicogenic headache' before the diagnosis is made on MRI.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Cervical hyperreflexia (brisk biceps, triceps, brachioradialis reflexes). Bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation in a cape-like distribution (C4-T4 dermatomal levels). Preserved joint position sense in upper limbs. Normal limb power. Left vocal cord palsy on laryngoscopy (CN X). Nystagmus on lateral gaze (downbeat nystagmus — pathognomonic of foramen magnum pathology). Normal gait.
prompts.reasoning: Downbeat nystagmus (fast phase downward) is highly characteristic of Chiari malformation and posterior fossa lesions affecting the vestibulocerebellum and flocculus. The dissociated sensory loss (pain/temperature loss, preserved proprioception) in a cape distribution confirms syringomyelia from central cord expansion. CN X palsy reflects compression of the vagus nerve at the foramen magnum. Upper limb hyperreflexia indicates concurrent CST compression from the descending cerebellar tonsils.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Spine
prompts.text: MRI brain (sagittal): cerebellar tonsillar herniation 12 mm below the foramen magnum (normal <5 mm). Small posterior fossa. Crowded foramen magnum. No evidence of posterior fossa mass or hydrocephalus. MRI cervical spine: large syrinx from C2 to T6 — central fluid-filled cavity expanding the spinal cord. No tumour. CSF flow studies (CINE MRI): impaired CSF flow at the foramen magnum on both systolic and diastolic phases.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is the diagnostic investigation of choice. Tonsillar herniation >5 mm below the foramen magnum is the diagnostic criterion for Chiari I. The syrinx is demonstrated as a T1-hypointense (CSF-signal) central cord cavity. CINE MRI demonstrates the pathophysiological mechanism: obstructed CSF pulsatile flow creates a pressure gradient that drives CSF through the cord's perivascular (Virchow-Robin) spaces, eventually forming a syrinx. Posterior fossa decompression (suboccipital craniectomy) is the treatment of choice and can lead to syrinx resolution.

prompts.label: Further Investigations & Management
prompts.text: Formal audiometry: mild bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Laryngoscopy: left vocal cord palsy confirmed. Sleep study: central sleep apnoea (obstructive apnoea from tonsillar compression of the respiratory centres). CT head: small posterior fossa volume, normal bone structure, no bony abnormality. Genetic testing: no FGFR3 or COL1A1/2 mutations. Posterior fossa decompression surgery performed: resolution of headaches and stabilisation of syrinx on follow-up MRI at 12 months.
prompts.reasoning: Central sleep apnoea is an underrecognised complication of Chiari I — tonsillar compression of the pre-Bötzinger complex (respiratory rhythm generator in the medulla) causes central apnoeas. Treatment: posterior fossa decompression (suboccipital craniectomy ± C1 laminectomy ± duraplasty) to enlarge the posterior fossa and restore CSF flow. Syringes typically stabilise or regress after decompression. Prognosis is good with timely surgery. Chiari II is managed differently — associated with myelomeningocele repair and VP shunting.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 3-month-old infant is brought to the paediatric neurology clinic with progressive increase in head circumference, poor feeding, and developmental delay. The parents have noticed the back of the infant's head appears unusually prominent and the fontanelle is tense.
prompts.reasoning: Rapidly enlarging head circumference (macrocephaly) with a tense fontanelle indicates raised intracranial pressure, most likely from hydrocephalus. A prominent occiput points to posterior fossa pathology expanding the posterior fossa. Dandy-Walker malformation is a key posterior fossa abnormality causing obstructive hydrocephalus in infants. It must be distinguished from mega cisterna magna (larger cisterna magna without vermis hypoplasia) and arachnoid cysts.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Head circumference was normal at birth (34 cm) but has now crossed two centile lines in 3 months. The infant has not achieved expected social smile or visual tracking. The parents note upward deviation of the eyes (sunset sign). No seizures reported. Antenatal anomaly scan at 20 weeks showed an abnormality in the posterior fossa that was 'being monitored'.
prompts.reasoning: The sunset sign (downward conjugate gaze deviation with scleral show above the iris) indicates obstructive hydrocephalus with pressure on the midbrain tectum (Parinaud syndrome equivalent in infants). The antenatal detection of posterior fossa abnormality and progressive macrocephaly is the classic presentation of Dandy-Walker malformation. Developmental delay reflects the combined effects of hydrocephalus and associated brain malformations (present in ~50%).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Full-term normal delivery. No teratogen exposure. No maternal alcohol or valproate use. No family history of cerebellar malformations. Antenatal screening at 12 weeks was normal. Trisomy 18 and 13 NIPT testing was negative. Karyotype at amniocentesis: 46,XX.
prompts.reasoning: Dandy-Walker malformation has associations with chromosomal abnormalities (trisomy 18, 13, 21), Joubert syndrome, Walker-Warburg syndrome, and various genetic mutations (ZIC1, ZIC4 genes). However, it also occurs as an isolated sporadic malformation with normal karyotype. Associated CNS abnormalities (agenesis of corpus callosum, heterotopias) occur in approximately 50% of cases. Teratogen exposure (valproate, warfarin, isotretinoin) and maternal diabetes are risk factors.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Head circumference 46 cm (>97th centile). Tense, bulging anterior fontanelle. Prominent occiput. Sunset sign bilaterally. Bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy. Truncal hypotonia. Absent head control for age. Normal limb tone and reflexes. No focal limb weakness. Prominent scalp veins. No dysmorphic features.
prompts.reasoning: Macrocephaly with tense fontanelle, sunset sign, and papilloedema confirm raised ICP from obstructive hydrocephalus. Truncal hypotonia reflects the underlying cerebellar vermis hypoplasia — the cerebellar vermis is essential for truncal coordination. The prominent occiput is caused by the enlarged posterior fossa. Normal limb tone suggests the pyramidal tracts are relatively preserved. The clinical picture localises to posterior fossa + hydrocephalus, pointing strongly to Dandy-Walker malformation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: massively enlarged posterior fossa. Complete or near-complete agenesis of the cerebellar vermis. Enormous posterior fossa cyst communicating with the fourth ventricle (enlarged fourth ventricle). Cerebellar hemispheres hypoplastic and splayed apart by the cyst. Marked obstructive hydrocephalus (dilated lateral and third ventricles). Partial agenesis of corpus callosum (splenium absent). Subcortical heterotopia noted.
prompts.reasoning: The diagnostic triad of Dandy-Walker malformation on MRI: (1) Absent/hypoplastic cerebellar vermis, (2) Cystic dilatation of the fourth ventricle expanding into the posterior fossa, and (3) Enlarged posterior fossa with upward displacement of the tentorium and torcular herophili. The cyst communicates with the fourth ventricle — hence it is a true posterior fossa cyst, not a mega cisterna magna (which is posterior to a normal vermis). Associated corpus callosum agenesis and heterotopias indicate broader cortical malformation.

prompts.label: Management & Outcome
prompts.text: Neuropsychological evaluation at 12 months: global developmental delay (cognitive, motor, language). Chromosomal microarray: deletion at 3q24 (ZIC1/ZIC4 locus). EEG: no epileptiform activity. VP shunt inserted for hydrocephalus — head circumference stabilises. At 18 months: sitting with support, limited intentional reach, no words. Ophthalmology: bilateral optic atrophy from prolonged hydrocephalus. Formal diagnosis: Dandy-Walker malformation with associated 3q24 deletion.
prompts.reasoning: Treatment is VP shunting for hydrocephalus — posterior fossa cyst endoscopic fenestration is an alternative. ZIC1 and ZIC4 are transcription factors required for cerebellar vermis development — haploinsufficiency causes Dandy-Walker malformation. Prognosis is variable: hydrocephalus control is essential to prevent cognitive damage. The severity of vermis hypoplasia and associated brain malformations determines long-term developmental outcome. Complete vermis agenesis + corpus callosum agenesis = poor prognosis for neurodevelopment. ~40% have normal or near-normal cognition if hydrocephalus is controlled early.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A male neonate is born at 37 weeks with a prominent midline facial cleft, a single central incisor, and hypotelorism (closely-set eyes). The 20-week anomaly scan had shown an abnormal fetal brain, and the parents were counselled antenatally.
prompts.reasoning: Midline facial defects (single central incisor, hypotelorism, cleft lip/palate, cyclopia) are the external markers of holoprosencephaly — 'the face predicts the brain'. Holoprosencephaly results from failure of the embryonic forebrain (prosencephalon) to divide into two cerebral hemispheres. The association with trisomy 13 is strong — 80% of trisomy 13 cases have holoprosencephaly. Septo-optic dysplasia (de Morsier) is a less severe related condition.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: At birth: the infant has a single monoventricle on neonatal cranial USS. He cannot suck or swallow (absent or severely impaired). Seizures begin on day 1 — both focal and generalised tonic-clonic. Temperature regulation is poor and he develops hypoglycaemia (hypothalamic dysfunction). Karyotype from amniocentesis: 47,XY,+13 (trisomy 13).
prompts.reasoning: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) is the most common chromosomal cause of holoprosencephaly — the HPE1 locus on chromosome 13q32 (ZIC2 gene on chr 13) is a key regulator of forebrain division. Early-onset seizures reflect the severe cortical malformation. Hypothalamic dysfunction causes temperature instability and panhypopituitarism (hypoglycaemia, adrenal insufficiency). The absence of the interhemispheric fissure means there is no corpus callosum, no olfactory bulbs, and a fused thalamus.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: First-trimester combined screening showed high risk for trisomy 13 (1:10). NIPT confirmed trisomy 13. Parents declined termination for religious reasons. Mother is 38 years old (advanced maternal age increases trisomy risk). No family history of chromosomal abnormalities. No maternal diabetes or teratogen exposure. Prior amniocentesis karyotype confirmed full trisomy 13.
prompts.reasoning: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) results from meiotic non-disjunction — advanced maternal age is the major risk factor. It occurs in approximately 1 in 10,000 live births. Beyond trisomy 13, holoprosencephaly is also caused by mutations in ZIC2 (most common non-chromosomal cause), SHH, PTCH1, FGF8, and FOXH1 genes. SHH (Sonic Hedgehog) signalling is essential for midline forebrain specification — SHH mutations cause both facial midline defects and holoprosencephaly.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Hypotelorism (interpupillary distance <3rd centile). Median cleft lip and palate. Single central incisor. Microcephaly (head circumference <3rd centile). Bilateral postaxial polydactyly. Cutis aplasia on scalp. Absent olfactory sense. Optic discs hypoplastic. Tonic seizures present. Severe hypotonia. Profound encephalopathic state — no purposeful movement. Congenital heart defect (VSD audible).
prompts.reasoning: The phenotype of trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) with alobar holoprosencephaly: microcephaly, midline facial cleft, hypotelorism, polydactyly, cutis aplasia, and severe intellectual disability. The combination of absent olfactory sense (anosmia — no olfactory bulbs/tracts) + hypotelorism + single incisor = classic HPE spectrum facial phenotype. Congenital heart defects (VSD, ASD, PDA) are present in >80% of trisomy 13. Median survival is 3–14 days; <10% survive beyond one year.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: alobar holoprosencephaly — complete failure of prosencephalic division. Single monoventricle (large horseshoe-shaped). Fused thalami. No interhemispheric fissure. No corpus callosum. No olfactory bulbs or tracts. No falx cerebri. Fused basal ganglia. Large dorsal cyst. Continuous cortex with no sulcation (agyria). Normal brainstem and cerebellum.
prompts.reasoning: Three types of HPE exist on a spectrum: Alobar (most severe — complete failure of division, single monoventricle, fused thalami, no falx), Semilobar (partial posterior division, partial interhemispheric fissure), and Lobar (near-complete division, only anterior poles fused). Alobar HPE is incompatible with prolonged survival. The fused thalami, absent falx, and monoventricle are diagnostic. The brainstem and cerebellum are derived from the rhombencephalon (not prosencephalon) and are therefore unaffected.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Outcome
prompts.text: Chromosomal microarray: trisomy 13 confirmed (full, non-mosaic). FISH: extra chromosome 13 in all cells (non-disjunction origin). SHH and ZIC2 sequencing: not applicable (chromosomal cause identified). Endocrine workup: panhypopituitarism — low cortisol, low T4, hypoglycaemia. EEG: burst-suppression pattern. Infant died on day 8 from respiratory failure and cardiac complications. Post-mortem: single holosphere with no interhemispheric fissure, fused thalami, absent olfactory bulbs, absent corpus callosum.
prompts.reasoning: Alobar HPE with trisomy 13 is invariably lethal in the neonatal period. Panhypopituitarism occurs because the pituitary and hypothalamus are derived from the developing forebrain region affected by HPE. Post-mortem pathology is confirmatory. Recurrence risk for non-mosaic trisomy 13 is ~1% (standard age-related risk for next pregnancy) — preimplantation genetic testing is available. For non-chromosomal HPE (ZIC2, SHH mutations), recurrence risk is 25-50% depending on inheritance pattern.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 4-month-old infant presents with drug-resistant epilepsy — daily clusters of infantile spasms beginning at 3 months, with no response to vigabatrin or ACTH. She has profound hypotonia and has not achieved any motor milestones. Her head circumference is below the 3rd centile (microcephaly).
prompts.reasoning: Drug-resistant infantile spasms with microcephaly and profound developmental failure demand neuroimaging to evaluate for structural brain malformations. Lissencephaly (agyria-pachygyria) from failed neuronal migration causes characteristic MRI appearances and severe refractory epilepsy. Tuberous sclerosis (cortical tubers), Aicardi syndrome (corpus callosum agenesis + chorioretinal lacunae in females), and other migration disorders are important differentials.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The infant was born at term with initial apparent normality, but then failed to develop beyond a 3-month developmental level. Seizures began at 3 months — initially infantile spasms, now evolving to include tonic seizures and generalised epileptic spasms. MRI brain was performed at 2 months due to concerns about cortical malformation on neonatal cranial USS.
prompts.reasoning: Lissencephaly ('smooth brain') results from failure of neuronal migration between gestational weeks 7–20, leaving neurons in incorrect cortical layers. The cortex is thickened (4-layered instead of normal 6-layer) with absent or reduced sulcation (agyria = completely smooth, pachygyria = broad shallow gyri). Neurons fail to migrate from the germinal matrix to their correct cortical position. Refractory epilepsy begins in early infancy; cognitive development is severely impaired (equivalent to 3–5 month developmental level).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Normal pregnancy — no teratogen exposure, no infections, no alcohol. Normal birth. Parents are non-consanguineous. No family history of epilepsy or developmental delay. Antenatal MRI at 30 weeks was performed due to a concern on USS — reported as 'reduced sulcation for gestational age'. Father has subclinical pachygyria on genetic testing (LIS1 carrier).
prompts.reasoning: Lissencephaly type I (classic) is caused by mutations in LIS1 (PAFAH1B1, chromosome 17p13.3) and DCX (doublecortin, X-linked). LIS1 deletions cause Miller-Dieker syndrome (lissencephaly + dysmorphic facies) or isolated lissencephaly. DCX mutations cause lissencephaly in males and band heterotopia ('double cortex') in carrier females. The father's subclinical pachygyria as a LIS1 carrier indicates autosomal dominant inheritance with variable expressivity. Antenatal USS/MRI diagnosis is possible — smooth brain surface detected from around 24–28 weeks.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Microcephaly (OFC 34 cm — <3rd centile). Dysmorphic facies: prominent forehead, bitemporal narrowing, upturned nose, thin upper lip, small jaw (micrognathia) — consistent with Miller-Dieker syndrome. Profound hypotonia — 'floppy infant'. No head control. No social smile or visual tracking. Bilateral clenched fists. Hyperekplexia. Daily epileptic spasms observed. Absent primitive reflexes (partial — only Moro partially present).
prompts.reasoning: Miller-Dieker syndrome = classic lissencephaly + characteristic facial dysmorphism (LIS1 deletion at 17p13.3 extends to include YWHAE gene). The facial features (prominent forehead, bitemporal narrowing) reflect abnormal brain development affecting cranial growth. Profound hypotonia is due to corticospinal tract malformation. The severity of lissencephaly (posterior vs anterior predominance) depends on the gene involved: LIS1 mutations cause posterior-predominant, DCX mutations cause anterior-predominant lissencephaly.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: complete agyria (no sulci or gyri visible) — 'figure of 8' or 'hourglass' brain appearance on axial sections. Thickened cortex (8–10 mm; normal 2–4 mm). No opercula (Sylvian fissures are wide open — shallow). Absent or markedly reduced white matter. Band of heterotopic grey matter between cortex and ventricles (shallow 4-layered cortex visible on coronal sections). Enlarged lateral ventricles (colpocephaly — disproportionate posterior horn enlargement). Absent corpus callosum.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is diagnostic. The 'figure of 8' or hourglass appearance is the classic axial MRI appearance of agyria — smooth cortical surface with wide open Sylvian fissures and no opercularisation. The thickened 4-layered cortex is due to arrested migration — neurons pile up midway, forming a thick dysorganised cortex with only 4 layers instead of 6. Colpocephaly (posterior lateral ventricle enlargement) reflects lack of posterior white matter development. The MRI grading scale (Dobyns) correlates with genetic aetiology and prognosis.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Management
prompts.text: Chromosomal microarray: 17p13.3 deletion (1.2 Mb) encompassing LIS1 and YWHAE genes — confirms Miller-Dieker syndrome. LIS1 gene sequencing: heterozygous deletion confirmed. EEG: hypsarrhythmia (high-amplitude chaotic pattern) — consistent with West syndrome. EMG/NCS: normal (no PNS involvement). Antiepileptic regimen: ACTH, vigabatrin, valproate, and nitrazepam — partial seizure reduction. Nasogastric tube feeding initiated for dysphagia. Prognosis: severe intellectual disability, seizure burden through life; median survival to adolescence.
prompts.reasoning: Chromosomal microarray is the first-line genetic test for lissencephaly. FISH for 17p13.3 is also diagnostic. Hypsarrhythmia on EEG with infantile spasms = West syndrome, which in this context is caused by the cortical malformation. No effective epilepsy cure — multiple anti-seizure medications are used for seizure palliation. Miller-Dieker syndrome (17p13.3 deletion) causes the most severe form of lissencephaly with the worst prognosis. Isolated LIS1 mutations (intragenic) cause grade 2-4 lissencephaly (some pachygyria) with slightly better prognosis. No curative treatment; supportive care and seizure management are the mainstays.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 8-year-old boy is referred by his GP for evaluation of multiple cafe-au-lait macules noted since infancy. His mother has similar skin lesions and a 'lump on her arm'. The boy has been having difficulty at school and his teacher reports learning difficulties.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple café-au-lait macules (CALMs) in a child should immediately raise suspicion for NF1 — six or more CALMs >5 mm (prepubertal) or >15 mm (postpubertal) meets one of the diagnostic criteria. A positive family history (autosomal dominant) strengthens the diagnosis. CALMs alone may occur in McCune-Albright and Legius syndrome (SPRED1 mutation — café-au-lait with no neurofibromas or Lisch nodules). Learning difficulties are present in ~50% of NF1 patients.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has 10 café-au-lait macules on his trunk and limbs, all >6 mm. He has been complaining of reduced vision in his left eye for 3 months. His mother has cutaneous neurofibromas and bilateral axillary freckling. His teacher reports he struggles with reading and mathematics but is not intellectually disabled.
prompts.reasoning: Axillary/inguinal freckling (Crowe's sign) is a highly specific diagnostic criterion for NF1 — small freckles in skin fold areas from NF1 gene haploinsufficiency in melanocytes. Visual loss in a child with NF1 should prompt urgent evaluation for an optic pathway glioma (OPG) — the most common NF1-associated tumour in children, typically a pilocytic astrocytoma of the optic nerve/chiasm. OPGs occur in ~15% of NF1 patients and can cause progressive visual loss.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Born by normal delivery. Diagnosed with NF1 by clinical criteria at age 6. Mother has confirmed NF1. Father unaffected. No known cardiac defects. Blood pressure previously elevated — found to have renal artery stenosis at age 7, now on amlodipine. No prior surgery. No other family members affected (mother is a new mutation).
prompts.reasoning: NF1 (chromosome 17q11.2, NF1 gene encoding neurofibromin — a RAS-GTPase activating protein) is autosomal dominant with full penetrance but extremely variable expressivity. ~50% are new mutations (high mutation rate — one of the highest in any single-gene disorder). Renal artery stenosis from neurofibroma or vascular dysplasia is a recognised NF1 complication causing hypertension in children. NF1 is a RASopathy — the NF1/RAS pathway is relevant to cardiac defects, skin tumours, and cancer risk.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: 12 café-au-lait macules (>6 mm) on trunk and limbs. Axillary and inguinal freckling. Two soft cutaneous neurofibromas (2–3 mm) on the back — soft, 'button-hole' invagination on palpation. One firm plexiform neurofibroma on the left forearm (bag-of-worms texture). Slit-lamp examination: multiple bilateral Lisch nodules (melanocytic iris hamartomas — tan-coloured dome-shaped lesions on the iris). Left visual acuity 6/24 (reduced). Right 6/6. Left afferent pupillary defect.
prompts.reasoning: NF1 diagnostic criteria (NIH): diagnosis requires ≥2 of: (1) ≥6 CALMs (>5 mm pre-pubertal), (2) ≥2 neurofibromas or 1 plexiform neurofibroma, (3) axillary/inguinal freckling, (4) optic glioma, (5) ≥2 Lisch nodules, (6) bony dysplasia, (7) first-degree relative with NF1. This child has: CALMs + freckling + neurofibromas + Lisch nodules = 4 criteria — definitive NF1. Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas) are NF1-specific and seen on slit-lamp in >90% of adults. Left APD indicates optic nerve/chiasm involvement.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Orbits
prompts.text: MRI brain and orbits (gadolinium): left optic nerve glioma — T2-hyperintense fusiform expansion of the left optic nerve, extending to the chiasm. Mild enhancement. Multiple T2 hyperintense foci in bilateral basal ganglia, thalami, cerebellar white matter, and brainstem — 'UBOs' (unidentified bright objects/focal areas of signal intensity, FASI). No cerebral oedema. No hydrocephalus. Optic chiasm involvement: moderate.
prompts.reasoning: Optic pathway gliomas (OPGs) in NF1 are typically WHO grade I pilocytic astrocytomas — slow-growing and often indolent. MRI is the investigation of choice. 'UBOs' or FASIs are T2-hyperintense foci in the basal ganglia, thalami, and cerebellar white matter — they are myelin vacuolation areas or areas of abnormal myelination specific to NF1. They are NOT tumours and typically disappear by adulthood. They correlate with cognitive difficulties in some studies. Treatment of OPG: carboplatin/vincristine chemotherapy if progressive visual loss.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Surveillance
prompts.text: NF1 gene sequencing: heterozygous frameshift mutation c.5839insC — pathogenic (confirms NF1 diagnosis genetically). Ophthalmology: formal visual fields — left inferior visual field defect. Renal Doppler: left renal artery stenosis confirmed (fibromuscular dysplasia variant). Annual MRI surveillance plan established. School: educational psychology assessment — borderline intellectual disability, spatial processing deficit, ADHD features. Referral: annual dermatology (neurofibroma surveillance), neurology, ophthalmology.
prompts.reasoning: NF1 gene sequencing confirms the clinical diagnosis. NF1 is a tumour suppressor gene — loss of neurofibromin leads to uncontrolled RAS signalling (cell proliferation). NF1 patients have increased risk of: neurofibrosarcoma (MPNST — malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour), rhabdomyosarcoma, leukaemia, and pheochromocytoma. Plexiform neurofibromas can undergo malignant transformation to MPNST (~10% lifetime risk) — hence surveillance is crucial. MEK inhibitors (selumetinib) are approved for paediatric NF1 plexiform neurofibromas causing morbidity.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss over 18 months, accompanied by tinnitus in both ears. She also reports intermittent imbalance and dizziness. There are no café-au-lait patches and no skin nodules.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss with tinnitus in a young adult should raise suspicion for bilateral vestibular schwannomas — the hallmark of NF2. Unilateral vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) is common in sporadic cases, but bilateral occurrence in a young patient is virtually diagnostic of NF2. NF2 differs from NF1: no café-au-lait spots, no cutaneous neurofibromas — instead schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas predominate. Audiometric testing should be the first investigation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She reports she has been wearing hearing aids for 6 months. She has noticed occasional facial twitching on the left side (CN VII irritation). Her father was diagnosed with a 'brain tumour' in his 30s and became deaf. A 3 cm firm mass is noted on her left upper back (posterior subcutaneous nodule).
prompts.reasoning: The combination of bilateral hearing loss, father with brain tumour and deafness (autosomal dominant inheritance), and subcutaneous schwannoma is highly specific for NF2. NF2 (chromosome 22q12, NF2 gene — merlin/schwannomin, a tumour suppressor) causes bilateral vestibular schwannomas as its pathognomonic feature. The subcutaneous nodule is a peripheral schwannoma. Facial nerve involvement (CN VII) occurs as schwannomas arise from CN VIII and compress adjacent CN VII in the internal auditory canal.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Father died at 42 years of a 'spinal tumour with multiple brain tumours'. Maternal family unaffected. She has no children. She was previously asymptomatic. Neurological surveillance had not been offered. She takes no medications. Non-smoker, social drinker. Works as a music teacher — concerned about her hearing.
prompts.reasoning: NF2 is autosomal dominant with high penetrance — ~95% by age 60. Approximately 50% are new mutations. The father's history (multiple CNS tumours, deafness, spinal tumour) is consistent with NF2 — ependymomas of the spinal cord are a common NF2 manifestation (~50%). NF2 has a worse prognosis than NF1 due to the bilateral vestibular schwannomas causing deafness and multiple CNS tumours. Manchester/NIH diagnostic criteria include: bilateral vestibular schwannomas OR first-degree relative with NF2 + unilateral VS or two NF2-associated tumours.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (audiometry: bilateral pure-tone average 55 dB, worse on right). Bilateral positive Romberg test. Left facial nerve weakness (House-Brackmann Grade II). No café-au-lait patches. Three subcutaneous firm nodules on back and arm (schwannomas). Posterior subcapsular cataracts noted on slit-lamp (NF2 feature). Weber test: lateralises to left (worse hearing on right). Rinne: AC > BC bilaterally (sensorineural pattern).
prompts.reasoning: Posterior subcapsular cataracts are a classic ophthalmological feature of NF2, occurring in ~80% — caused by merlin dysfunction in lens epithelial cells. Subcutaneous schwannomas are peripheral nerve tumours — they are firm, Tinel-positive. NF2 diagnostic criteria are met: bilateral vestibular schwannomas confirmed on MRI (next step) + positive family history. CN VII weakness indicates schwannoma expansion compressing the facial nerve in the CPA. Sensorineural pattern on audiometry (Weber lateralises to better ear — left) confirms cochlear/VIII nerve pathology.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Spine with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain (gadolinium): bilateral enhancing masses in the internal auditory canals and cerebellopontine angles — right 2.8 cm, left 1.4 cm. Homogeneous enhancement. CPA cistern compression bilaterally. Additional findings: 3 intracranial meningiomas (parasagittal × 2, right sphenoidal wing × 1). MRI spine: multiple intradural extramedullary schwannomas (cervical, thoracic, lumbar). One cervical ependymoma at C3-C4 level (intramedullary, rod-shaped, central cord position).
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral CPA/IAC enhancing masses are pathognomonic of NF2 on MRI. The combination of bilateral vestibular schwannomas + meningiomas + spinal ependymomas is the classic multi-tumour profile of NF2. Meningiomas in NF2 are NF2 gene-driven (merlin loss) — NF2 gene is also mutated in sporadic meningiomas. Spinal ependymomas (intramedullary) are the most common spinal tumour in NF2. This pattern is distinct from NF1, which rarely has bilateral vestibular schwannomas or meningiomas.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Management
prompts.text: NF2 gene sequencing: heterozygous nonsense mutation c.1081C>T (p.Arg361*) in the NF2 gene — pathogenic. Auditory brainstem response (ABR): bilateral absent wave V. Vestibular testing: bilateral reduced caloric responses. Surgical plan: right vestibular schwannoma resection (larger, causing worse hearing loss) — auditory brainstem implant (ABI) planned. Left side: surveillance with annual MRI. Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) commenced for right VS (hearing preservation protocol). Spinal ependymoma: monitored — no cord compression currently.
prompts.reasoning: NF2 gene (merlin) is a tumour suppressor — merlin links the cell membrane to the cytoskeleton and inhibits cell proliferation. Loss of merlin leads to uncontrolled Schwann cell and meningeal cell proliferation. Management of bilateral VSs requires balancing hearing preservation (hearing on better ear must be preserved) vs. tumour growth. Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) is used for progressive NF2 VSs — it can improve hearing and reduce tumour volume. ABI (auditory brainstem implant — electrode placed in cochlear nucleus) is used when both cochleas are non-functional. Prognosis: progressive deafness is almost universal; multiple CNS tumours require surveillance.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 2-year-old girl is referred for drug-resistant epilepsy — daily tonic and myoclonic seizures beginning at 4 months. She has developmental delay and her parents have noticed hypopigmented patches on her skin since birth. Her father has a bumpy rash across his nose and cheeks.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of refractory infantile epilepsy, hypopigmented skin macules, and a father with characteristic facial rash (angiofibromas) is the classic presentation of tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC). Drug-resistant infantile spasms (West syndrome) are a frequent early presentation of TSC — cortical tubers are the epileptogenic foci. Hypomelanotic macules ('ash-leaf spots') are the earliest cutaneous sign, often visible under Wood's lamp. NF1 has different skin lesions (CALMs, neurofibromas).

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The child was diagnosed with infantile spasms (West syndrome) at 5 months — hypsarrhythmia on EEG. She has 8 hypopigmented ('ash-leaf') patches on her trunk.
