CD4+ T cells and B cells (not CD8+ as in polymyositis) target intramuscular vessels. MAC (membrane attack complex) deposition on capillaries is the earliest histological change. In contrast, polymyositis shows endomysial CD8+ T cell infiltration without perifascicular pattern. ILD on HRCT (NSIP pattern) — managed with high-dose steroids and immunosuppression. Breast cancer treatment (surgery, chemotherapy) may improve the dermatomyositis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 54-year-old man with known rheumatoid arthritis presents with a 3-week history of sudden-onset right foot drop followed 10 days later by left wrist drop. He describes these as completely separate events, each appearing acutely within hours.
prompts.reasoning: Acute, sequential, anatomically distinct peripheral nerve deficits — affecting the right peroneal nerve (foot drop) then the left radial nerve (wrist drop) — define mononeuritis multiplex (MM). Unlike symmetric polyneuropathy, MM affects named individual nerves non-contiguously. The temporal pattern (sudden onset, separate events) distinguishes it from a global peripheral neuropathy. The underlying aetiology is usually vasculitis (nerve infarction from occlusion of the vasa nervorum) — and rheumatoid arthritis is a well-recognised systemic vasculitis cause.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The right foot drop appeared acutely while walking and has not resolved. The left wrist drop appeared one morning after waking and has also persisted. Each deficit was preceded by 1–2 days of severe aching and burning pain in the affected limb. He has lost 6 kg over 3 months and reports fatigue and night sweats.
prompts.reasoning: Acute painful onset of a mononeuropathy — with pain preceding the weakness — is the hallmark of ischaemic nerve infarction (the pathological mechanism in vasculitic MM). The vasa nervorum (small nutrient vessels supplying peripheral nerves) are occluded by vasculitic inflammation, causing infarction of the nerve fascicles. Constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fatigue, night sweats) are 'B symptoms' pointing toward systemic vasculitis or an underlying malignancy driving MM.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Rheumatoid arthritis for 12 years — currently on methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine (no anti-TNF therapy yet). No diabetes. No recent infection. Non-smoker. Alcohol: 8 units/week. No family history. No prior neuropathy.
prompts.reasoning: Rheumatoid arthritis is the most common connective tissue disease causing vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex. Other causes include: ANCA-associated vasculitis (PAN, GPA, eosinophilic GPA), SLE, cryoglobulinaemia (often hepatitis C-associated), sarcoidosis, leprosy, and diabetes mellitus. In diabetic MM (diabetic amyotrophy/Bruns-Garland syndrome), the femoral nerve or lumbosacral plexus is preferentially affected. Malignancy must be excluded as a paraneoplastic cause of MM.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right foot: inability to dorsiflex or evert right foot (MRC 0/5); absent ankle jerk; reduced sensation dorsum of right foot (common peroneal nerve distribution). Left hand: wrist drop — wrist and finger extension power MRC 0/5; absent brachioradialis reflex; reduced sensation dorsum of 1st web space (radial nerve distribution). No fasciculations. All other limbs normal. Synovitic joints at bilateral MCPs (RA). Palpable purpura on both shins.
prompts.reasoning: Examination confirms two anatomically distinct peripheral nerve lesions: right common peroneal nerve (foot drop + dorsal foot sensory loss) and left radial nerve (wrist drop + 1st web space sensory loss). These are individual named peripheral nerves — not a radiculopathy or plexopathy. The pattern of isolated individual nerve deficits, each with motor and sensory loss in a named nerve distribution, is the hallmark of MM. Palpable purpura on the shins is a skin manifestation of small-vessel vasculitis — a key diagnostic clue.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: FBC: normocytic anaemia (Hb 102 g/L). CRP 84 mg/L. ESR 102 mm/hr. ANCA: positive (MPO-ANCA/p-ANCA titre 1:160). Cryoglobulins: negative. Hepatitis B/C serology: negative. RF: markedly elevated (RA-related). Nerve conduction studies: reduced CMAP amplitudes in right common peroneal and left radial nerve with normal conduction velocities — consistent with axonal mononeuropathies (nerve infarction).
prompts.reasoning: Elevated inflammatory markers with positive MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA) indicates ANCA-associated vasculitis overlapping with RA — rheumatoid vasculitis is typically ANCA-positive. NCS showing reduced CMAP amplitudes (axonal loss) with preserved conduction velocities confirms axonal mononeuropathies — the pattern of nerve infarction (ischaemic axonal death from vasa nervorum occlusion), as opposed to demyelinating neuropathy. Cryoglobulins and hepatitis serology exclude cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis from hepatitis C.

prompts.label: Nerve Biopsy & Management
prompts.text: Sural nerve biopsy: epineural vessel wall thickening, inflammatory infiltrate (predominantly CD4+ T cells and monocytes) in vessel walls, fibrinoid necrosis of arteriolar walls, axonal degeneration in nerve fascicles with wallerian degeneration — confirming vasculitic neuropathy. Treatment: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3 days, then oral prednisolone 60 mg/day. Cyclophosphamide added for ANCA-positive vasculitis. At 6 months: no new nerve lesions; partial recovery of right foot (MRC 3/5).
prompts.reasoning: Nerve biopsy is the gold standard for confirming vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex — histology shows inflammatory vessel wall destruction (fibrinoid necrosis) and resultant axonal infarction (wallerian degeneration in fascicles). The sural nerve (pure sensory, easily accessible at the ankle) is the standard biopsy site. Treatment: high-dose corticosteroids for acute vasculitic MM; cyclophosphamide for ANCA-associated or severe disease. Recovery is partial — axonal regeneration is slow (1 mm/day) and often incomplete. Ongoing management of RA with anti-TNF therapy requires careful monitoring for infections.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old male presents with excruciating right shoulder and upper arm pain that began 5 days ago, severe enough to wake him from sleep. Today he noticed he is unable to lift his right arm and the pain is beginning to subside.
prompts.reasoning: The classic biphasic presentation of neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner syndrome) is intense, acute-onset, non-positional shoulder/upper arm pain followed by rapid onset of weakness as the pain subsides. This sequence — severe pain then weakness — is characteristic. Rotator cuff tear and dislocation are possible but would not explain the subsequent flaccid weakness. Cervical radiculopathy causes radicular pain that worsens with neck movement. The spontaneous onset without trauma narrows the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The pain was severe (10/10), constant, burning and aching, localised to the right shoulder and lateral arm — unaffected by arm position. It lasted 4 days then abruptly improved. He now cannot abduct his right arm at all and cannot flex the elbow fully. He had a flu-like illness 2 weeks ago. No neck pain. No sensory symptoms in the hand.
prompts.reasoning: The trigger of a preceding viral illness (immune activation) is present in 25–50% of neuralgic amyotrophy cases — supporting an immune-mediated pathogenesis (post-infectious or idiopathic autoimmune attack on brachial plexus fascicles). Inability to abduct (deltoid — axillary nerve, C5) and reduced elbow flexion (biceps — musculocutaneous nerve, C5/C6) with pain that is non-positional (unlike cervical root compression) localises to the upper brachial plexus. Absent sensory symptoms in the hand distinguishes upper plexus involvement from lower trunk lesions.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological conditions. No recent surgery or vaccination in the past 4 weeks. No family history of recurrent brachial plexus neuropathy (which would suggest hereditary neuralgic amyotrophy — SEPT9 mutation). No diabetes. Works as a physiotherapist — the weakness is significantly impacting his work.
prompts.reasoning: Triggers for neuralgic amyotrophy include: viral illness, vaccination (particularly influenza and COVID-19 vaccines), surgery, trauma, and childbirth — all linked to immune activation. Hereditary neuralgic amyotrophy (HNA) caused by SEPT9 (Septin-9) mutation presents with recurrent bilateral episodes and may have dysmorphic features (hypotelorism, epicanthal folds). The absence of family history and this being a first episode makes sporadic neuralgic amyotrophy more likely.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right shoulder: deltoid wasting visible. Shoulder abduction: MRC 1/5 (right), 5/5 (left). Elbow flexion: MRC 3/5 (right). Elbow extension: MRC 5/5. Wrist and hand power: normal. Right biceps reflex: absent. Brachioradialis reflex: reduced. Sensation: mild reduced sensation over the right lateral arm (axillary nerve territory — regimental badge area) and lateral forearm. Passive shoulder range of motion: normal (excluding rotator cuff pathology).
prompts.reasoning: Examination confirms upper brachial plexus (C5–C6) involvement: deltoid wasting (axillary nerve), reduced shoulder abduction and elbow flexion (musculocutaneous nerve), absent biceps reflex (C5/C6 arc). Preserved wrist and hand function excludes lower plexus or cord involvement. Sensory loss over the lateral arm (axillary nerve cutaneous branch — regimental badge area) is the key sensory finding in axillary nerve involvement. Normal passive shoulder movement excludes structural joint pathology.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies & MRI
prompts.text: NCS/EMG: reduced CMAP amplitude of right axillary nerve (deltoid recording). EMG: acute denervation (fibrillations and positive sharp waves) in right deltoid, supraspinatus, and infraspinatus with normal cervical paraspinals (excluding cervical radiculopathy). Sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs): reduced amplitude lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve (musculocutaneous). MRI brachial plexus: hyperintense signal in the right upper trunk of the brachial plexus on T2 — no mass lesion, no cervical disc prolapse.
prompts.reasoning: EMG is the key investigation: denervation in multiple nerve territories (deltoid — axillary; supraspinatus/infraspinatus — suprascapular) with normal cervical paraspinals confirms brachial plexus involvement (not C5/C6 root — which would show paraspinal changes). Paraspinal muscles are supplied by the dorsal primary rami, which branch proximal to the plexus — their normality localises the lesion distal to the root. MRI plexus shows T2 hyperintensity from oedema/inflammation in the plexus fascicles — confirming neuralgic amyotrophy. Absence of mass lesion excludes malignant plexopathy.

prompts.label: Diagnosis & Management
prompts.text: Diagnosis: Parsonage-Turner syndrome (idiopathic neuralgic amyotrophy), upper brachial plexus pattern. Acute pain management: NSAIDs and short course oral prednisolone 60 mg/day × 2 weeks (reduces acute pain duration; not proven to improve motor recovery). Intensive physiotherapy commenced for shoulder stabilisation. At 3 months: deltoid power improved to MRC 3/5. At 12 months: MRC 4/5, returning to modified clinical work. Prognosis: 90% make substantial recovery by 3 years; complete recovery in 70–80%.
prompts.reasoning: Neuralgic amyotrophy is a self-limiting condition caused by immune-mediated inflammation of brachial plexus fascicles — likely a focal inflammatory mononeuritis. The triphasic course (severe pain → weakness onset as pain subsides → slow recovery over months) is characteristic. Steroids given early may reduce pain duration but do not clearly improve motor outcomes. Recovery depends on axonal regeneration (1 mm/day) — muscles close to the plexus (deltoid, biceps) recover faster than those distant (intrinsic hand muscles if lower plexus affected). Physiotherapy prevents adhesive capsulitis and maintains range of motion during recovery.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of numbness and tingling in both hands, predominantly affecting the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger. The symptoms are worst at night and often wake her from sleep.
prompts.reasoning: Nocturnal paraesthesiae in the median nerve sensory distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring finger) is the classic presenting symptom of carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) — the most common peripheral nerve entrapment neuropathy. Nocturnal worsening is characteristic because wrist flexion during sleep increases carpal tunnel pressure. Bilateral presentation is common, especially in the context of systemic predisposing conditions. Cervical radiculopathy (C6/C7) can mimic CTS but typically has neck pain and radicular radiation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She describes waking at 2–3 am with burning, tingling, and numbness in both hands — relieved by shaking or dangling her hands out of bed (the 'flick sign'). She has noticed difficulty doing up buttons (fine motor impairment). She works as a data entry clerk (repetitive keyboard use for 8+ hours/day). She is 30 weeks pregnant.
prompts.reasoning: The 'flick sign' (shaking the hand to relieve nocturnal paraesthesiae) is highly sensitive and specific for CTS. Fine motor impairment (difficulty with buttons) suggests early thenar weakness. CTS is common in pregnancy due to fluid retention causing increased carpal tunnel pressure — typically resolving postpartum. Repetitive wrist flexion-extension (keyboard use) is an occupational risk factor. The distribution (median nerve: thumb to radial ring finger) distinguishes CTS from ulnar neuropathy (little and ulnar ring finger).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Hypothyroidism (myxoedema — a cause of CTS via infiltration of the carpal tunnel with glycosaminoglycans) — on levothyroxine. Gestational diabetes this pregnancy. No rheumatoid arthritis. BMI 28. Previous pregnancy 3 years ago — had similar symptoms that resolved postpartum. No acromegaly symptoms. No amyloidosis.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple predisposing factors for CTS are present: hypothyroidism (accumulation of glycosaminoglycans in connective tissue increases tunnel volume), pregnancy (oedema and fluid retention), and repetitive hand use. Other causes: RA (synovial proliferation in the tunnel), DM, acromegaly (bony enlargement of the wrist), amyloidosis (infiltration), obesity, wrist fractures (post-traumatic). Recurrent CTS in a previous pregnancy and resolution postpartum strongly suggests the same aetiology here.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Tinel's sign: percussion over the carpal tunnel at the wrist reproduces tingling in the right median nerve distribution (positive). Phalen's test: 60 seconds of maximum wrist flexion reproduces bilateral median distribution paraesthesiae (positive bilaterally). Thenar eminence: early wasting of the right abductor pollicis brevis (APB). Thumb opposition (APB): right MRC 4/5, left MRC 5/5. Sensation: reduced light touch over right thumb and index finger. Grip strength reduced (right). Cervical spine: full range of motion, no Spurling's test positive.
prompts.reasoning: Tinel's sign (tingling with percussion over the carpal tunnel — at the flexor retinaculum) and Phalen's test (wrist flexion for 60 seconds reproduces symptoms) are the key bedside tests for CTS. Thenar wasting (specifically APB — abductor pollicis brevis, the muscle most consistently supplied by the recurrent motor branch of the median nerve) indicates significant motor involvement. Normal cervical spine examination and negative Spurling's test exclude cervical radiculopathy. Right hand more severely affected — dominant hand with heavier keyboard use.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: Nerve conduction studies: prolonged median nerve distal sensory latency (right 4.8 ms, left 4.2 ms — both >3.5 ms, normal). Median nerve SNAP amplitude: reduced bilaterally. Prolonged median nerve distal motor latency to APB (right 5.1 ms, left 4.4 ms — both >4.2 ms, normal). Conduction velocity of ulnar nerve: normal. Median-ulnar latency comparison (palmar stimulation): difference >0.3 ms — confirms CTS. Ultrasound of right wrist: cross-sectional area of median nerve at the tunnel inlet: 15 mm² (normal <10 mm²) — swollen nerve confirming entrapment.
prompts.reasoning: NCS is the diagnostic gold standard for CTS: prolonged median nerve distal sensory and motor latencies across the wrist, with reduced SNAP amplitudes. Normal ulnar nerve conduction excludes a generalised neuropathy. The median-ulnar sensory latency comparison (at the same palm stimulation distance) is the most sensitive NCS test for CTS. Ultrasound shows a swollen, flattened median nerve at the carpal tunnel inlet (cross-sectional area >12 mm² is diagnostic) — an increasingly used alternative or adjunct to NCS, particularly in pregnancy where NCS may not be needed.

prompts.label: Management
prompts.text: Given pregnancy: night splints (wrist in neutral position) commenced — 60% improvement in nocturnal symptoms. Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel: deferred until postpartum given gestational diabetes. Levothyroxine dose reviewed — TSH mildly elevated; dose optimised. Postpartum (6 weeks after delivery): symptoms resolved bilaterally. At 3-month postpartum review: mild residual right-hand symptoms — corticosteroid injection performed; complete resolution. Surgical decompression (carpal tunnel release): planned if symptoms recur — divides the flexor retinaculum under local anaesthesia; >90% success rate.
prompts.reasoning: Management of CTS in pregnancy is conservative (splinting, steroid injection if needed) as most cases resolve postpartum. Night splints (maintain wrist in neutral position — minimises carpal tunnel pressure) are first-line for mild-moderate CTS. Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel: effective short-term relief (avoid in diabetics/pregnancy where possible). Surgical carpal tunnel release (division of the flexor retinaculum): definitive treatment for moderate-severe or refractory CTS — performed under local anaesthesia as day surgery; >90% long-term success. Thenar wasting is an indication for early surgery (motor involvement).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old male presents with numbness and tingling in the left little finger and ring finger for 3 weeks, along with weakness and difficulty spreading his fingers. He returned from a 10-day cycling holiday in the Alps 3 weeks ago.
prompts.reasoning: Sensory loss in the little and ring fingers with intrinsic hand weakness (difficulty spreading fingers) localises to the ulnar nerve — the 'cyclist's palsy' pattern. Prolonged handlebar pressure compresses the ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal (the wrist). This is distinct from carpal tunnel syndrome (which affects the thumb/index/middle finger — median nerve distribution). C8/T1 radiculopathy can cause similar sensory symptoms but would have associated cervical root signs and affect the medial forearm.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: During the 10-day cycling tour he spent 6–8 hours/day on his bicycle, gripping the handlebars. He noticed the tingling developing by day 5, affecting the left little and ulnar half of the ring finger. He now reports weakness when trying to grip objects firmly and difficulty with fine pinch (making the 'OK' sign). No neck pain, no elbow pain. No symptoms in the right hand.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged handlebar compression of the hypothenar eminence causes ulnar nerve entrapment at Guyon's canal (wrist), producing purely distal ulnar neuropathy — affecting the deep motor branch (intrinsic hand muscles) and the superficial sensory branch (little and ulnar ring finger). The absence of elbow pain, medial forearm sensory loss, and normal flexor carpi ulnaris power (FCU is innervated proximal to Guyon's canal — above the wrist) localises the lesion to the wrist rather than the cubital tunnel at the elbow.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neuropathy or hand problems. No diabetes, no hypothyroidism. Right-handed — the affected hand is his left (non-dominant). Works as an architect — detailed drawing is now difficult. No trauma to the elbow. No history of elbow fractures (cubitus valgus — tardy ulnar palsy). No recent weight loss or lymphadenopathy (excluding malignant infiltration of the ulnar nerve).
prompts.reasoning: Ulnar neuropathy has two common sites: (1) Cubital tunnel at the elbow (most common overall — compressed by leaning on elbow, post-fracture cubitus valgus, or tardy ulnar palsy after lateral condyle fracture in childhood); (2) Guyon's canal at the wrist (handlebar compression, occupational). At the elbow, FCU (wrist ulnar deviation) and FDP (little/ring DIP flexion) are also weak; sensation is lost on the dorsal ulnar hand (dorsal cutaneous branch leaves proximal to Guyon's canal). At the wrist (Guyon's canal), FCU and dorsal ulnar sensation are SPARED — a key localising feature.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Left hand: hypothenar wasting visible. Finger abduction (dorsal interossei): MRC 2/5 left vs 5/5 right. Froment's sign positive (left — compensates for weak adductor pollicis by flexing thumb IP joint using FPL). Ulnar claw hand: ring and little fingers — hyperextension at MCP, flexion at PIP/DIP (more prominent at rest). Sensation: reduced pin-prick and light touch over left little finger and ulnar half of ring finger — palmar surface only. Dorsal ulnar hand sensation: NORMAL. Flexor carpi ulnaris power: normal. Elbow Tinel's sign: negative.
prompts.reasoning: Key localisation: (1) Froment's sign (weak adductor pollicis — deep motor branch of ulnar nerve) — positive; (2) Claw hand affecting ring and little fingers (unopposed FDP flexion of IP joints, loss of interossei and lumbricals at MCP); (3) Hypothenar wasting; (4) SPARED dorsal ulnar hand sensation (the dorsal cutaneous branch leaves the ulnar nerve proximal to Guyon's canal — its preservation confirms the lesion is at the wrist, not the elbow); (5) Normal FCU (also spared with Guyon's canal lesion). Negative elbow Tinel's sign confirms no cubital tunnel involvement.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies & Ultrasound
prompts.text: NCS: reduced CMAP amplitude of the left ulnar nerve recording from the left ADM (abductor digiti minimi) — with normal conduction velocity across the elbow. Prolonged distal motor latency to ADM. Dorsal ulnar cutaneous SNAP: NORMAL amplitude — confirming the lesion is distal to the take-off of the dorsal cutaneous branch. Deep motor branch conduction: absent. Ultrasound of left wrist: enlarged cross-sectional area of ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal inlet (12 mm²) with perineural oedema — consistent with entrapment neuropathy.
prompts.reasoning: NCS confirms the localisation precisely: reduced ADM CMAP (deep motor branch) with normal conduction across the elbow confirms the lesion is not at the cubital tunnel. The critical finding is a NORMAL dorsal ulnar cutaneous SNAP — this nerve branches off the ulnar nerve 5–6 cm proximal to Guyon's canal; its normality proves the lesion is distal to this branch point (i.e., at or within Guyon's canal). Ultrasound confirms focal nerve swelling at the entrapment site. This combined NCS + ultrasound approach is the modern standard for localising ulnar neuropathy.

prompts.label: Management & Recovery
prompts.text: Conservative management: padded cycling gloves and handlebar padding recommended for future cycling. Neutral wrist splint at night. Activity modification — avoid sustained wrist pressure. Hand physiotherapy for intrinsic strengthening. At 6 weeks: sensation returning (little and ring fingers — pin-prick returning to 80% of normal). At 3 months: interossei power MRC 4/5; Froment's sign resolving. Full recovery expected at 6 months. Surgical decompression of Guyon's canal: offered if no improvement by 4 months — release of the palmar carpal ligament.
prompts.reasoning: Ulnar neuropathy at Guyon's canal (cyclist's palsy) generally has a good prognosis with conservative management when the cause is identified and removed. The extent of axonal loss on NCS predicts recovery: partial axonal loss → good recovery; complete axonal loss → slow, incomplete recovery (axonal regeneration 1–2 mm/day). Conservative measures (padding, splinting, activity modification) are first-line. Surgery (Guyon's canal release) is reserved for cases that fail to improve or those with severe axonal loss. Claw hand deformity (more severe at the ring and little fingers, less so than in high ulnar lesions because the flexor digitorum profundus to those digits is also paralysed in high lesions — 'ulnar paradox').

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 25-year-old man presents with right wrist drop — he cannot extend his wrist or fingers — noticed when he woke this morning. He has no pain. He attended a party last night and drank heavily before falling asleep with his arm draped over a chair back.
prompts.reasoning: Acute wrist drop following a period of sustained arm compression (falling asleep in an awkward position — 'Saturday night palsy') is the classic presentation of radial nerve compression at the spiral groove of the humerus. Alcohol intoxication leads to loss of protective pain response, allowing prolonged compression. Wrist and finger extension power (radial nerve, C7) is lost, producing wrist drop. Stroke is excluded by the isolated peripheral nerve distribution and absence of other neurological signs.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has complete inability to extend his wrist and fingers (MRC 0/5). He notices numbness over the dorsum of the first web space of the right hand. He can flex his wrist normally and has normal grip strength. His elbow extension is full and normal (triceps spared). No shoulder weakness. No neck pain.
prompts.reasoning: The preservation of triceps (elbow extension) and presence of sensory loss over the first dorsal web space (superficial radial nerve — sensory branch) localises the lesion to the spiral groove of the humerus. At the spiral groove, the nerve has already given off the branch to triceps — so elbow extension is SPARED. This distinguishes spiral groove compression from a more proximal axillary lesion (which would also affect triceps and deltoid). Pure posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) palsy causes wrist and finger drop WITHOUT sensory loss (PIN is the deep motor branch only, distal to the radial tunnel).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He is otherwise healthy. No prior neurological problems. No diabetes. He had a similar episode 2 years ago (left arm) that resolved fully within 3 months. No family history. Drinks approximately 25 units/week (hazardous alcohol use — recurrent alcohol-related nerve injuries). No recent humerus fracture or surgery. No weight loss or systemic symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The previous identical episode on the left arm confirms a pattern of alcohol-related recurrent compressive radial neuropathy. Alcohol causes prolonged unconscious positioning (loss of protective pain awakening). Other causes of radial neuropathy at the spiral groove: midshaft humeral fracture (the nerve wraps around the humerus at this level — direct fracture damage or late callus compression), iatrogenic (arm positioning during surgery, tight cast), crutch palsy (axillary compression — affects triceps too). Recurrent episodes in young people with alcohol use is a characteristic clinical pattern.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right hand: wrist drop — wrist lies in flexion at rest. Wrist extension: MRC 0/5. Finger extension at MCP joints: MRC 0/5. Thumb extension and abduction: MRC 0/5. Elbow flexion and extension: normal (MRC 5/5 — including triceps). Shoulder and arm: normal. Grip strength: normal when wrist manually supported in neutral (intrinsic hand muscles — median and ulnar — intact). Sensation: reduced pin-prick over dorsum of right 1st web space (superficial radial nerve territory). No sensory loss on medial arm or forearm.
prompts.reasoning: The key examination findings: (1) Wrist and finger extension loss (posterior interosseous nerve territory — all wrist and finger extensors); (2) NORMAL triceps (elbow extension preserved — branch to triceps leaves radial nerve before the spiral groove); (3) Sensory loss limited to 1st dorsal web space (superficial radial nerve branch — leaves the main radial nerve in the forearm); (4) Normal grip strength (when supported) confirms intact ulnar and median intrinsic muscles. This pattern precisely localises the lesion to the spiral groove of the humerus — between the triceps branch takeoff and the radial tunnel.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: NCS: right radial motor nerve — markedly reduced CMAP amplitude recording from extensor digitorum communis (EDC) with stimulation above and below the spiral groove; slowed conduction velocity across the spiral groove segment (26 m/s — normal >50 m/s); conduction block identified at the spiral groove. Superficial radial SNAP: absent (sensory axon involvement). Triceps recording: normal CMAP (confirming the branch to triceps is above the lesion). EMG: fibrillations and positive sharp waves in radial-innervated forearm muscles (brachioradialis, extensor carpi radialis) with spared triceps.
prompts.reasoning: NCS confirms radial nerve compression at the spiral groove with conduction block (neurapraxia) — the most common mechanism in compressive palsy. Conduction block (reduced CMAP amplitude when stimulating above the lesion but not below) indicates focal demyelination without axonal loss — predicting full recovery within 6–12 weeks. If axonal loss were present (wallerian degeneration), recovery would take months (axonal regeneration at 1–2 mm/day). Normal CMAP from triceps and normal EMG in triceps confirms the lesion is below the triceps branch point. Conduction block at the spiral groove is the key NCS finding.

prompts.label: Management & Prognosis
prompts.text: Wrist extension splint (cock-up splint) applied to prevent wrist contracture and maintain hand function during recovery. Physiotherapy for passive range-of-motion exercises. Alcohol harm-reduction counselling initiated. At 4 weeks: wrist extension returning (MRC 2/5). At 10 weeks: wrist extension MRC 4/5, finger extension MRC 4/5, sensory loss resolved. At 14 weeks: full recovery (MRC 5/5 throughout). If no recovery at 3 months: repeat NCS and consider surgical exploration for iatrogenic or fracture-associated radial neuropathy.
prompts.reasoning: Saturday night palsy has an excellent prognosis — 70–90% fully recover within 3–6 months because the mechanism is neurapraxia (focal demyelination from compression, no axonal loss). Recovery proceeds from proximal to distal: brachioradialis recovers first, then wrist extensors, then finger extensors, then thumb extensors (reflecting the sequence of branch innervation from proximal to distal). A cock-up splint prevents wrist flexion contracture and restores hand function during recovery. If NCS shows complete axonal loss (absent CMAP and SNAP distal to the lesion), recovery will be much slower and may be incomplete — requiring surgical exploration if caused by humeral fracture.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old male presents with a 3-week history of severe left-sided lower back pain that radiates down the back of the left leg to the heel and lateral foot. The pain is sharp, shooting, and electric-shock in quality.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral leg pain in a radicular distribution — shooting from the back down the posterior thigh, calf, and lateral foot — describes true sciatica (sciatic nerve irritation). The electric-shock quality and dermatomal radiation are characteristic of radicular pain. The most common cause is compression of a lumbar nerve root (usually L4, L5, or S1) by a posterolateral disc prolapse. Red flags must be excluded: bilateral symptoms, saddle anaesthesia, and bladder/bowel dysfunction would suggest cauda equina syndrome — a surgical emergency.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The pain radiates from the left lower back, down the back of the left thigh, lateral calf, and to the lateral heel and lateral border of the foot to the little toe. It is worsened by sitting, coughing, and sneezing (Valsalva manoeuvre increases intradiscal pressure). He lifted a heavy box 3 weeks ago. No saddle anaesthesia. No bladder or bowel dysfunction. No bilateral symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The distribution (posterior thigh → lateral calf → lateral heel → lateral foot → little toe) describes the S1 dermatome — consistent with an L5/S1 disc prolapse compressing the S1 nerve root. Worsening with Valsalva manoeuvres (coughing, sneezing, straining — increases intrathecal and intradiscal pressure, compressing the irritated root further) is a hallmark of disc-related radiculopathy. Acute onset after heavy lifting is the classic precipitant. Absent red flag symptoms (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction) makes cauda equina syndrome less likely but must be monitored.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior back problems. Works as a warehouse supervisor with regular manual lifting. BMI 32 (obesity increases lumbar disc loading). Smokes 15 cigarettes/day (smoking impairs disc nutrition — avascular disc depends on diffusion). No diabetes. No prior malignancy. No unintentional weight loss. No fever. No night sweats (excluding infection/malignancy).
prompts.reasoning: Risk factors for lumbar disc prolapse are confirmed: heavy manual lifting (acute axial loading), obesity (chronic increased intradiscal pressure), and smoking (impairs disc nucleus pulposus nutrition via reduced vertebral endplate blood flow — accelerating disc degeneration). Absence of red flags for malignancy (no weight loss, no prior cancer, no fever) makes spinal metastasis unlikely. Piriformis syndrome (sciatic nerve compression by the piriformis muscle in the buttock) would not worsen with Valsalva — distinguishing feature.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Antalgic gait (leaning away from the left). Lumbar spine: paraspinal muscle spasm on the left. Straight leg raise (SLR) test: positive left at 35° (reproduction of radicular pain down the left leg below the knee — Lasègue's sign). Crossed SLR: negative. Ankle jerk: absent on the left (reduced/absent right: symmetrically diminished). Left EHL (extensor hallucis longus) power: MRC 4/5. Ankle plantarflexion: MRC 4/5 (left) vs 5/5 (right). Sensation: reduced pin-prick over left lateral heel and lateral foot (S1 distribution). Rectal tone: not tested (no bladder symptoms).
prompts.reasoning: A positive SLR (Lasègue's sign) at 35° — reproducing radicular pain below the knee — is the most sensitive clinical test for lumbar disc herniation with root compression (sensitivity 80–90%). A crossed SLR (contralateral leg straight leg raise reproduces ipsilateral pain) is less sensitive but highly specific (~88%) for a large central or paracentral disc herniation. Absent left ankle jerk (S1 — carried by the tibial nerve, part of the sciatic nerve) and reduced ankle plantarflexion confirm S1 root dysfunction. Diminished EHL would suggest additional L5 component. Absent ankle jerk is the most consistent finding in S1 radiculopathy.

prompts.label: MRI Lumbar Spine
prompts.text: MRI lumbar spine (T2-weighted): large posterolateral left-sided disc herniation at L5/S1 causing significant compression of the left S1 nerve root in the lateral recess. Disc nucleus pulposus signal is dehydrated (dark on T2 — disc degeneration). No spinal stenosis. No epidural abscess. No bony metastases. Vertebral body signal normal. Conus medullaris terminates normally at L1/L2.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is the investigation of choice for sciatica, providing excellent soft-tissue resolution of disc herniations, nerve root compression, and associated pathology. The classic MRI finding: posterolateral disc herniation (nucleus pulposus extruded through a tear in the annulus fibrosus) compressing the descending nerve root in the lateral recess or exit foramen. At L5/S1, a posterolateral herniation compresses the S1 root (the root exiting at the level below — the S1 root traverses the L5/S1 disc space). MRI should be reserved for: failure of conservative management, neurological deficit, or red flags — not for uncomplicated acute sciatica.

prompts.label: Management
prompts.text: Conservative management: analgesia (NSAIDs + short course diazepam for muscle spasm). Physiotherapy: McKenzie extension exercises, core stabilisation. Advice: remain active, avoid prolonged bed rest. At 6 weeks: 60% improvement in pain (VAS 8→3). At 12 weeks: near-complete resolution of radicular symptoms — ankle jerk returned. Weight loss and smoking cessation counselled. If no improvement at 6 weeks: transforaminal epidural steroid injection. Surgical referral (microdiscectomy): offered if persistent neurological deficit or intractable pain at 6–12 weeks — >80% success rate for sciatica relief.
prompts.reasoning: Most disc herniations (90%) resolve with conservative management within 6–12 weeks as the herniated nucleus pulposus undergoes resorption (phagocytosis by macrophages). Remaining active is superior to bed rest. NSAIDs are first-line for inflammatory radicular pain; opioids should be avoided long-term. Epidural steroid injections provide temporary relief (4–8 weeks) allowing rehabilitation. Microdiscectomy (surgical removal of the herniated fragment) provides faster pain relief than conservative management but long-term outcomes are equivalent at 1–2 years. Absolute surgical indication: cauda equina syndrome — which requires emergency decompression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old woman presents to ED at midnight with bilateral leg weakness, inability to void urine for 8 hours, and numbness between her legs. She had a 2-week history of worsening low back pain and bilateral sciatica prior to tonight.
prompts.reasoning: The triad of bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention (inability to void for 8 hours — a surgical emergency), and saddle anaesthesia (numbness between the legs — perineum, inner thighs) is the classic presentation of cauda equina syndrome (CES). CES results from compression of the cauda equina nerve roots (below L1, where the spinal cord ends and the nerve roots descend as a 'horse's tail'). This must be distinguished from conus medullaris syndrome (at T12/L1) which gives mixed UMN and LMN signs. CES is a neurosurgical emergency — delayed decompression leads to permanent incontinence.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She describes numbness and tingling in the perineum, inner thighs, and buttocks — 'like sitting on a bicycle saddle' — which developed over the past 24 hours. Her bladder feels full but she cannot initiate micturition (overflow/retention). She has also noticed she cannot feel herself passing stool. Bilateral leg weakness has developed over the past 12 hours — she can barely stand. The back pain suddenly worsened this evening.
prompts.reasoning: Saddle anaesthesia (sensory loss in the S3–S5 dermatomes — perineum, inner thighs, buttocks — in the shape of a riding saddle) is the hallmark sensory deficit of cauda equina syndrome. Urinary retention (S2–S4 parasympathetics and pudendal nerve control) is the most critical finding — painless retention (overflow incontinence) indicates loss of bladder sensation and detrusor muscle denervation. Faecal incontinence reflects S2–S4 pudendal nerve involvement (external anal sphincter). Acute deterioration in a patient with pre-existing sciatica is the classic history — a large central disc herniation suddenly worsening.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Known lumbar disc disease — had L4/L5 disc prolapse diagnosed by MRI 8 months ago, managed conservatively. No malignancy. No anticoagulation (important for surgical planning). No prior spinal surgery. No diabetes. Not immunocompromised (excluding abscess as cause). BMI 34.
prompts.reasoning: Pre-existing lumbar disc disease with acute worsening is the most common cause of CES (massive central disc herniation causing acute cauda equina compression — usually L4/L5 or L3/L4). Other causes include: spinal metastases (vertebral collapse), epidural haematoma (post-procedural or anticoagulated patient), epidural abscess (immunocompromised or IVDU), spinal stenosis (usually gradual onset), iatrogenic. Absence of malignancy and immunosuppression, with prior disc disease, strongly implicates a large central disc herniation as the cause. CES from disc herniation tends to be sudden or rapidly progressive.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral leg weakness (flaccid — LMN pattern): hip flexors MRC 4/5, knee extension MRC 3/5 bilaterally. Knee reflexes: reduced bilaterally. Ankle reflexes: ABSENT bilaterally. Plantar responses: ABSENT (not upgoing — pure LMN). Rectal examination: absent anal tone, no voluntary anal contraction. Perineal sensation (S3–S5): absent bilaterally — saddle anaesthesia confirmed. Bladder: distended percussed to umbilicus — urinary catheterisation drains 750 mL of urine. No UMN signs.
prompts.reasoning: The examination reveals pure LMN signs: flaccid weakness, absent reflexes (ankle areflexia is universal in CES — S1/S2 roots), absent plantar responses (not extensor — distinguishing from spinal cord compression above the conus which gives UMN signs). Absent anal tone on PR examination and confirmed saddle anaesthesia clinch the diagnosis. Urinary retention (750 mL drained) confirms bladder denervation. The pure LMN pattern (no UMN signs, no spasticity, no upgoing plantars) is the key distinction from conus medullaris syndrome (which gives mixed UMN + LMN) and from a cord lesion above L1 (which gives UMN signs).

prompts.label: Emergency MRI Spine
prompts.text: Emergency MRI lumbar spine (performed within 60 minutes): massive central L3/L4 disc herniation with free fragment — complete obliteration of the thecal sac at L3/L4 level. Cauda equina nerve roots are severely compressed bilaterally. No spinal metastases. No abscess. Conus medullaris intact at L1. Nerve roots below the compression: normal signal on T2.
prompts.reasoning: MRI of the whole spine is mandatory in suspected CES — it is the diagnostic gold standard, confirms the level and cause of compression, and guides surgical planning. A massive central disc herniation at L3/L4 (above L5/S1 — more central and affecting a greater proportion of cauda equina roots) is confirmed as the cause. Key MRI findings: obliteration of the thecal sac (the dura) by disc material on axial T2 images, with nerve roots compressed or deviated. The conus medullaris (at L1) is intact — confirming this is a pure cauda equina lesion. MRI should not be delayed — time to surgical decompression is the key determinant of outcome.

prompts.label: Emergency Surgical Decompression
prompts.text: Emergency posterior laminectomy and discectomy performed within 4 hours of MRI (total time from ED presentation to decompression: 6 hours). Post-operatively: saddle anaesthesia improving by day 2. At 1 week: voluntary anal contraction returning. At 6 weeks: bilateral leg strength MRC 4/5; urethral catheter removed — voiding independently with residual 120 mL (on intermittent self-catheterisation programme). At 6 months: walking independently; persistent mild saddle sensory deficit and urgency incontinence (partial recovery).
prompts.reasoning: CES is a neurosurgical emergency — emergency decompression within 24–48 hours is the standard of care. Outcome is directly linked to the severity of deficit at presentation and the time to decompression: (1) Incomplete CES (some perineal sensation or bladder awareness retained) has a much better prognosis than complete CES (no perineal sensation, complete retention); (2) Decompression within 24 hours gives the best outcomes for bladder and bowel function recovery. Prognosis: bladder function recovers best (especially if incomplete CES); saddle anaesthesia and sexual dysfunction may be permanent in complete CES. Litigation risk is high if diagnosis or decompression is delayed.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old male presents following a road traffic accident with the inability to walk, perineal numbness, and inability to control his bladder and bowel. He was wearing a seatbelt and struck the dashboard with his lower chest and upper abdomen.
prompts.reasoning: A high-velocity impact to the lower thoracic/upper lumbar spine raises suspicion for a thoracolumbar fracture with spinal cord or conus medullaris injury. The conus medullaris (the terminal cone of the spinal cord) lies at T12/L1 in adults — a fracture-dislocation at this level causes conus medullaris syndrome. The combination of leg weakness, perineal numbness, and early sphincter dysfunction is characteristic. The level of injury (dashboard impact to lower chest) localises to T12/L1, distinguishing this from pure cauda equina syndrome (below L1) or thoracic cord syndrome (above T10).

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He cannot stand or walk since the accident. His legs feel weak but not completely paralysed. He reports numbness and tingling in the perineum and inner thighs (saddle distribution), and he cannot feel the urge to void (has not passed urine since the accident, 4 hours ago). He also reports priapism (involuntary sustained erection) — a sign of spinal cord injury. Spine pain at T12/L1 on palpation at the scene.
prompts.reasoning: Priapism following spinal injury is a pathological sign of spinal cord injury — it results from loss of inhibitory sympathetic control (normally suppresses erection) combined with intact parasympathetic input. It is virtually pathognomonic of a significant cord injury. The early occurrence of sphincter dysfunction (urinary retention with absent urge) is characteristic of conus medullaris syndrome — the sacral micturition centre (S2–S4) is contained within the conus itself, so conus injury disrupts bladder control immediately and severely. This contrasts with cauda equina syndrome where roots are compressed but the conus is intact.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological conditions. No prior back problems. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol. Works as a construction engineer. He was restrained (seatbelt) — the seatbelt syndrome mechanism (hyperflexion of the thoracolumbar spine over the restrained abdomen) commonly causes Chance fractures (horizontal fracture through the vertebral body) at T12/L1. No medical comorbidities. No anticoagulation.
prompts.reasoning: Seatbelt-related thoracolumbar injuries follow the 'seatbelt syndrome' mechanism: the lap belt acts as a fulcrum, causing violent hyperflexion of the upper body over the restrained pelvis — producing a Chance fracture (horizontal fracture splitting the vertebral body, pedicles, and spinous process) at the thoracolumbar junction (T12–L2). The conus medullaris at T12/L1 is directly in the path of this injury. Associated intra-abdominal injuries (bowel, mesentery, pancreas) from the seatbelt must be excluded. Thoracolumbar injuries (T12/L1) are the most common site for burst and Chance fractures.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: ABCDE primary survey: haemodynamically stable (systolic 108 mmHg — may reflect neurogenic shock — vasodilation from loss of sympathetic tone). Spinal examination: marked tenderness and step-off palpable at T12/L1. Bilateral leg weakness: hip flexors MRC 3/5 (UMN component — some cord sparing), knee extensors MRC 3/5, ankle dorsiflexion MRC 2/5. Ankle reflexes: ABSENT (LMN component — S1/S2). Knee reflexes: BRISK bilaterally (UMN component — intact above L3/L4). Babinski sign: UPGOING bilaterally (UMN). Saddle anaesthesia: absent pin-prick sensation S3–S5. PR examination: absent voluntary anal contraction; reduced anal tone. Priapism noted.
prompts.reasoning: The hallmark of conus medullaris syndrome is MIXED UMN and LMN signs in the same patient — because the conus contains the sacral spinal cord segments (S2–S5) which have UMN pathways passing through them, while the nerve roots exiting from the conus are LMN. Specifically: brisk knee reflexes and upgoing plantars (UMN component from corticospinal tract disruption) with absent ankle reflexes (LMN component — S1/S2 anterior horn cells in the conus are directly destroyed). This mixed pattern is the key distinguishing feature from: (1) Pure cauda equina syndrome — all LMN, absent plantars; (2) Thoracic cord injury — all UMN, upgoing plantars, preserved sacral reflexes.

prompts.label: Emergency Imaging
prompts.text: CT thoracolumbar spine: burst fracture of L1 vertebral body with retropulsed bone fragment causing 70% thecal sac compromise at L1. Associated Chance fracture element at T12/L1. CT abdomen/pelvis: mesenteric contusion; no hollow viscus perforation. MRI thoracolumbar spine: conus medullaris at T12/L1 shows haemorrhagic signal (dark on T2, blooming on GRE) indicating cord haemorrhage (haematomyelia) — poor prognostic sign. Conus is compressed and distorted by the retropulsed L1 fragment. Cauda equina roots below: intact.
prompts.reasoning: CT provides the fastest bony detail — essential in trauma to assess fracture pattern, degree of canal compromise, and stability. A burst fracture with >50% canal compromise is an indication for surgical decompression. MRI is then performed to assess the neural elements: haemorrhagic signal within the conus (haematomyelia) on GRE/T2 is a poor prognostic sign — indicates direct cord destruction rather than compression alone, meaning functional recovery will be limited regardless of surgical decompression. Intact cauda equina roots below the level confirm the injury is at the conus itself — the LMN signs from cauda equina root involvement only develop if the compression extends to involve the roots.

prompts.label: Surgical Management & Prognosis
prompts.text: Emergency posterior stabilisation and decompression (laminectomy + pedicle screw fixation T11–L2) performed within 8 hours of injury. Post-operative MRI: adequate decompression of the thecal sac. Spinal cord injury rehabilitation commenced (urological: suprapubic catheter → intermittent self-catheterisation programme; bowel programme; physiotherapy). At 6 months: bilateral leg strength improved to MRC 4/5 (proximal) — walking with elbow crutches. Persistent: absent ankle reflexes (LMN component permanent — anterior horn cell destruction); urinary incontinence (requiring ISC programme); sexual dysfunction. Upper motor neuron signs (brisk knees, upgoing plantars) persist.
prompts.reasoning: Conus medullaris syndrome has an intermediate prognosis — better than complete thoracic cord injury but worse than pure cauda equina syndrome. The mixed UMN/LMN pattern reflects the dual pathology: UMN disruption (corticospinal tract) may partially recover with decompression; LMN destruction (anterior horn cells of S1–S5 within the conus) is permanent — explaining why the ankle reflexes (S1/S2) may never return even after good leg strength recovery. Sphincter dysfunction in conus injury is often permanent (sacral micturition centre destroyed) — neurogenic bladder management with ISC is lifelong. Sexual dysfunction is common and often permanent. Surgical decompression and stabilisation prevent further injury and allow neural recovery from reversible compression components.
